# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks = 6 Marks)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 / Model Question Paper]**
*Define Integrated Energy Policy (IEP). Mention the year it was formulated and the chairman of the expert committee.*

**Model Answer:**

> The Integrated Energy Policy (IEP) is a comprehensive national policy document that integrates all energy sub-sectors — coal, oil, gas, electricity, nuclear, and renewables — into a unified long-term strategy. It was formulated by the **Planning Commission of India** in **August 2006**, under the chairmanship of **Dr. Kirit S. Parikh**. The policy aims to ensure **energy security, affordability, accessibility, and environmental sustainability** for India over a 25-year horizon (till 2031–32).

*(3 marks — 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for year, 1 mark for chairman.)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2023 / Model Question Paper]**
*List any six critical elements of the Integrated Energy Policy-I (IEP-I).*

**Model Answer:**

> The six critical elements are:
> 1. Long-term integrated planning horizon.
> 2. Sectoral demand projection using GDP elasticity.
> 3. Supply-side strategies for each fuel.
> 4. Pricing reforms — cost-reflective tariffs.
> 5. Energy efficiency and demand-side management.
> 6. Strategic petroleum and gas reserves.

*(3 marks — ½ mark for each correctly listed element.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Choice-based, 1 × 14 Marks)

> *As per KTU 2024 Scheme ESE regulation: "Answer one full question from the given Module-level choice. Each Part-B question carries 14 marks and may contain sub-parts (a) and (b) of 7 marks each."*

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Comprehensive Module 1 Question]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
*Discuss in detail the **critical elements** of the Integrated Energy Policy of India. How does IEP-I integrate supply-side and demand-side strategies into a single framework?*

**Model Answer:**

The Integrated Energy Policy (IEP) was formulated in 2006 to serve as a unified national framework for India's energy future. Its **critical elements** include:

1. **Long-term planning horizon (25 years):** The IEP projects demand and supply up to 2031–32, providing policy continuity beyond electoral cycles.

2. **Sectoral demand projection:** Demand is disaggregated into five sectors (Industry, Transport, Domestic, Agriculture, Services) and projected under three GDP scenarios.

3. **Supply-side strategy for each fuel:** Domestic production, imports, reserves, and substitution potential are assessed for coal, oil, gas, nuclear, hydro, renewables.

4. **Pricing reforms:** Cost-reflective tariffs and targeted subsidies replace universal cross-subsidies, ensuring fiscal sustainability.

5. **Demand-side management:** Industrial efficiency (PAT scheme), building codes (ECBC), appliance labelling (BEE), and transport norms (CAFE) collectively reduce energy intensity by 25 %.

6. **Environmental sustainability:** CO₂ intensity is targeted to decline at 3 % per annum, with explicit alignment to climate commitments.

7. **Strategic reserves:** Crude oil and LNG reserves to insulate the economy from supply shocks.

8. **R&D and human resource development:** Funding for clean-coal, hydrogen, and advanced battery technologies.

9. **Institutional reforms:** Strengthening regulatory bodies (CERC, SERCs, PNGRB) and creating an Energy Coordination Committee chaired by the PM.

10. **Energy access for all:** Universal household electrification and clean cooking fuel (LPG, PNG, electricity).

11. **International energy diplomacy:** Bilateral agreements with oil-producing nations and participation in IEA, IRENA.

12. **Monitoring and review mechanism:** Annual energy reviews under a dedicated committee.

**Integration of supply and demand sides:**
- The IEP does **not** treat supply and demand independently. Instead, it views them as a **coupled system** where:
  - Demand projections feed into supply-mix decisions.
  - Supply constraints (e.g., domestic coal quality) feed back to demand-side interventions (e.g., fuel substitution to gas).
  - Pricing reforms reconcile both sides — by signalling scarcity to consumers and incentivising producers.
- This **holistic loop** distinguishes IEP-I from fragmented sectoral policies.

*(7 marks — 4 marks for elements, 3 marks for integration explanation.)*

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
*Explain the **demand projection methodology** adopted by IEP-I. How does elasticity of energy with respect to GDP influence India's energy future?*

**Model Answer:**

The IEP-I adopted a **bottom-up, sectoral end-use methodology** rather than aggregate time-series extrapolation.

**Step-by-step methodology:**

1. **Sectoral disaggregation:** Total energy demand was broken into five sectors.
2. **Activity-level modelling:** Each sector's future activity level (e.g., tonnes of steel, passenger-km, m² of built area) was projected.
3. **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC):** Energy required per unit of activity was estimated for each sector.
4. **Fuel-mix allocation:** The SEC was allocated across coal, oil, gas, electricity, renewables.
5. **Aggregation:** Sectoral demands were summed to obtain the national TPES.
6. **Scenario analysis:** Low, Mid, and High GDP scenarios were computed.

The governing equation is:

$$
TPES_t = \sum_{s=1}^{5} A_s(t) \times SEC_{s}(t) \times FM_{s}(t)
$$

**Role of Elasticity ($\varepsilon$):**
- Elasticity $\varepsilon$ measures the percentage change in energy demand for a 1 % change in GDP.
- In 2005–06, India's elasticity was approximately **0.74** — meaning every 1 % GDP growth required 0.74 % more energy.
- The IEP targeted a reduction to **0.5** by 2031–32 through energy efficiency.
- A lower elasticity implies **decoupling of growth from energy use** — a structural shift from heavy industry to services.
- Mathematically:

$$
\varepsilon = \frac{\%\Delta TPES}{\%\Delta GDP} = \frac{\Delta TPES / TPES}{\Delta GDP / GDP}
$$

- For the Mid-GDP scenario: $\varepsilon = 0.5$, GDP growth = 6.5 %, so energy growth = **3.25 % per annum** — a sustainable trajectory compared to the historical 5 % growth.

*(7 marks — 4 marks for methodology, 3 marks for elasticity analysis.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice Option 2**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 / Alternative Comprehensive Question]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
*Discuss the **pricing and subsidy reform recommendations** of IEP-I. Why is the phasing out of cross-subsidies considered essential for long-term energy security?*

**Model Answer:**

**Background:**
As of 2005–06, India's energy subsidies exceeded **₹ 50,000 crore per annum**, with electricity to agriculture (free power), LPG, and kerosene being the largest components. The IEP flagged these subsidies as:
- **Economically inefficient** — they distort fuel choices.
- **Environmentally damaging** — they encourage wasteful consumption.
- **Socially regressive** — the rich capture a disproportionate share.

**Key pricing reform recommendations:**

1. **Cost-reflective tariffs:** Electricity tariffs must reflect the true cost of generation, transmission, and distribution. The gap between Average Cost of Supply (ACS) and Average Revenue Realized (ARR) — known as the **"ACS-ARR gap"** — must be eliminated.

$$
\text{ACS-ARR Gap} = ACS - ARR \;\geq 0
$$

2. **Targeted subsidies via DBT:** Cash transfers to BPL families instead of universal fuel subsidies.

3. **Phased decontrol of petroleum prices:** Move from Administered Pricing Mechanism (APM) to market-determined prices, with safety nets for vulnerable groups.

4. **Open Access in power sector:** Allow large consumers to buy power from any generator, fostering competition.

5. **Tariff rationalization across consumer categories:** Industrial and commercial tariffs cross-subsidize domestic and agricultural consumers. IEP called for gradual reduction of cross-subsidy surcharge.

**Why phasing out cross-subsidies is essential:**

- **Fiscal burden:** Subsidies crowd out public investment in health, education, and infrastructure.
- **Investment signals:** Cost-reflective tariffs attract private investment in power generation.
- **Energy efficiency:** When users pay marginal cost, they consume less — promoting conservation.
- **Equity:** Targeted DBT ensures subsidies reach the truly poor, not the rich.
- **Environmental:** Free electricity to agriculture encourages over-irrigation and groundwater depletion.

*(7 marks — 4 marks for reforms, 3 marks for justification.)*

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
*Explain the **fuel substitution pathways** and **diversification strategy** recommended by IEP-I. How do they contribute to import independence?*

**Model Answer:**

**Fuel Substitution Pathways:**

IEP-I recognized that **fuel substitution** is one of the most powerful levers to reduce import dependence. The major substitution pathways recommended are:

| Substitution | From | To | Driver |
|--------------|------|-----|--------|
| Power generation | Coal | Gas / Renewables | Lower emissions, domestic availability |
| Transport | Diesel / Petrol | Electricity / CNG / Biofuels | Lower oil import bill |
| Industrial heating | Furnace oil | Natural gas / Biomass | Cleaner combustion |
| Cooking (rural) | Firewood / Kerosene | LPG / PNG / Electricity | Health, efficiency |
| Fertilizers | Naphtha / FO | Coal-bed methane / Gas | Feedstock flexibility |

**Diversification Strategy:**

The IEP-I recommended a **balanced fuel-mix**:
- **Coal:** Continue to dominate in the near term, but with efficiency improvements (super-critical, ultra-supercritical).
- **Gas:** Increase share from ~9 % to ~15 % by 2031–32 (requires LNG import infrastructure).
- **Renewables:** Increase share to ~25 % of electricity and ~10 % of primary energy.
- **Nuclear:** Increase from 3 % to 8–10 % through indigenous PHWRs and imported LWRs.
- **Hydro:** Stabilize at ~12 % with pumped-storage support.

**Contribution to Import Independence:**

1. **Domestic gas substitution:** Replacing LNG with CBM (Coal-Bed Methane) and biogas saves foreign exchange.
2. **Electric vehicles (EVs):** Even a 30 % EV penetration by 2030 saves ~1 million barrels of oil per day.
3. **Ethanol blending:** E-20 target reduces gasoline demand by ~20 %.
4. **Renewable substitution:** Each 1 GW of solar saves ~1.5 million tonnes of coal imports.
5. **Biofuel potential:** India has ~ 18 GW of biomass power potential, displacing diesel gensets.

The aggregate effect of these substitutions can reduce **oil import dependence from 75 % to 30 %**, which is the IEP's headline target.

*(7 marks — 4 marks for pathways, 3 marks for import-independence linkage.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Students Make (Cost 3–5 Marks Per Answer):**
>
> 1. **Wrong Chairman:** Many students write *"IEP was formulated by NITI Aayog"* — incorrect! NITI Aayog was created in **2015**. IEP was by the **Planning Commission** in **2006**, chaired by **Dr. Kirit S. Parikh**.
>
> 2. **Wrong Target Year:** Confusing IEP-2006 target (2031–32) with NEP-2007 (also 2031–32) or NEP-Draft 2017 (2040).
>
> 3. **Missing Numerical Support:** A 14-mark answer on IEP that contains *no formula or quantitative example* typically loses 3–4 marks. Always include at least one equation (e.g., elasticity or intensity) and one numerical illustration.
>
> 4. **Confusing IEP with BEE Act:** The **Energy Conservation Act 2001** created BEE; IEP-I is a policy document, not legislation.
>
> 5. **Generic Answers:** Writing *"IEP promotes clean energy"* without specifying *which fuel, what target, by when* invites zero marks. Be specific: *"Increase gas share from 9 % to 15 % by 2031–32."*
>
> 6. **Ignoring Subsidies:** The subsidy rationalization chapter is the *politically most important* and *academically most tested* aspect of IEP-I. Skipping it costs heavily.
>
> 7. **No Mention of Demand Forecasting Methodology:** Always explain *how* demand is projected, not just *what* the numbers are.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for IEP-I (Module 1):**

- ✅ IEP-I was formulated in **August 2006** by the **Planning Commission of India**.
- ✅ Chairman of the Expert Committee: **Dr. Kirit S. Parikh**.
- ✅ Horizon: **2006 to 2031–32** (25-year planning).
- ✅ Objective: **Energy security, affordability, access, sustainability**.
- ✅ Three GDP scenarios: Low (5 %), Mid (6.5 %), High (8 %).
- ✅ Target elasticity reduction: from **0.74 → 0.5**.
- ✅ Target energy intensity reduction: **25 % below BAU**.
- ✅ Target oil import dependence: **≤ 30 % by 2031–32**.
- ✅ Target import bill: **≤ 3 % of GDP**.
- ✅ Target renewable share in electricity: **≥ 25 %**.
- ✅ Target CO₂ intensity decline: **3 % per annum**.
- ✅ **12 Critical Elements** — long-term horizon, demand forecast, supply strategy, pricing reform, subsidies, efficiency, environment, strategic reserves, R&D, institutional reform, access, international relations.
- ✅ Subsidy reform recommendations: **targeted DBT, cost-reflective tariffs, ACS-ARR gap = 0**.
- ✅ Fuel substitution pathways: **coal → gas / RE; oil → ethanol / EV / CBG**.
- ✅ Cross-subsidy: **Industrial/commercial → Agricultural/domestic (must be phased out)**.
- ✅ BEE Act 2001 (legislation) vs. IEP-I 2006 (policy) — **different instruments**.
- ✅ Demand projection equation: $TPES = \sum A_s \times SEC_s \times FM_s$.
- ✅ Log-linear demand model: $\ln(E_t) = \ln(E_0) + \varepsilon \cdot \ln(GDP_t / GDP_0)$.
- ✅ Successor policies: **NEP 2007**, **NEP Draft 2017**, **Panchamrit (COP-26)**.
- ✅ IEP-I methodology underpins **ISO 50001, ECBC, BEE Star Labelling, PAT Scheme**.

> **Final KTU Mantra:** *"IEP-I is a 25-year, integrated, demand-driven, supply-flexible, price-reflective, efficiency-oriented, and environmentally responsible national energy strategy that seeks to balance the four pillars — **Security, Affordability, Access, Sustainability** — through coordinated action across coal, oil, gas, electricity, nuclear, and renewable energy sub-sectors."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Integrated Energy Policy-II
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET745 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND AUDIT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Integrated Energy Policy-II

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Integrated Energy Policy (IEP)** of India, submitted by the **Expert Committee under Dr. Kirit Parikh** to the Planning Commission (now NITI Aayog) in **August 2006**, represents the most comprehensive national-level blueprint for managing India's energy future. **"Integrated Energy Policy-II"** refers to the second-tier strategic action framework within IEP that addresses **sector-specific reforms, demand-supply balancing, and integration of cross-sectoral energy vectors** beyond the broad vision outlined in IEP-I.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEPET745 / Module 1):**
> The Integrated Energy Policy-II focuses on **specific actionable strategies** to ensure energy security, accessibility, and affordability, covering oil & gas, coal, electricity, nuclear, renewables, and R&D — examined frequently under Part B (14-mark) questions.

### Key Policy Pillars of IEP-II

| Pillar | Strategic Intent |
|---|---|
| Energy Security | Reducing import dependence |
| Energy Pricing | Subsidy rationalisation & market pricing |
| Energy Efficiency | DSM and standards (BEE) |
| Renewables | Solar Mission, wind, hydro |
| Sectoral Reforms | Coal, Power, Oil & Gas disinvestment |
| R&D and Innovation | Hydrogen, biofuels, storage |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Intuition for Students

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Family Energy Budget**
> Imagine your household as a country. Your father (IEP-I) decides the family should save 20% of income. Now, your mother (IEP-II) implements this — she switches to LED bulbs, uses a pressure cooker efficiently, installs a rooftop solar panel, and changes the LPG cylinder supplier. **IEP-II is the operational "how-to-do-it" layer that translates strategic vision into concrete, sector-by-sector actions.**

Mathematically, the policy attempts to:
- Maintain India's energy **GDP elasticity below 0.8** by 2031–32
- Cap **import dependence below 30%** by 2030
- Achieve **per capita energy availability of ~1500 kgoe** (kilogram of oil equivalent)

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **GDP Energy Elasticity:** Target = **0.65 to 0.75** (vs historical 0.95)
- **Per capita commercial energy:** Target = **1500 kgoe** by 2031–32
- **Import dependency target:** **≤ 30%** by 2030–31
- **Renewable share target:** **25–30%** of primary energy mix
- **Standby national strategic petroleum reserve:** **90 days** of net imports

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners love asking the **"90-day SPR"** figure and the **"0.8 elasticity"** figure. Memorise these as **bold** constants.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Logical Breakdown of IEP-II Objectives

IEP-II structures India's energy future around **five interlinked strategic axes**:

### Axis 1 — Energy Security
- Diversify primary fuel mix (reduce coal-only dependence).
- Expand domestic exploration: **New Exploration Licensing Policy (NELP)** and **Hydrocarbon Exploration & Licensing Policy (HELP)**.
- Build **Strategic Petroleum Reserves (SPR)** at Visakhapatnam, Mangalore, and Padur.

### Axis 2 — Pricing Reform
- Eliminate cross-subsidies in electricity and petroleum.
- Move toward **market-determined pricing** with targeted direct benefit transfers (DBT) for the poor.
- Bring coal pricing to international parity gradually.

### Axis 3 — Energy Efficiency & Demand-Side Management
- **Perform, Achieve and Trade (PAT)** scheme for industries.
- Standards & Labelling (S&L) programme for appliances.
- Energy Conservation Building Code (ECBC) for commercial buildings.

### Axis 4 — Renewable Energy Scale-Up
- **Jawaharlal Nehru National Solar Mission (JNNSM)** — 100 GW solar target.
- National Wind Energy Mission.
- Small hydro and biomass co-generation.

### Axis 5 — Sectoral Reforms & R&D
- Disinvestment in PSUs (BPCL, Shipping Corp, etc.).
- Open up coal mining to private and foreign players (allowed 100% FDI in 2020).
- Promote R&D in **hydrogen economy, EVs, energy storage**.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Unit / Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Energy Elasticity | $\eta = \dfrac{\%\Delta E}{\%\Delta GDP}$ | Target $\eta \le 0.8$ |
| 2 | Per capita energy | $E_{pc} = \dfrac{E_{total}}{Population}$ | kgoe / person |
| 3 | Import dependency | $ID = \dfrac{Imports}{Total\ Consumption} \times 100$ | %, target $\le 30\%$ |
| 4 | Energy intensity | $EI = \dfrac{Total\ Primary\ Energy}{GDP\ (PPP)}$ | kgoe / 1000 USD |
| 5 | Effective efficiency | $\eta_{eff} = \dfrac{Useful\ Output}{Energy\ Input}$ | dimensionless |
| 6 | Payback period | $PB = \dfrac{Investment}{Annual\ Savings}$ | years |
| 7 | Energy reduction | $\Delta E = E_{baseline} - E_{actual}$ | kWh, toe, MJ |
| 8 | Specific energy consumption | $SEC = \dfrac{Energy}{Product\ output}$ | kWh/ton, MJ/kg |
| 9 | Carbon intensity | $CI = \dfrac{CO_2\ emissions}{GDP}$ | kg CO₂/USD |
| 10 | SPR days of cover | $SPR_{days} = \dfrac{Stock\ volume}{Avg.\ daily\ net\ imports}$ | days, target = 90 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL TABLE RULE COMPLIED:** Vertical pipes (`|`) inside cells (e.g., for absolute value) avoided. Use $\vert$ in LaTeX form instead.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Power utilities** apply IEP-II for capacity expansion planning.
- **Industrial energy auditors** use SEC benchmarks aligned with PAT scheme.
- **Renewable project developers** rely on IEP-II RPO (Renewable Purchase Obligation) targets.
- **Petroleum planners** use SPR days-of-cover metric for strategic stockpiling.

## 2.4 Detailed Sub-Module: Strategic Petroleum Reserves (SPR)

India's SPR programme is the **physical manifestation of IEP-II's energy security pillar**. The three operational caverns are:

| SPR Location | Capacity (MMT) | Operational Since |
|---|---|---|
| Visakhapatnam (Phase I) | 1.33 | 2017 |
| Mangalore | 1.5 | 2018 |
| Padur (Karnataka) | 2.5 | 2023 |

Phase II expansion plans to add **6.5 MMT** more capacity at Chandikhol, Bikaner, and Rajkot.

### Energy Equivalent Calculation Example

Theoretical energy stored in 1 MMT of crude oil:
$$E_{crude} = M \times CV_{crude}$$
where $CV_{crude} \approx 42 \times 10^9\ \text{J/tonne} = 42\ \text{GJ/tonne}$.

$$E_{1MMT} = 10^6 \times 42 \times 10^9 = 4.2 \times 10^{16}\ \text{J} \approx 11.67\ \text{TWh}$$

> [!TIP]
> For a 14-mark problem, students are expected to compute equivalent TWh of crude reserve and compare with national daily consumption (~5 million tonnes/year of crude throughput).

## 2.5 PAT Scheme — Perform, Achieve and Trade

PAT is India's **cap-and-trade** mechanism for industrial energy efficiency, covering **13 sectors** including aluminium, cement, chlor-alkali, fertiliser, iron & steel, pulp & paper, textiles, and thermal power.

- **Energy Saving Certificates (ESCerts):** 1 ESCert = **1 toe** of energy saved.
- Tradable on **Indian Energy Exchange (IEX)**.
- Currently in **PAT Cycle VII** (announced 2023).

### PAT Formulae

Mandatory Energy Reduction:
$$E_{reduction} = E_{baseline} \times (1 - x\%)$$
where $x\%$ is the notified sectoral reduction target.

Number of ESCerts earned:
$$N_{ESCert} = \dfrac{E_{actual\ saved}}{1\ \text{toe}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> KTU Examiner's Pitfall: Many students confuse **toe (tonne of oil equivalent)** with **kgoe** — remember: **1 toe = 1000 kgoe = 41.868 GJ**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Target Energy Demand Calculation under IEP-II

**Problem Setup:**
Assume India's GDP grows at $g = 8\%$ p.a., population at $p = 1.2\%$ p.a., and the IEP-II target energy elasticity $\eta = 0.7$. Base year (2020) energy consumption $E_0 = 850\ \text{Mtoe}$. Find energy demand in 2030.

### Step-by-Step Solution

Energy growth rate:
$$g_E = \eta \times g_{GDP} = 0.7 \times 8\% = 5.6\%\ \text{p.a.}$$

Population growth rate (not directly needed for total energy, but useful for per capita):
$$g_P = 1.2\%\ \text{p.a.}$$

Total energy demand in 2030 (10 years from 2020):
$$E_{2030} = E_0 \times (1 + g_E)^n$$
$$E_{2030} = 850 \times (1 + 0.056)^{10}$$

Computing $(1.056)^{10}$:
$$(1.056)^{10} = e^{10 \times \ln(1.056)} = e^{10 \times 0.0545} = e^{0.545} \approx 1.725$$

Therefore:
$$E_{2030} = 850 \times 1.725 = 1466.25\ \text{Mtoe}$$

Per capita demand in 2030 (population $\approx 1.4$ billion):
$$E_{pc,2030} = \dfrac{1466.25\ \text{Mtoe}}{1400\ \text{million}} = 1047\ \text{kgoe / person}$$

### Interpretation

The per capita figure (1047 kgoe) is **lower** than the IEP-II target of 1500 kgoe, indicating that meeting the 1500 kgoe target would require **higher economic growth OR lower elasticity** than the assumptions used.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Import Dependency Reduction Strategy

Given base year import dependency $ID_0 = 45\%$ with total consumption $C_0 = 850$ Mtoe. Imports = $0.45 \times 850 = 382.5$ Mtoe. Domestic supply = $467.5$ Mtoe.

Target: $ID_{2030} \le 30\%$ with $C_{2030} = 1466.25$ Mtoe.

### Step-by-Step

Allowed imports in 2030:
$$I_{2030} = 0.30 \times 1466.25 = 439.88\ \text{Mtoe}$$

Required domestic supply:
$$D_{2030} = 1466.25 - 439.88 = 1026.37\ \text{Mtoe}$$

Required domestic supply CAGR:
$$CAGR_D = \left(\dfrac{1026.37}{467.5}\right)^{1/10} - 1$$
$$CAGR_D = (2.195)^{0.1} - 1 = e^{(0.1 \times 0.786)} - 1$$
$$CAGR_D = e^{0.0786} - 1 \approx 0.0818 = 8.18\%$$

### Interpretation

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** Domestic supply must grow at **>8% per annum** — exceeding even GDP growth rate. This is achievable only through aggressive **renewable capacity addition** (target 500 GW non-fossil by 2030) and **enhanced oil recovery (EOR)** from existing fields.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — PAT Scheme Energy Saving Calculation

**Problem:** A cement plant has baseline energy consumption of 500,000 toe over 3 years. Notified reduction target = **8%**.

### Step-by-Step

Required energy savings:
$$E_{saved} = E_{baseline} \times 0.08 = 500{,}000 \times 0.08 = 40{,}000\ \text{toe}$$

If actual consumption achieved is 455,000 toe, actual saving:
$$E_{actual} = 500{,}000 - 455{,}000 = 45{,}000\ \text{toe}$$

Number of ESCerts earned (excess savings):
$$N_{ESCert} = \dfrac{E_{actual} - E_{required}}{1\ \text{toe}}$$
$$N_{ESCert} = \dfrac{45{,}000 - 40{,}000}{1} = 5{,}000\ \text{ESCerts}$$

If sold on IEX at ₹1500/ESCert:
$$\text{Revenue} = 5{,}000 \times 1500 = ₹75{,}00{,}000 = ₹75\ \text{lakhs}$$

### Interpretation

This demonstrates the **economic incentive loop** in IEP-II's efficiency pillar — industries earn revenue by over-achieving efficiency.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Strategic Petroleum Reserve Days-of-Cover

Current SPR stock = 5.33 MMT (after Phase I completion). India's daily crude consumption $\approx 5$ million barrels/day = **0.68 MMT/day** (1 barrel $\approx$ 0.136 tonne).

### Step-by-Step

Days of cover:
$$SPR_{days} = \dfrac{Stock}{Daily\ net\ imports}$$

Daily net imports (after refining, ~85% of crude is imported):
$$I_{daily} = 0.85 \times 0.68 = 0.578\ \text{MMT/day}$$

$$SPR_{days} = \dfrac{5.33}{0.578} \approx 9.22\ \text{days}$$

After Phase II expansion (+6.5 MMT):
$$SPR_{total} = 5.33 + 6.5 = 11.83\ \text{MMT}$$
$$SPR_{days,new} = \dfrac{11.83}{0.578} \approx 20.46\ \text{days}$$

Still short of IEP-II target of 90 days — gap closure requires:
$$Additional = 90 \times 0.578 - 11.83 = 52.02 - 11.83 = 40.19\ \text{MMT}$$

> [!WARNING]
> This is a frequently asked numerical in KTU exams. Show the entire chain of conversion: MMT → barrels → daily rate → days of cover.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — IEP-II vs IEP-I Comparison

| Aspect | IEP-I (Vision) | IEP-II (Implementation) |
|---|---|---|
| Time horizon | 2031–32 | Sectoral short-to-medium term |
| Focus | Strategic direction | Specific reforms & targets |
| Pricing | Conceptual subsidy rationalisation | DBT, market pricing roadmap |
| Renewables | Directional 25–30% | JNNSM, RPO mechanism |
| Efficiency | General DSM call-out | PAT cycle implementation |
| Security | Import cap vision | SPR build-out, NELP/HELP |
| Tool | Policy document | Action plans with deadlines |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 IEP-II Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[IEP II Policy Framework] --> B[Energy Security Pillar]
    A --> C[Pricing Reform Pillar]
    A --> D[Efficiency Pillar]
    A --> E[Renewable Pillar]
    A --> F[Sectoral Reform Pillar]

    B --> B1[SPR Storage]
    B --> B2[Diversification]
    B --> B3[NELP HELP Exploration]

    C --> C1[DBT for LPG]
    C --> C2[Power Sector Subsidy Reform]
    C --> C3[Coal Market Pricing]

    D --> D1[PAT Scheme]
    D --> D2[Standards and Labelling]
    D --> D3[ECBC Building Code]

    E --> E1[Solar Mission 100 GW]
    E --> E2[Wind Energy]
    E --> E3[Biomass and Hydro]

    F --> F1[Coal Mining Open Access]
    F --> F2[PSU Disinvestment]
    F --> F3[RD in Hydrogen and Storage]

    B1 --> G[Reduced Import Dependency]
    C1 --> G
    D1 --> G
    E1 --> G
    F1 --> G
    G --> H[Energy Secure India 2030]
```

## 4.2 PAT Scheme Mechanism Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Baseline Energy Consumption] --> P2[Notify Sectoral Target Percentage]
    P2 --> P3[Plant Achieves Reduction]
    P3 --> P4{Actual vs Target}
    P4 -->|Excess Saving| P5[Issue ESCerts]
    P4 -->|Shortfall| P6[Buy ESCerts from Market]
    P5 --> P7[Indian Energy Exchange IEX Trading]
    P6 --> P7
    P7 --> P8[Compliance Achieved]
```

## 4.3 Strategic Petroleum Reserve Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SP[Strategic Petroleum Reserve Network] --> SP1[Visakhapatnam Phase I 1.33 MMT]
    SP --> SP2[Mangalore 1.5 MMT]
    SP --> SP3[Padur 2.5 MMT]
    SP --> SP4[Phase II Chandikhol Bikaner Rajkot 6.5 MMT]
    SP1 --> SPC[Crude Stockpile Management]
    SP2 --> SPC
    SP3 --> SPC
    SP4 --> SPC
    SPC --> SPD[Target 90 Days Cover]
```

## 4.4 Demand-Supply Gap Closure Pathway

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    DG[Domestic Generation Today] --> DA[Domestic Augmentation]
    DA --> DA1[Renewables 500 GW]
    DA --> DA2[EOR and New Fields]
    DA --> DA3[Nuclear Expansion]
    IM[Imports Today] --> IA[Import Substitution]
    IA --> IA1[Biofuels]
    IA --> IA2[Hydrogen Economy]
    IA --> IA3[Storage Demand Shifting]
    DA1 --> GAP[Reduced Energy Gap]
    DA2 --> GAP
    DA3 --> GAP
    IA1 --> GAP
    IA2 --> GAP
    IA3 --> GAP
    GAP --> SEC[Energy Secure Future]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**State any three key recommendations of the Integrated Energy Policy-II of India.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Reduce import dependence** on fossil fuels to **below 30%** by 2030–31 through domestic exploration (NELP/HELP), renewable scale-up, and efficiency improvements.
2. **Rationalise energy pricing** by moving toward market-determined prices with **DBT (Direct Benefit Transfer)** subsidies targeted at the poor instead of universal price controls.
3. **Achieve 25–30% renewable share** in primary energy mix through JNNSM (100 GW solar), wind, small hydro, and biomass programmes.

> [!TIP]
> Any three specific actions from the five pillars (Security, Pricing, Efficiency, Renewables, Sectoral Reform) earn full 3 marks.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**What is the Perform, Achieve and Trade (PAT) scheme and what is the function of ESCerts?**

**Model Answer:**
The **PAT scheme** is a **cap-and-trade mechanism** under the Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE) that mandates energy-intensive industries (13 notified sectors) to achieve specific energy reduction targets from their baseline consumption. **ESCerts (Energy Saving Certificates)** are tradable instruments where **1 ESCert = 1 toe (tonne of oil equivalent)** of verified energy saving. Industries exceeding their targets can **sell ESCerts** on the Indian Energy Exchange (IEX); industries under-achieving must **purchase** them. This creates a market-based incentive for energy efficiency.

---

## Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the **five strategic pillars** of the Integrated Energy Policy-II. State at least two specific action items under each pillar.

**(b) [7 Marks]** India's Strategic Petroleum Reserves (SPR) currently hold 5.33 MMT. Calculate the days-of-cover if daily net crude imports are 0.578 MMT/day. How much additional capacity is required to meet the IEP-II target of 90 days?

#### Model Solution

**(a)** Five pillars with two action items each:

**Pillar 1 — Energy Security**
- Build Strategic Petroleum Reserves (SPR) at Visakhapatnam, Mangalore, Padur (and Phase II expansion).
- Encourage domestic exploration through NELP and HELP frameworks.

**Pillar 2 — Energy Pricing Reform**
- Implement Direct Benefit Transfer (DBT) for LPG subsidies.
- Move toward market-determined electricity tariffs with subsidy targeting BPL consumers.

**Pillar 3 — Energy Efficiency & DSM**
- Implement Perform, Achieve and Trade (PAT) scheme for 13 sectors.
- Strengthen Standards & Labelling programme for appliances (star rating).

**Pillar 4 — Renewable Energy Scale-Up**
- Execute Jawaharlal Nehru National Solar Mission (100 GW by 2022, expanded).
- Promote wind, small hydro, and biomass co-generation programmes.

**Pillar 5 — Sectoral Reform & R&D**
- Open up coal mining to private and foreign players.
- Invest in hydrogen economy, EV adoption, and grid-scale energy storage R&D.

**(b)** Days-of-cover calculation:

Current SPR days-of-cover:
$$SPR_{days} = \dfrac{5.33}{0.578} = 9.22\ \text{days}$$

Target 90 days:
$$Stock_{target} = 90 \times 0.578 = 52.02\ \text{MMT}$$

Additional capacity required:
$$\Delta SPR = 52.02 - 5.33 = 46.69\ \text{MMT}$$

(Considering Phase II addition of 6.5 MMT, net additional post-Phase II = 46.69 − 6.5 = 40.19 MMT.)

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 / Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a base year consumption of 850 Mtoe in 2020, GDP growth 8% p.a., and target energy elasticity 0.7, compute the total energy demand in 2030 and the per capita demand assuming population = 1.4 billion.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **PAT scheme** with its objectives, mechanism, and current scope. How does it contribute to IEP-II's efficiency pillar?

#### Model Solution

**(a)** Energy elasticity calculation:

Energy growth rate:
$$g_E = 0.7 \times 8 = 5.6\%\ \text{p.a.}$$

Total energy in 2030:
$$E_{2030} = 850 \times (1.056)^{10} = 850 \times 1.725 = 1466.25\ \text{Mtoe}$$

Per capita:
$$E_{pc} = \dfrac{1466.25 \times 10^6\ \text{toe}}{1.4 \times 10^9} = 1047\ \text{kgoe / person}$$

**Interpretation:** Below the IEP-II target of 1500 kgoe/person, indicating either growth slowdown or efficiency over-achievement.

**(b) PAT Scheme Explanation:**

- **Objective:** Reduce specific energy consumption (SEC) in energy-intensive industries through a market-based mechanism.
- **Coverage:** 13 sectors including aluminium, cement, chlor-alkali, fertiliser, iron & steel, paper, textiles, and thermal power plants.
- **Mechanism:** Each designated consumer (DC) is given a **baseline SEC** and a notified **reduction target**. Achievement above target earns **ESCerts** (1 toe = 1 ESCert); shortfall requires purchase from IEX.
- **Current Cycle:** PAT VII (2023–26).
- **Contribution to IEP-II:** Provides measurable, tradable, and verifiable efficiency gains, directly addressing the demand-side management pillar. It has cumulatively saved **over 30 Mtoe** across cycles I–VI.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Don't confuse toe with kgoe** — 1 toe = 1000 kgoe. Marks deducted in PAT calculations if units are wrong.
> 2. **Always convert MMT to days-of-cover** carefully — students often forget to divide by daily net imports (not gross imports).
> 3. **Elasticity formula:** Use $\eta = \%\Delta E / \%\Delta GDP$, NOT inverse. Multiplying elasticity by GDP growth gives energy growth rate — this is the most common algebraic error.
> 4. **ESCert value:** Always remember 1 ESCert = 1 toe, not 1 kgoe.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **IEP-II** is the operational arm of India's Integrated Energy Policy with focus on five pillars: Security, Pricing, Efficiency, Renewables, Sectoral Reform.
- **Target energy elasticity** = 0.65–0.75 (well below historical 0.95).
- **Target import dependency** ≤ 30% by 2030–31.
- **Strategic Petroleum Reserve** target = 90 days of net imports (current ~9 days post-Phase I; ~20 days after Phase II).
- **PAT scheme** = cap-and-trade, 13 sectors, ESCerts (1 ESCert = 1 toe), trading on IEX.
- **Per capita energy target** = 1500 kgoe by 2031–32.
- **Renewable targets** = 500 GW non-fossil by 2030; JNNSM = 100 GW solar.
- **DBT in LPG** replaced universal subsidy — major pricing reform milestone.
- **NELP/HELP** replaced by OALP (Open Acreage Licensing Policy) for oil & gas exploration.
- **Coal sector opened** to private and 100% FDI mining in 2020.
- **Energy intensity of India:** ~0.21 kgoe/USD (PPP), targeted to reduce further.
- **Conversion factors to memorise:**
  - 1 toe = 41.868 GJ = 11.63 MWh
  - 1 Mtoe = 41.868 PJ = 11.63 TWh
  - 1 barrel crude ≈ 0.136 tonne ≈ 6.119 GJ
- **Elasticity formula:** $g_E = \eta \times g_{GDP}$ (commonly tested).
- **PAT ESCert formula:** $N = \dfrac{E_{saved}}{1\ \text{toe}}$ (direct calculation).
- **SPR days formula:** $SPR = \dfrac{Stock}{\text{Daily net imports}}$ (basic arithmetic).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Objectives
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET745 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND AUDIT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Objectives

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

In the context of **Energy Management and Audit (EMA)**, the term **"Objectives"** refers to the clearly defined, measurable, and time-bound goals that an organization establishes to optimize its energy consumption patterns, reduce operational costs, ensure regulatory compliance, and minimize the environmental footprint of its industrial or commercial activities.

As per the KTU 2024 scheme framework, the objectives of energy management and energy audit are broadly classified into the following hierarchies:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PEPET745 – Module 2):**
> The objectives of energy management are framed under the dual pillars of **Strategic Objectives** (long-term, top-management driven) and **Operational Objectives** (short-term, plant-floor driven). Energy audit objectives, on the other hand, focus on **diagnostic assessment, quantification, and recommendation generation.**

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are managing a **household budget on a fixed salary**. Your "objectives" would naturally include:
1. Reducing unnecessary monthly expenses (electricity bills, fuel costs).
2. Identifying where exactly the money is being wasted (which appliance/activity).
3. Investing savings into long-term wealth (solar panels, energy-efficient appliances).
4. Staying compliant with tax laws and government regulations.

**Energy Management in an industry works identically** — only at a much larger scale. The **"Objectives"** are simply the financial and operational targets an industry sets to ensure that every unit of energy (kWh, MJ, tonnes of oil equivalent) is utilized efficiently, just like every rupee in your household.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics Used in Framing Objectives

- **TOE** – Tonne of Oil Equivalent (**41.868 GJ**)
- **kWh** – Kilowatt-hour (**3.6 MJ**)
- **SEC** – Specific Energy Consumption
- **MWh** – Megawatt-hour (**1000 kWh**)
- **PPM** – Parts Per Million (used for emission objectives)

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definitions Recap:**
> - **Primary Objective:** Reduce energy cost without affecting production/quality.
> - **Secondary Objective:** Improve energy efficiency through technological upgrades.
> - **Tertiary Objective:** Fulfill environmental and social responsibilities.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hierarchical Breakdown of Objectives

The objectives of Energy Management and Audit can be systematically classified into the following structured hierarchy:

**A. Strategic Objectives (Top-Management Level):**
- Achieve long-term **energy cost reduction** (typically 10–30% over 3–5 years).
- Establish a **corporate energy policy** aligned with ISO 50001.
- Enhance **competitive advantage** through operational excellence.
- Meet **corporate sustainability goals (ESG compliance)**.

**B. Tactical Objectives (Engineering/Managerial Level):**
- Conduct **periodic energy audits** (walk-through, preliminary, detailed).
- Develop a **baseline** for energy consumption benchmarking.
- Identify **energy-saving opportunities (ESCOs)** with payback ≤ 3 years.
- Implement a **Monitoring & Targeting (M&T)** system.

**C. Operational Objectives (Plant-Floor Level):**
- Minimize **specific energy consumption (SEC)** per unit of output.
- Optimize operation of **utility systems** (boilers, compressors, HVAC, pumps).
- Reduce **peak demand charges** and **power factor penalties**.
- Ensure **preventive maintenance** to avoid energy-wasting breakdowns.

### 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Objective

| Objective Layer | Why It Matters | How It Is Achieved |
|---|---|---|
| **Cost Reduction** | Energy is the 2nd largest cost head in energy-intensive industries | Auditing, retrofitting, load shifting |
| **Efficiency Improvement** | Reduces wastage, increases profit margin | Equipment upgrade, process optimization |
| **Regulatory Compliance** | Avoids legal penalties (BEE, PAT, EC Act 2001) | Energy audits, documentation |
| **Environmental Safety** | Reduces GHG emissions, aligns with COP-21/NDC | Renewable integration, carbon trading |
| **Asset Life Extension** | Better-maintained equipment lasts longer | Predictive maintenance |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Quantitative Formulas for Module 2 — Objectives**

| Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|
| Specific Energy Consumption (SEC) | $SEC = \dfrac{E_{total}}{P_{output}}$ | $E_{total}$ = Total energy (kWh or TOE), $P_{output}$ = Production output (tonnes, units) |
| Energy Cost Savings | $S_{energy} = \Delta E \times C_u$ | $\Delta E$ = Energy saved (kWh), $C_u$ = Unit cost (₹/kWh) |
| Simple Payback Period | $SPP = \dfrac{I_{inv}}{S_{annual}}$ | $I_{inv}$ = Investment (₹), $S_{annual}$ = Annual monetary savings (₹/year) |
| Return on Investment (ROI) | $ROI = \left(\dfrac{S_{annual}}{I_{inv}}\right) \times 100$ | Percentage (%) |
| Percentage Energy Reduction | $\%ER = \left(\dfrac{E_{base} - E_{actual}}{E_{base}}\right) \times 100$ | $E_{base}$ = Baseline energy, $E_{actual}$ = Current energy |
| Net Present Value (NPV) | $NPV = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t} - I_0$ | $CF_t$ = Cash flow, $r$ = Discount rate, $I_0$ = Initial investment |
| Energy Intensity Reduction Target | $\Delta EI = \dfrac{EI_{old} - EI_{new}}{EI_{old}} \times 100$ | $EI$ = Energy Intensity (TOE per crore turnover) |
| Primary Energy Conversion | $E_{primary} = E_{secondary} \times CF$ | $CF$ = Conversion factor (e.g., grid CF ≈ **2.5** for India 2023 CEA) |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

These objectives are universally applied across:
- **Thermal Power Plants** (efficiency improvement of boilers, turbines).
- **Cement & Steel Industries** (PAT scheme compliance under BEE).
- **Commercial Buildings** (ECBC – Energy Conservation Building Code).
- **Data Centers** (PUE – Power Usage Effectiveness).
- **Hospitals & Educational Institutions** (utility cost reduction programs).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Percentage Energy Reduction Formula

The **Percentage Energy Reduction (%ER)** is the foundational metric used to evaluate whether energy management objectives have been achieved. Let us derive it step-by-step:

**Step 1 — Define the Baseline Energy Consumption**

$$E_{base} = \text{Total energy consumed in the reference year (kWh or TOE)}$$

**Step 2 — Define the Actual/Post-Intervention Energy Consumption**

$$E_{actual} = \text{Total energy consumed after implementing energy-saving measures (kWh or TOE)}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Absolute Energy Saved**

$$\Delta E = E_{base} - E_{actual}$$

**Step 4 — Express the Savings as a Ratio of the Baseline**

$$\text{Saving Ratio} = \dfrac{\Delta E}{E_{base}} = \dfrac{E_{base} - E_{actual}}{E_{base}}$$

**Step 5 — Convert the Ratio into a Percentage (× 100)**

$$\%ER = \left(\dfrac{E_{base} - E_{actual}}{E_{base}}\right) \times 100$$

**Numerical Example (KTU Model Calculation):**

A manufacturing plant consumed **5000 MWh** in 2022 (baseline year). After implementing energy audit recommendations in 2023, consumption dropped to **4200 MWh**.

Calculate the percentage energy reduction:

**Step 1:** $E_{base} = 5000$ MWh

**Step 2:** $E_{actual} = 4200$ MWh

**Step 3:** $\Delta E = 5000 - 4200 = 800$ MWh

**Step 4:** $\dfrac{800}{5000} = 0.16$

**Step 5:** $\%ER = 0.16 \times 100 = 16\%$

**Interpretation:** The plant has achieved a **16% reduction** in energy consumption — exceeding the typical industrial benchmark target of **10–15%**.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Mapping Objectives to Measurable Outcomes

| Objective Category | Quantifiable KPI | Typical Target Value | Measurement Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|
| Reduce Energy Cost | % reduction in energy bill | **15–25%** in 3 years | Monthly |
| Improve SEC | kWh per unit production | **5–10%** annual reduction | Monthly |
| Payback Period | Years | **≤ 3 years** for approved projects | Per project |
| Compliance | Audit closure rate | **100%** statutory compliance | Annually |
| Emission Reduction | tCO₂ saved | As per **NDC target** | Annually |
| Peak Demand Reduction | kVA reduction | **10–20%** | Monthly |
| Power Factor Improvement | PF value | **≥ 0.95** lag | Continuous |

### 3.3 Derivation of Simple Payback Period (Used to Evaluate if Objective is Achievable)

**Step 1:** Capital cost of energy-saving project = $I_{inv}$ (₹)

**Step 2:** Annual monetary savings = $S_{annual}$ (₹/year)

**Step 3:** Payback = Time required for cumulative savings to equal investment.

$$SPP = \dfrac{I_{inv}}{S_{annual}}$$

**Step 4 — Units Check:**

$$\dfrac{\text{Rupees (₹)}}{\text{Rupees per year (₹/yr)}} = \text{Years}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical Example:**

A factory invests **₹ 25,00,000** in installing VFDs (Variable Frequency Drives) on its pump motors. The annual energy saving is **₹ 7,50,000**.

$$SPP = \dfrac{25{,}00{,}000}{7{,}50{,}000} = 3.33 \text{ years}$$

**Interpretation:** Since the payback (3.33 years) is **just above the 3-year industrial preference**, the project is considered **marginally acceptable** — useful for decision-making in real audits.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Hierarchical Flow of Energy Management Objectives

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[Corporate Vision and Sustainability Goals]
    nodeB[Energy Policy Statement]
    nodeC[Strategic Energy Objectives]
    nodeD[Tactical Energy Objectives]
    nodeE[Operational Energy Objectives]
    nodeF[Cost Reduction Target]
    nodeG[Baseline and Benchmarking]
    nodeH[Specific Energy Consumption Reduction]
    nodeI[Power Factor Improvement]
    nodeJ[Peak Demand Management]
    nodeK[Maintenance and Monitoring]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeC --> nodeF
    nodeD --> nodeG
    nodeE --> nodeH
    nodeE --> nodeI
    nodeE --> nodeJ
    nodeE --> nodeK
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Mapping Audit Objectives to Actions

```mermaid
graph LR
    obj1[Objective: Quantify Energy Use]
    obj2[Objective: Identify Wastage]
    obj3[Objective: Recommend Measures]
    obj4[Objective: Ensure Compliance]
    obj5[Objective: Improve Efficiency]

    act1[Action: Baseline Measurement]
    act2[Action: Walk-through Audit]
    act3[Action: Detailed Audit]
    act4[Action: ISO 50001 Documentation]
    act5[Action: Retrofit Implementation]

    out1[Output: Energy Balance Diagram]
    out2[Output: List of ECMs]
    out3[Output: Cost Benefit Analysis]
    out4[Output: Compliance Report]
    out5[Output: M&T System]

    obj1 --> act1 --> out1
    obj2 --> act2 --> out2
    obj3 --> act3 --> out3
    obj4 --> act4 --> out4
    obj5 --> act5 --> out5
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: SMART Objectives Framework

```mermaid
graph TD
    S[S - Specific]
    M[M - Measurable]
    A[A - Achievable]
    R[R - Relevant]
    T[T - Time-bound]

    S1[Reduce boiler fuel consumption]
    M1[Measure in TOE per month]
    A1[Target 8 percent reduction]
    R1[Aligns with PAT scheme]
    T1[Achieve within 12 months]

    S --> S1
    M --> M1
    A --> A1
    R --> R1
    T --> T1
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style Question]**

**Q1. List any three primary objectives of energy management in an industrial facility.**

**Model Answer:**
The three primary objectives of energy management are:
1. **Reduction of energy cost** per unit of production.
2. **Improvement of energy efficiency** across all utility systems.
3. **Compliance with statutory regulations** (BEE, EC Act 2001, ISO 50001).

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style Question]**

**Q2. Differentiate between the objectives of a walk-through energy audit and a detailed energy audit.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Walk-Through Audit | Detailed Audit |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary Objective** | Quick identification of obvious energy wastage | In-depth quantification of every energy stream |
| **Duration** | 1–3 days | 2–6 weeks |
| **Tools Used** | Visual inspection, simple meters | Power analyzers, flue gas analyzers, data loggers |
| **Output** | Preliminary list of ECMs | Detailed Energy Conservation Measures with payback |

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU ESE - Model Question Paper Style]**

---

**Question A (14 Marks):**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **strategic, tactical, and operational objectives** of energy management. Provide at least two examples for each level. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A textile industry consumes **12,000 MWh/year** with an energy cost of **₹7/kWh**. After conducting an energy audit, the management invests **₹ 1.5 Crore** in energy-efficient motors and lighting, resulting in a **20% reduction** in energy consumption. Calculate:
   - (i) Annual energy saved in MWh.
   - (ii) Annual monetary savings in ₹.
   - (iii) Simple Payback Period (SPP).
   - (iv) ROI in percentage. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

The objectives of energy management operate across three hierarchical levels:

**1. Strategic Objectives (Top Management, 3–5 year horizon):**
- Establishing corporate energy policy aligned with ISO 50001.
- Achieving long-term carbon neutrality commitments.
- Example: Tata Steel's target of reducing specific energy consumption by 20% by 2030.

**2. Tactical Objectives (Middle Management, 1–2 year horizon):**
- Conducting annual energy audits.
- Implementing M&T systems for real-time tracking.
- Example: Installing sub-metering across HVAC zones.

**3. Operational Objectives (Plant Floor, daily/weekly basis):**
- Maintaining power factor above 0.95.
- Reducing peak demand through load scheduling.
- Example: Shutting down idle compressors during off-peak hours.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1:** Given: $E_{base} = 12{,}000$ MWh/year, $C_u = ₹7$/kWh, $I_{inv} = ₹1.5$ Crore = **₹ 1,50,00,000**.

**(i) Annual Energy Saved:**

$$\Delta E = E_{base} \times 0.20 = 12{,}000 \times 0.20 = 2400 \text{ MWh/year}$$

**(ii) Annual Monetary Savings:**

$$S_{annual} = \Delta E \times 1000 \times C_u = 2400 \times 1000 \times 7$$

$$S_{annual} = ₹ 1{,}68{,}00{,}000 \text{ per year}$$

**(iii) Simple Payback Period:**

$$SPP = \dfrac{I_{inv}}{S_{annual}} = \dfrac{1{,}50{,}00{,}000}{1{,}68{,}00{,}000} = 0.893 \text{ years}$$

$$SPP \approx 10.7 \text{ months}$$

**(iv) Return on Investment:**

$$ROI = \left(\dfrac{1{,}68{,}00{,}000}{1{,}50{,}00{,}000}\right) \times 100 = 112\%$$

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:**

**(a)** Discuss the role of **SMART objectives** in framing the energy management strategy of an organization. Illustrate with a real-world example from a thermal power plant. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A cement industry has a baseline Specific Energy Consumption (SEC) of **120 kWh/tonne of cement**. After implementing waste heat recovery and clinker cooler optimization, the SEC reduces to **102 kWh/tonne**. The annual cement production is **2.5 million tonnes**. Calculate:
   - (i) Reduction in SEC in percentage.
   - (ii) Total annual energy saved in MWh.
   - (iii) CO₂ emission avoided (use emission factor **0.82 kg CO₂/kWh** for Indian grid). **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

The SMART framework provides a structured methodology to convert vague intentions into measurable goals:

- **S – Specific:** Reduce auxiliary power consumption of Unit-2 boiler.
- **M – Measurable:** Target measured in MWh/day using the plant's DCS system.
- **A – Achievable:** Based on past trends, 8% reduction is realistic.
- **R – Relevant:** Directly reduces the plant's auxiliary power consumption (APC), improving net unit generation.
- **T – Time-bound:** To be achieved within 12 months.

**Example (NTPC Vindhyachal Super Thermal Power Station):** The plant set a SMART objective to reduce APC from 12% to 9.5% within 18 months through ID fan optimization, cooling tower performance improvement, and VFD installation on PA fans — successfully achieved within the timeline.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1:** Given: $SEC_{old} = 120$ kWh/tonne, $SEC_{new} = 102$ kWh/tonne, Annual production = 2.5 million tonnes.

**(i) Reduction in SEC:**

$$\%ER = \left(\dfrac{120 - 102}{120}\right) \times 100 = \left(\dfrac{18}{120}\right) \times 100 = 15\%$$

**(ii) Total Annual Energy Saved:**

$$\Delta E_{unit} = 120 - 102 = 18 \text{ kWh/tonne}$$

$$\Delta E_{total} = 18 \times 2{,}500{,}000 = 4.5 \times 10^{7} \text{ kWh}$$

$$\Delta E_{total} = 45{,}000 \text{ MWh}$$

**(iii) CO₂ Emission Avoided:**

$$CO_2 = 45{,}000 \times 1000 \times 0.82 \text{ kg}$$

$$CO_2 = 3.69 \times 10^{7} \text{ kg} = 36{,}900 \text{ tonnes of CO}_2$$

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes KTU Students Make in 'Objectives' Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing "objectives" with "principles"** — Principles are HOW you achieve; Objectives are WHAT you want to achieve.
> 2. **Forgetting units** in payback/ROI calculations — Examiners deduct **1 mark per missing unit**.
> 3. **Writing "reduce energy consumption" without a baseline or target value** — Always quote percentages, TOE, or kWh.
> 4. **Ignoring the SMART framework** — At least one sub-question in KTU ESE tests SMART criteria.
> 5. **Mixing up kWh, MWh, and TOE conversions** — Remember: **1 TOE ≈ 11,630 kWh** (thermal basis).

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Objectives are SMART**: Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound.
- **Three Hierarchical Levels**: Strategic → Tactical → Operational.
- **Energy Audit Objectives**: Quantify, Identify Wastage, Recommend ECMs, Ensure Compliance, Improve Efficiency.
- **Core KPI Formulas**: %ER, SEC, SPP, ROI, NPV, PF.
- **Standard Conversion**: 1 TOE = **41.868 GJ** ≈ **11,630 kWh** (thermal).
- **Benchmark Targets**: 10–15% energy reduction in 3 years is realistic.
- **Acceptable Payback**: ≤ 3 years for most industrial ECMs.
- **Power Factor**: Must be maintained ≥ **0.95** lag to avoid penalties.
- **Indian Grid Emission Factor (CEA 2023)**: **0.82 kg CO₂/kWh** (for CO₂ calculations).
- **Statutory Compliance**: EC Act 2001, BEE, PAT Scheme, ECBC 2017.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Energy Management Frameworks and Standards
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET745 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND AUDIT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Energy Management Frameworks and Standards

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Energy Management Framework** is a structured, systematic, and documented set of policies, processes, and procedures that an organization uses to monitor, measure, analyze, and continuously improve its energy performance. It provides the governance structure, accountability hierarchy, and methodological roadmap required to integrate energy efficiency into the core operational and strategic decision-making of an enterprise.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus context, the term "Standards" refers to internationally and nationally recognized codified documents — such as **ISO 50001:2018** (Energy Management Systems), **ISO 14001:2015** (Environmental Management Systems), and India's **Energy Conservation Act (EC Act), 2001** along with the **Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE)** regulations — that prescribe the minimum requirements and best practices for establishing, implementing, maintaining, and improving an Energy Management System (EnMS).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Module 2 of PEPET745 specifically emphasizes *why* frameworks exist (to institutionalize energy savings rather than treat them as one-time projects) and *which* standards govern them globally and nationally.

## 1.2 Key Terminology — The KTU Lexicon

| Term | Full Form | Meaning in EnMS Context |
|------|-----------|-------------------------|
| **EnMS** | Energy Management System | The complete set of interrelated elements used to establish an energy policy and objectives |
| **EnB** | Energy Baseline | The quantitative reference point(s) that provide a basis for comparison of energy performance |
| **EnPI** | Energy Performance Indicator | A measurable quantity or metric used to track and compare energy performance |
| **M&V** | Measurement & Verification | The process of quantifying energy savings achieved through specific interventions |
| **EC Act** | Energy Conservation Act | India's primary legislation for promoting energy efficiency |
| **PAT** | Perform, Achieve and Trade | A market-based mechanism under EC Act for industries |
| **SDA** | Specific Energy Consumption | Energy used per unit of output (e.g., kWh/tonne) |

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: "A Company's Gym Membership for Energy"**
>
> Think of an Energy Management Framework as a *fitness regimen* applied to an entire organization. Just as a person who wants to get fit doesn't just "go to the gym once" but follows a structured routine — measuring weight (baseline), setting goals (objectives), exercising regularly (action plans), eating well (operational control), checking the scale weekly (monitoring), and adjusting the diet (management review) — a company that adopts an energy framework doesn't just install one LED bulb. Instead, it commits to an ongoing, measurable, leadership-driven transformation of how it consumes energy.
>
> The **standards** (ISO 50001, EC Act) are like the *certified personal trainer and dietitian* — they ensure the company follows a globally-proven discipline rather than guessing.

## 1.4 Why Frameworks & Standards Are Necessary

- **Avoids Ad-hoc Practices:** Without a framework, energy-saving initiatives remain isolated, dependent on individual champions, and disappear when those people leave.
- **Enables Verification:** Standardized M&V protocols (e.g., **IPMVP** — International Performance Measurement and Verification Protocol) allow quantification of savings.
- **Legal & Regulatory Compliance:** India's **EC Act 2001** mandates designated consumers (DCs) to comply with BEE norms.
- **Market Access & Reputation:** ISO 50001 certification is increasingly a procurement criterion in global supply chains.
- **Financial Returns:** Structured EnMS implementations routinely deliver **10–30 %** annual energy cost reduction in industrial setups.

## 1.5 Global vs National Standards — A Snapshot

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 Scheme expects students to differentiate between **international voluntary standards** and **national mandatory regulations**. Confusing these two categories is one of the most common mark-losing errors in board examinations.

| Category | Standard / Act | Nature | Governing Body |
|----------|---------------|--------|----------------|
| International (Voluntary) | **ISO 50001:2018** | Voluntary | ISO, Geneva |
| International (Voluntary) | ISO 14001:2015 | Voluntary | ISO, Geneva |
| International (Methodology) | IPMVP | Voluntary | EVO (Efficiency Valuation Organization) |
| National (Mandatory) | **Energy Conservation Act, 2001** | Statutory | Govt. of India |
| National (Mandatory) | PAT Cycle | Regulatory | BEE, India |
| National (Voluntary) | BEE Star Rating | Voluntary labelling | BEE, India |

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Architecture of an Energy Management Framework

Every mature energy management framework — whether ISO 50001, ISO 14001, or a custom corporate one — rests on **four foundational pillars** mapped onto the **Plan-Do-Check-Act (PDCA)** cycle introduced by **Walter Shewhart** and popularized by **W. Edwards Deming**.

### Pillar 1: PLAN — Strategic Foundation

- **Energy Policy:** A top-management-endorsed declaration of commitment to energy performance. Must be appropriate to the nature and scale of energy use.
- **Energy Review:** A data-driven analysis of current energy consumption, energy sources, and past/ongoing improvement projects. Output: identification of **Significant Energy Uses (SEUs)**.
- **Energy Baseline (EnB):** A quantified reference (typically annual) against which future performance is compared.
- **Energy Performance Indicators (EnPIs):** Metrics selected to compare energy performance over time — must be **appropriate, measurable, and reproducible**.
- **Objectives, Targets, and Action Plans:** Quantified improvement goals with assigned responsibilities, resources, timelines, and KPIs.

### Pillar 2: DO — Tactical Implementation

- **Competence, Training, and Awareness:** Workforce upskilling on energy practices.
- **Communication:** Internal and external reporting on EnMS performance.
- **Operational Control:** Documented procedures, set-points, and maintenance schedules for SEUs.
- **Design and Procurement Criteria:** Energy efficiency as a mandatory consideration in capital project evaluation.

### Pillar 3: CHECK — Performance Verification

- **Monitoring, Measurement, and Analysis:** Routine tracking of EnPIs against objectives.
- **Evaluation of Compliance:** Periodic legal/regulatory compliance audits.
- **Internal Audit:** Systematic audits of the EnMS itself.
- **Non-conformities, Incidents, and Corrective Actions:** Documented response to deviations.

### Pillar 4: ACT — Continual Improvement

- **Management Review:** Top-management evaluation of the entire EnMS at planned intervals.
- **Continual Improvement:** Iterative enhancement guided by audit findings, reviews, and data.

## 2.2 ISO 50001:2018 — Clause-wise Structural Map

> [!IMPORTANT]
> ISO 50001 follows the **High-Level Structure (HLS)**, also called **Annex SL**, which is the same 10-clause skeleton used by ISO 9001 and ISO 14001. KTU board exams frequently ask students to "list the clauses of ISO 50001."

| Clause | Title | Core Requirement |
|--------|-------|------------------|
| 1 | Scope | Boundaries and applicability of the EnMS |
| 2 | Normative References | Documents indispensable to the application |
| 3 | Terms and Definitions | Glossary of EnMS terminology |
| 4 | Context of the Organization | External/internal issues, needs of interested parties |
| 5 | Leadership | Top-management commitment, policy, roles |
| 6 | Planning | Risks, opportunities, objectives, EnPIs, EnB |
| 7 | Support | Resources, competence, awareness, communication, documented info |
| 8 | Operation | Operational planning & control, design, procurement |
| 9 | Performance Evaluation | Monitoring, evaluation, audit, management review |
| 10 | Improvement | Non-conformity, corrective action, continual improvement |

## 2.3 The Six-Step Energy Review Methodology (KTU High-Yield)

ISO 50001 mandates a **six-step structured energy review** for every EnMS implementation:

1. **Step 1 — Measure Past & Present Energy Use:** Collect 12–24 months of utility bills, fuel data, and process records.
2. **Step 2 — Conduct an Energy Analysis:** Decompose total energy use by site, system, process, and energy type.
3. **Step 3 — Identify Significant Energy Uses (SEUs):** Rank by consumption magnitude × criticality to operations.
4. **Step 4 — Determine Current Energy Performance & EnPIs:** Establish EnB and select EnPIs (e.g., kWh/tonne, MJ/km).
5. **Step 5 — Identify Improvement Opportunities:** Generate intervention ideas via benchmarking, audits, and engineering analysis.
6. **Step 6 — Prioritize and Document:** Rank opportunities by NPV, payback, and strategic fit; document in the **Energy Action Plan**.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **high-yield quantitative toolkit** for Module 2 calculations. Memorize the units and typical reference values.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units |
|---|---------|---------|-------------------|
| 1 | Specific Energy Consumption (SEC) | $$SEC \;=\; \frac{E_{total}}{Q_{output}}$$ | $E_{total}$ = total energy in **kWh** or **MJ**; $Q_{output}$ = physical output (tonne, kg, km) |
| 2 | Energy Baseline (EnB) — simple average | $$EnB \;=\; \frac{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} E_i}{n}$$ | $E_i$ = annual energy use in year $i$ in **kWh**; $n$ = number of baseline years |
| 3 | EnPI Normalization | $$EnPI_{norm} \;=\; \frac{E_{actual}}{E_{baseline}} \;\times\; 100$$ | Result in **%**; <100% indicates improvement |
| 4 | Energy Performance Index (per unit output) | $$EnPI \;=\; \frac{E_{consumed}}{P_{produced}}$$ | $E$ in **kWh**; $P$ in **tonnes / m³ / units** |
| 5 | Cost of Wasted Energy | $$C_{waste} \;=\; (E_{actual} - E_{theoretical}) \;\times\; C_{unit}$$ | $C_{unit}$ = energy unit cost in **₹/kWh** or **₹/MJ** |
| 6 | % Energy Improvement | $$\%\Delta E \;=\; \frac{E_{baseline} - E_{current}}{E_{baseline}} \times 100$$ | Result in **%** |
| 7 | Carbon Dioxide Equivalent (Energy-related) | $$CO_{2e} \;=\; E_{consumed} \;\times\; EF_{fuel}$$ | $EF_{fuel}$ = emission factor in **kg CO₂e/kWh** (e.g., grid India ≈ **0.82**) |
| 8 | Simple Payback Period | $$SPP \;=\; \frac{C_{investment}}{\Delta C_{annual}}$$ | Result in **years** |
| 9 | Net Present Value (Energy Project) | $$NPV \;=\; \sum_{t=1}^{T} \frac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t} - C_0$$ | $r$ = discount rate; $CF_t$ = annual cash flow in **₹** |
| 10 | Annual Energy Cost Reduction | $$\Delta C_{annual} \;=\; E_{saved} \;\times\; C_{unit}$$ | $E_{saved}$ in **kWh/year**; $C_{unit}$ in **₹/kWh** |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Trap:** Many students confuse the **Energy Baseline (EnB)** with the **Energy Performance Indicator (EnPI)**. EnB is a *static reference value* of energy consumption; EnPI is the *ratio* (or metric) that changes with performance. They are not interchangeable.

## 2.5 Comparison Table — ISO 50001 vs ISO 14001 vs EC Act 2001

| Dimension | ISO 50001:2018 | ISO 14001:2015 | EC Act 2001 (India) |
|-----------|----------------|----------------|---------------------|
| Focus | Energy performance | Environmental performance | Energy efficiency & conservation |
| Origin | International (ISO) | International (ISO) | National (Parliament of India) |
| Mandatory? | Voluntary | Voluntary | **Mandatory** for DCs |
| Key Driver | EnB, EnPI, SEU | Aspects, Impacts, Compliance | PAT, Standards & Labelling |
| Penalty for Non-Compliance | Loss of certification | Loss of certification | **Fines up to ₹10 lakh + imprisonment** |
| Energy Audit | EnMS Internal Audit | EMS Audit | **Mandatory Energy Audit** by BEE-accredited auditors |
| Target Audience | All energy-using organizations | All organizations impacting environment | **Industries in 15+ sectors** with connected load ≥ 1 MW |

## 2.6 Real-World Utility of These Frameworks

- **Industrial Sector:** A cement plant adopting ISO 50001 can achieve **5–15 %** energy savings within 2 years by systematically managing its SEUs (kiln, raw mill, cement mill).
- **Commercial Buildings:** EnMS enables LEED/BREEAM-aligned operation and BEE star rating achievement.
- **Data Centers:** PDCA-driven PUE (Power Usage Effectiveness) optimization.
- **Utility Compliance:** Indian DCs in aluminium, cement, chlor-alkali, and pulp & paper sectors must comply with PAT targets.
- **Supply Chain:** Multinational OEMs (e.g., automotive majors) require ISO 50001 certification from tier-1 suppliers.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Establishing an Energy Baseline (EnB)

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
A manufacturing facility has recorded the following annual electricity consumption over the last 4 years:

| Year | Electricity Consumption (kWh) | Production Output (tonnes) |
|------|------------------------------|----------------------------|
| 2020 | 9,80,000 | 4,200 |
| 2021 | 10,50,000 | 4,500 |
| 2022 | 11,20,000 | 4,800 |
| 2023 | 11,60,000 | 5,000 |

Compute:
1. The **Energy Baseline (EnB)** for electricity.
2. The **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)** for each year.
3. The **EnPI normalized** for 2023.
4. The **% energy improvement** if a new technology brings 2024 consumption to 10,80,000 kWh for 5,200 tonnes of production.

### Step 1 — Compute the Energy Baseline (EnB)

We choose a 4-year average of electricity consumption as the EnB.

$$
\begin{aligned}
EnB &= \frac{E_{2020} + E_{2021} + E_{2022} + E_{2023}}{n} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{9{,}80{,}000 + 10{,}50{,}000 + 11{,}20{,}000 + 11{,}60{,}000}{4} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{43{,}10{,}000}{4} \\[6pt]
&= 10{,}77{,}500 \ \text{kWh}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** The EnB is **10,77,500 kWh/year**. Any future year below this represents gross energy improvement; however, since output also changed, the *normalized* metric is more meaningful.

### Step 2 — Compute Specific Energy Consumption (SEC) per Year

Using the formula $SEC = E_{total} / Q_{output}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
SEC_{2020} &= \frac{9{,}80{,}000}{4{,}200} = 233.33 \ \text{kWh/tonne} \\[4pt]
SEC_{2021} &= \frac{10{,}50{,}000}{4{,}500} = 233.33 \ \text{kWh/tonne} \\[4pt]
SEC_{2022} &= \frac{11{,}20{,}000}{4{,}800} = 233.33 \ \text{kWh/tonne} \\[4pt]
SEC_{2023} &= \frac{11{,}60{,}000}{5{,}000} = 232.00 \ \text{kWh/tonne}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Insight:** Gross energy has risen year-on-year, but **SEC has remained nearly constant** (232–233 kWh/tonne), indicating that the energy increase is *proportional* to the production increase. This is a *constant-intensity* baseline.

### Step 3 — Compute EnPI Normalized for 2023

To express 2023 performance relative to the EnB in percentage form:

$$
\begin{aligned}
EnPI_{norm,\,2023} &= \frac{E_{2023}}{EnB} \times 100 \\[6pt]
&= \frac{11{,}60{,}000}{10{,}77{,}500} \times 100 \\[6pt]
&= 107.66 \ \%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** A value *above 100 %* means the current year consumed *more* than the baseline. In 2023, electricity was **7.66 % above** the 4-year average — but recall this must be *normalized to output* for true performance assessment.

### Step 4 — Compute % Energy Improvement for 2024

Given 2024: $E = 10{,}80{,}000$ kWh and $Q = 5{,}200$ tonnes.

First, compute 2024 SEC:

$$
\begin{aligned}
SEC_{2024} &= \frac{10{,}80{,}000}{5{,}200} = 207.69 \ \text{kWh/tonne}
\end{aligned}
$$

Use 2023 SEC as the reference baseline (since 2023 is the last pre-intervention year):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%\Delta E &= \frac{SEC_{2023} - SEC_{2024}}{SEC_{2023}} \times 100 \\[6pt]
&= \frac{232.00 - 207.69}{232.00} \times 100 \\[6pt]
&= \frac{24.31}{232.00} \times 100 \\[6pt]
&= 10.48 \ \%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conclusion:** The intervention has produced a **10.48 % improvement** in specific energy consumption. This is the type of quantifiable, verifiable savings a board examiner expects a KTU student to demonstrate.

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — CO₂ Equivalent Calculation

**Problem Statement:**
A facility saved **5,00,000 kWh** of grid electricity in a year. Using the **CEA-2023 emission factor** for the Indian grid of **0.82 kg CO₂e/kWh**, calculate the **annual CO₂ equivalent avoided emission**.

### Step-by-Step Solution

$$
\begin{aligned}
CO_{2e\,avoided} &= E_{saved} \times EF_{grid} \\[6pt]
&= 5{,}00{,}000 \ \text{kWh} \times 0.82 \ \frac{\text{kg CO}_2\text{e}}{\text{kWh}} \\[6pt]
&= 4{,}10{,}000 \ \text{kg CO}_2\text{e} \\[6pt]
&= 410 \ \text{tonnes CO}_2\text{e}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Engineering Insight:** Quantifying avoided CO₂ emissions is a **mandatory reporting requirement** under ISO 50001 (clause 9.1) and is increasingly tied to **Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanisms (CBAM)** in international trade.

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Selecting the Right EnPI

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The choice of EnPI determines whether savings claims are *credible*. KTU students should be able to justify the choice.

| Plant Type | SEU | Recommended EnPI | Justification |
|------------|-----|------------------|---------------|
| Cement plant | Cement mill | kWh/tonne of cement | Output is the primary driver of energy use |
| Steel plant | Electric arc furnace | kWh/tonne of liquid steel | Output-based; consistent with IEA benchmarks |
| Commercial HVAC | Chiller plant | kWh/(TR-hour) | Cooling demand is the most direct driver |
| Data center | IT + Cooling | PUE = Total Facility Energy / IT Energy | Industry-standard (The Green Grid) |
| Sugar mill | Crushing season | kWh/tonne of cane crushed | Seasonal variability; output is reliable |
| Hospital | Steam generation | kg of steam / m² of floor area | Occupancy-independent baseline |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Figure 1 — The PDCA Cycle Applied to Energy Management

The Plan-Do-Check-Act cycle is the *engine* of every Energy Management Framework. The diagram below shows how each phase of ISO 50001 maps onto the PDCA loop and produces the continual improvement cycle required for certification.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[PLAN: Energy Policy, Review, EnB, EnPI, Objectives, Action Plans] --> B[DO: Operational Control, Training, Communication, Design, Procurement]
    B --> C[CHECK: Monitor, Measure, Analyze, Internal Audit, Compliance Eval]
    C --> D[ACT: Management Review, Nonconformity, Corrective Action, Continual Improvement]
    D -.feedback.-> A
    C -.feedback.-> A
    B -.feedback.-> A
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Solid arrows represent the *forward* sequence of activities; dashed arrows represent the *feedback* loops that close the cycle and drive iterative improvement. This is exactly the topology shown in **ISO 50001:2018, Figure 1** in the official standard.

## 4.2 Figure 2 — Block-Level Architecture of ISO 50001:2018 EnMS

The following functional block diagram maps the **10-clause High-Level Structure** of ISO 50001:2018 to the four PDCA quadrants. It is the *master blueprint* of an Energy Management Framework.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Clause 4: Context of Organization] --> P2[Clause 5: Leadership]
    P2 --> P3[Clause 6: Planning - EnB, EnPI, Objectives]
    P3 --> D1[Clause 7: Support - Resources, Competence, Communication]
    D1 --> D2[Clause 8: Operation - SEU Control, Design, Procurement]
    D2 --> C1[Clause 9: Performance Evaluation - Monitor, Audit, Mgmt Review]
    C1 --> A1[Clause 10: Improvement - Corrective Action, Continual Improvement]
    A1 -.-> P1
```

> **Engineering Interpretation:** Notice how **Clause 10 (Improvement)** feeds back into **Clause 4 (Context)** — this is the *Annex SL* requirement that the organization's context be *re-evaluated* in light of new risks, opportunities, and performance data each cycle.

## 4.3 Figure 3 — The Six-Step Energy Review Methodology (ISO 50001)

The energy review is the *analytical engine* that produces the data inputs (EnB, EnPI, SEUs) needed by the planning phase. The flowchart below represents the **mandatory six-step sequence**.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1: Measure Past and Present Energy Use] --> S2[Step 2: Conduct Energy Analysis - Sources, End-Uses, Drivers]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Identify Significant Energy Uses - SEU Matrix]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Determine EnB and EnPIs]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Identify Improvement Opportunities - Audit, Benchmark, Brainstorm]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Prioritize and Document - NPV, Payback, Action Plan]
    S6 --> Plan[Inputs to Energy Action Plan - Clause 6.2]
```

> **Board Exam Tip:** A common KTU 2024 question asks: *"List the six steps of the energy review methodology in ISO 50001."* Memorize them in this exact order.

## 4.4 Figure 4 — Compliance and Reporting Flow under EC Act 2001

The Energy Conservation Act, 2001, in conjunction with the **PAT (Perform, Achieve & Trade) scheme**, imposes a *mandatory annual reporting and compliance loop* on **Designated Consumers (DCs)**. The flowchart below shows how data flows from the plant to the regulator and back.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F1[Designated Consumer - Plant Operator] -->|Mandatory Energy Audit Report| F2[BEE-Accredited Energy Auditor]
    F2 -->|Verified Audit Report| F3[Bureau of Energy Efficiency - BEE]
    F3 -->|PAT Target Setting| F4[Specific Energy Consumption Reduction Targets]
    F4 --> F1
    F1 -->|Annual Compliance Reporting| F3
    F3 -->|Non-Compliance Notice + Penalty| F1
```

> **Key Insight:** Unlike ISO 50001, the EC Act 2001 framework is *coercive* — non-compliance attracts **financial penalties** of up to **₹10 lakh** and **imprisonment up to 3 years** for repeated violations under **Section 26 of the Act**.
