# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. Define *Energy Baseline (EnB)* and explain its role in an Energy Management System. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model)] — 3 Marks

**Model Answer:**

> An **Energy Baseline (EnB)** is the **quantitative reference point(s)** that provide a basis for comparison of an organization's energy performance. It is established during the energy review process and typically uses historical data (12–24 months) adjusted for variables that significantly affect energy use (such as production volume, weather, or occupancy).
>
> **Role in EnMS:**
> - Acts as the *anchor* against which all future performance is measured.
> - Enables objective *year-on-year* comparison of energy efficiency.
> - Forms the foundation for setting **realistic EnPIs** and improvement targets.
> - Required as a documented record under **Clause 6.3 of ISO 50001:2018**.
>
> For example, a factory's EnB might be **10,77,500 kWh/year** based on a 4-year average; any deviation above or below this value is evaluated for root causes and improvement actions.

---

### Question 2. Differentiate between **ISO 50001** and **ISO 14001** in terms of focus, scope, and applicability. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)] — 3 Marks

**Model Answer:**

| Dimension | ISO 50001:2018 | ISO 14001:2015 |
|-----------|----------------|----------------|
| **Primary Focus** | Energy performance & efficiency | Environmental performance & impact |
| **Scope** | All forms of energy use and consumption | All environmental aspects (air, water, waste, biodiversity) |
| **Mandatory Tools** | EnB, EnPI, SEU, Energy Review | Aspects-Impacts Register, Compliance Obligations |
| **Applicability** | Energy-intensive organizations (industry, buildings, transport) | Any organization affecting the environment |
| **Outcome Metric** | Energy Performance Indicators (EnPIs) | Environmental Performance Indicators (EPIs) |
| **Legal Status** | International voluntary standard | International voluntary standard |
| **Specific Mandate** | Mandatory **energy review** and **energy audit** | Mandatory **aspect-impact assessment** |

> **Conclusion:** Both share the **Annex SL High-Level Structure**, but ISO 50001 is *narrower* (energy-only) and *more quantified* (EnB & EnPIs), while ISO 14001 is *broader* (multi-media environmental management) and *more qualitative* in many cases.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Internal Choice Pattern:** Each Part-B question offers two sub-questions (a) and (b), typically **7 + 7** marks. We provide two full alternative questions (Q1 and Q2) for practice.

### Question A. [14 Marks]

**Q1 (a)** Explain in detail the **High-Level Structure (Annex SL)** of **ISO 50001:2018**. List all **ten clauses** and describe the core content of Clauses 4, 6, 9, and 10. [7 Marks] **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)]**

**Q1 (b)** A chemical process plant recorded the following specific energy consumption (SEC) over 4 years:

| Year | Production (tonnes) | Energy (MWh) |
|------|---------------------|--------------|
| 2020 | 2,000 | 1,800 |
| 2021 | 2,400 | 2,040 |
| 2022 | 2,800 | 2,240 |
| 2023 | 3,200 | 2,400 |

Compute:
(i) Year-wise **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)** in **kWh/tonne**.
(ii) The **4-year average EnB (in kWh)**.
(iii) The **% energy improvement** in 2023 vs the average SEC of 2020 and 2021.
(iv) Comment on whether the 2023 performance qualifies as a *continual improvement* under **Clause 10 of ISO 50001**. [7 Marks]

---

#### Model Solution for Q1 (a)

**High-Level Structure (Annex SL) of ISO 50001:2018:**

ISO 50001:2018 adopts the **10-clause Annex SL structure** common to all modern ISO management system standards. The ten clauses are:

1. **Scope**
2. **Normative References**
3. **Terms and Definitions**
4. **Context of the Organization**
5. **Leadership**
6. **Planning**
7. **Support**
8. **Operation**
9. **Performance Evaluation**
10. **Improvement**

**Description of Key Clauses:**

- **Clause 4 – Context of the Organization:** Requires the organization to identify *external and internal issues* (e.g., energy regulations, climate policy, technology trends) and the *needs and expectations of interested parties* (e.g., shareholders, regulators, customers) that are relevant to the EnMS. The organization must also define the **scope and boundaries** of its EnMS.

- **Clause 6 – Planning:** Addresses **risks and opportunities** related to energy performance; mandates an **energy review**; requires establishment of **EnB**, **EnPIs**, **energy objectives**, and **action plans** with measurable targets, timelines, resources, and assigned responsibilities.

- **Clause 9 – Performance Evaluation:** Requires **monitoring, measurement, analysis, and evaluation** of energy performance; periodic **evaluation of compliance** with legal/other requirements; conduct of **internal audits** at planned intervals; and a top-management **review** of the EnMS.

- **Clause 10 – Improvement:** Mandates identification and handling of **non-conformities** with documented **corrective action**; encourages the organization to actively pursue **continual improvement** of energy performance and the EnMS itself, using audit results, management review outcomes, and EnPI trends as inputs.

> **Conclusion:** The HLS structure ensures *compatibility and integration* with other ISO management systems (ISO 9001, ISO 14001), enabling organizations to implement a single, unified management system with energy as one of several focus areas.

---

#### Model Solution for Q1 (b)

**(i) Year-wise Specific Energy Consumption (SEC):**

Using $SEC = E_{total} / Q_{output}$ with consistent units (convert MWh → kWh by multiplying by 1000):

$$
\begin{aligned}
SEC_{2020} &= \frac{1{,}800 \times 1000}{2{,}000} = 900.00 \ \text{kWh/tonne} \\[4pt]
SEC_{2021} &= \frac{2{,}040 \times 1000}{2{,}400} = 850.00 \ \text{kWh/tonne} \\[4pt]
SEC_{2022} &= \frac{2{,}240 \times 1000}{2{,}800} = 800.00 \ \text{kWh/tonne} \\[4pt]
SEC_{2023} &= \frac{2{,}400 \times 1000}{3{,}200} = 750.00 \ \text{kWh/tonne}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) 4-Year Average EnB (in kWh):**

The EnB is the average of total annual energy consumption (in kWh) over the baseline period.

$$
\begin{aligned}
EnB &= \frac{1{,}800{,}000 + 2{,}040{,}000 + 2{,}240{,}000 + 2{,}400{,}000}{4} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{8{,}480{,}000}{4} \\[6pt]
&= 2{,}120{,}000 \ \text{kWh}
\end{aligned}
$$

> Note: Some interpretations normalize the EnB per unit of output (e.g., kWh/tonne) when production varies significantly. The average SEC over the period is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
SEC_{avg} &= \frac{900 + 850 + 800 + 750}{4} = 825.00 \ \text{kWh/tonne}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) % Energy Improvement in 2023 vs Average SEC of 2020 and 2021:**

First, the average SEC of 2020 and 2021:

$$
\begin{aligned}
SEC_{ref} &= \frac{SEC_{2020} + SEC_{2021}}{2} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{900 + 850}{2} = 875.00 \ \text{kWh/tonne}
\end{aligned}
$$

Then, the percentage improvement:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%\Delta E &= \frac{SEC_{ref} - SEC_{2023}}{SEC_{ref}} \times 100 \\[6pt]
&= \frac{875 - 750}{875} \times 100 \\[6pt]
&= \frac{125}{875} \times 100 \\[6pt]
&= 14.29 \ \%
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iv) Comment on Continual Improvement (Clause 10 of ISO 50001):**

> The 2023 SEC of **750 kWh/tonne** is **14.29 % lower** than the 2020–2021 average, and the year-on-year SEC has been declining *monotonically* (900 → 850 → 800 → 750). This **consistent, quantifiable downward trend** in the EnPI qualifies as *continual improvement* as required under **Clause 10.3 of ISO 50001:2018**. The improvement is *measurable* (14.29 % vs reference period), *sustained* (4 consecutive years), and *systematic* (aligned with the organization's energy objectives and action plans). Therefore, the organization can validly claim continual improvement during its management review.

---

### Question B. [14 Marks — Alternative Choice]

**Q2 (a)** Explain the **Perform, Achieve and Trade (PAT) scheme** under the **Energy Conservation Act, 2001**. Discuss its coverage, target-setting methodology, and benefits. [7 Marks] **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model)]**

**Q2 (b)** A foundry installs a new VFD (Variable Frequency Drive) on a motor. The pre-installation data and post-installation data are:

| Parameter | Before VFD | After VFD |
|-----------|------------|-----------|
| Average Power Draw (kW) | 45.0 | 32.5 |
| Annual Operating Hours | 6,000 | 6,000 |
| Electricity Tariff (₹/kWh) | 7.50 | 7.50 |
| Investment (₹) | — | 4,50,000 |

Calculate:
(i) Annual energy saved (kWh).
(ii) Annual cost savings (₹).
(iii) Simple Payback Period (years).
(iv) NPV over 5 years at a 10 % discount rate, assuming constant annual savings. [7 Marks]

---

#### Model Solution for Q2 (a)

**The Perform, Achieve and Trade (PAT) Scheme:**

PAT is a **market-based regulatory mechanism** introduced by the **Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE)** under the **Energy Conservation Act, 2001**, designed to drive energy efficiency in energy-intensive Indian industries. It is the **world's largest energy efficiency trading programme** for Designated Consumers (DCs).

**Coverage:**

- Initially launched in 2012 for **8 energy-intensive sectors**: Thermal Power, Cement, Iron & Steel, Aluminium, Fertilizer, Pulp & Paper, Chlor-Alkali, and Textiles.
- The programme has since been expanded to additional sectors including **Petrochemicals, Refineries, Distribution, Railways, and more**.
- Participation is **mandatory** for all DCs that meet threshold levels of energy consumption (notified by the Central Government).

**Target-Setting Methodology:**

1. **Baseline Determination:** The baseline Specific Energy Consumption (SEC) is established from historical data of each DC.
2. **Sectoral Benchmarking:** BEE assigns each DC an **energy efficiency improvement target** (e.g., 4–6 % reduction in SEC over a 3-year cycle).
3. **Cycle Duration:** Each PAT cycle is typically **3 years** long; multiple cycles have been completed (Cycle I to Cycle VI as of 2024).
4. **Achievement Verification:** Energy audits by BEE-accredited auditors verify achievement of targets.
5. **Trading of ESCerts:** DCs that **over-achieve** their targets receive **Energy Saving Certificates (ESCerts)** — one ESCert = **1 toe (tonne of oil equivalent)** saved. DCs that **under-achieve** must purchase ESCerts from the market to compensate.

**Benefits:**

- **Cost-Effective Compliance:** Inefficient plants buy certificates; efficient plants earn revenue from them.
- **National-Level Energy Security:** Reduction in fossil fuel imports.
- **Carbon Co-Benefit:** Tangible reductions in CO₂ emissions.
- **Decarbonization Pathway:** PAT lays the groundwork for India's **carbon credit trading** under the **Carbon Credit Trading Scheme, 2023**.
- **Job Creation:** Drives demand for energy auditors, ESCOs, and energy-efficient equipment.

---

#### Model Solution for Q2 (b)

**Given Data:**
- $P_{before} = 45.0$ kW; $P_{after} = 32.5$ kW
- $H = 6{,}000$ hours/year
- Tariff = ₹7.50/kWh
- $C_0$ = ₹4,50,000
- Discount rate $r = 10\% = 0.10$; $T = 5$ years

**(i) Annual Energy Saved (kWh):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta P &= P_{before} - P_{after} = 45.0 - 32.5 = 12.5 \ \text{kW} \\[6pt]
E_{saved} &= \Delta P \times H = 12.5 \times 6{,}000 = 75{,}000 \ \text{kWh/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Annual Cost Savings (₹):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta C_{annual} &= E_{saved} \times \text{Tariff} \\[4pt]
&= 75{,}000 \times 7.50 = 5{,}62{,}500 \ \text{₹/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Simple Payback Period (years):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
SPP &= \frac{C_0}{\Delta C_{annual}} = \frac{4{,}50{,}000}{5{,}62{,}500} \\[6pt]
&= 0.80 \ \text{years} \ (\approx 9.6 \ \text{months})
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** A payback period of less than **1 year** is exceptional and indicates a *highly attractive* energy project.

**(iv) NPV over 5 years at r = 10 %:**

Assuming constant annual savings, the present value of savings over 5 years is an *annuity*:

$$
\begin{aligned}
PV_{savings} &= \Delta C_{annual} \times \frac{1 - (1 + r)^{-T}}{r} \\[6pt]
&= 5{,}62{,}500 \times \frac{1 - (1.10)^{-5}}{0.10} \\[6pt]
&= 5{,}62{,}500 \times \frac{1 - 0.6209}{0.10} \\[6pt]
&= 5{,}62{,}500 \times \frac{0.3791}{0.10} \\[6pt]
&= 5{,}62{,}500 \times 3.7908 \\[6pt]
&= 21{,}32{,}325 \ \text{₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
NPV &= PV_{savings} - C_0 \\[4pt]
&= 21{,}32{,}325 - 4{,}50{,}000 \\[4pt]
&= 16{,}82{,}325 \ \text{₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conclusion:** With a **positive NPV of approximately ₹16.82 lakh** and a payback of less than one year, the VFD installation is a *highly viable* energy conservation project. This kind of project, with rapid payback, is precisely what the **PAT scheme** is designed to incentivize and what **BEE** promotes through its **Standards & Labelling** programme.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in Energy Management Framework Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing EnB with EnPI (up to 2 marks lost):** EnB is the *baseline* (a number); EnPI is the *indicator* (a ratio). State both clearly in your answer.
> 2. **Omitting "Mandate" Status:** The EC Act 2001 is **mandatory** for DCs; ISO 50001 is **voluntary**. Examiners deduct marks when you claim ISO 50001 is mandatory.
> 3. **Wrong Unit on SEC:** Always write the unit explicitly — e.g., **kWh/tonne** or **MJ/kg**. Bare numbers without units are penalized.
> 4. **Skipping "Continual Improvement":** In Clause 10 questions, the term *continual* (not *continuous*) is mandatory per ISO wording.
> 5. **PAT Description Error:** PAT is **not** a *carbon* trading scheme; it is an **energy efficiency** (ESCert) trading scheme, even though it yields carbon co-benefits.
> 6. **Forgetting Clauses 1, 2, 3:** When asked to list all 10 clauses, students often miss Clauses 1, 2, and 3. A common mark-deduction error.
> 7. **PDCA Phases Mismatched:** Operational control belongs to **DO**; internal audit belongs to **CHECK**; management review belongs to **ACT**. Wrong mapping = lost marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2: Energy Management Frameworks and Standards**

- **Framework Definition:** A structured, documented system for managing energy; the international reference is **ISO 50001:2018**.
- **Core Pillars:** **PDCA cycle** is the foundation — Plan (Policy, EnB, EnPI, Objectives), Do (Operational Control, Training), Check (Monitoring, Internal Audit), Act (Management Review, Continual Improvement).
- **ISO 50001 Clauses:** Memorize all 10 clauses of the **Annex SL High-Level Structure**; especially emphasize Clauses 6 (Planning), 9 (Performance Evaluation), and 10 (Improvement).
- **Energy Review:** A **six-step methodology** — Measure → Analyze → Identify SEU → Determine EnB/EnPI → Identify Opportunities → Prioritize and Document.
- **EnB Definition:** The *static* quantitative reference (e.g., kWh/year) for comparing energy performance.
- **EnPI Definition:** A *measurable ratio* (e.g., kWh/tonne, PUE) that tracks energy performance over time.
- **SEU (Significant Energy Use):** High-consumption or high-impact end-uses that deserve focused management attention.
- **ISO 50001 vs ISO 14001:** ISO 50001 = energy focus, EnB, EnPI; ISO 14001 = multi-media environmental focus, aspects-impacts.
- **EC Act 2001:** India's **mandatory** national legislation; enforced by **BEE**; covers DCs in 15+ sectors; non-compliance invites penalties up to **₹10 lakh + 3 years imprisonment**.
- **PAT Scheme:** A market-based mechanism under EC Act; **ESCerts** are traded; **1 ESCert = 1 toe** of energy saved.
- **Key Formulae to Memorize:** SEC, EnB (average), EnPI normalized (%), % energy improvement, CO₂e, SPP, NPV.
- **CEA Grid Emission Factor (2023):** Approximately **0.82 kg CO₂e/kWh** for the Indian grid.
- **Six-Sigma of Energy Management:** Use **PDCA + EnB + EnPI + SEU + Action Plan + Continual Improvement** as the *operating vocabulary* in every KTU answer.
- **Continual vs Continuous:** Continual = *ongoing incremental*; Continuous = *uninterrupted*. ISO 50001 mandates **continual**, not continuous.
- **Real-World Benchmark:** A well-implemented EnMS typically delivers **10–30 %** energy savings within 2–3 years of full implementation.

> **One-Line Mantra for Board Exam:**
> *"A framework turns energy savings from an accident into a discipline."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Energy Audit and Its Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET745 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND AUDIT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Energy Audit and Its Types

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Energy Audit (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Energy Audit** is the systematic, documented verification, monitoring, and analysis of the use of energy in a building, process, or system to identify energy consumption patterns, quantify energy losses, and recommend improvements to achieve energy efficiency, cost reduction, and environmental sustainability while maintaining occupant comfort and production output.

According to the **Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE), Government of India**, an Energy Audit is defined as:
> *"The verification, monitoring and analysis of the use of energy to identify energy conservation opportunities and recommend changes to achieve energy savings without affecting the quality of the product/process."*

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEPET745)** framework, an energy audit is the foundational engineering exercise that converts raw utility bills (electricity, thermal, fuel) into **actionable data**: where energy is used, how much is wasted, and what is the cost-effective remedy.

**Standard Energy Audit Metrics (as per BEE / ISO 50002):**
- **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)** – Energy used per unit of product/output (e.g., kWh/tonne, MJ/kg)
- **Energy Performance Indicator (EnPI)** – kWh per square meter per year, or GJ per lakh-rupee production
- **Power Factor (PF)** – Ideally maintained above **0.95** (lagging)
- **Tonnes of CO₂ avoided per year** – the carbon footprint reduction metric

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Human Health Check-up" for Industry

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Box:** Imagine a patient going for a comprehensive medical check-up. The doctor measures blood pressure, sugar, cholesterol, asks lifestyle questions, identifies problems (high BP, obesity), and prescribes a treatment plan (exercise, diet, medicine). After a few months, another check-up is done to measure improvement.
>
> An **Energy Audit** works exactly the same way for a factory or building:
> - **Patient** = Plant/Building
> - **Blood Test/Reports** = Energy meters, logged data, bills
> - **Doctor** = Certified Energy Auditor (CEA – Certified Energy Auditor, BEE Empanelled)
> - **Diagnosis** = Identification of energy waste areas
> - **Prescription** = Energy Conservation Measures (ECMs)
> - **Follow-up** = Post-implementation verification audit (M&V — Measurement and Verification)

Just as one check-up cannot fix every ailment, an audit is **not a one-time activity** but a **cyclical, continuous improvement process** aligned with the **Plan–Do–Check–Act (PDCA)** cycle of ISO 50001.

## 1.3 Objectives of an Energy Audit

A typical KTU 2024 semester answer should list these objectives:

1. **Quantify** energy usage and identify wastage hotspots.
2. **Benchmark** performance against industry standards (BAT — Best Available Techniques).
3. **Recommend** Energy Conservation Measures (ECMs) with engineering and economic feasibility.
4. **Estimate** the cost of inaction versus the investment required for improvements.
5. **Establish** a baseline (M&V baseline) for future performance comparison.
6. **Ensure** statutory compliance with the **Energy Conservation Act, 2001** (mandatory for Designated Consumers under PAT — Perform, Achieve, Trade scheme).

## 1.4 Phases of an Energy Audit — The "Audit Hierarchy"

Every energy audit is broadly divided into three progressive phases, with increasing depth and cost:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Phases of Energy Audit:**
> 1. **Pre-Audit Phase** (Walk-through Audit / Preliminary Audit)
> 2. **Audit Phase** (Detailed Energy Audit / Comprehensive Audit)
> 3. **Post-Audit Phase** (Implementation, M&V, and Continuous Monitoring)

Each phase feeds into the next, balancing **cost of audit** against **depth of insight**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Anatomy of an Energy Audit — Step-by-Step Logic

A **certified energy audit** follows a structured engineering methodology. Here is the complete operational breakdown:

### Step 1: Pre-Audit Planning & Scoping
- **Why:** Avoids scope creep and focuses the audit on the highest energy-cost centers.
- **How:** Collect 12–24 months of **utility bills** (electricity, diesel, LPG, PNG, steam, compressed air), production data, and equipment list.
- **Output:** A **walk-through plan** and an **energy cost block diagram** identifying the 20% of equipment consuming 80% of energy (Pareto Principle / 80-20 rule).

### Step 2: On-Site Survey & Data Logging
- **Why:** Bills only give aggregate data; field measurements reveal where energy is actually lost.
- **How:** Use calibrated instruments — clamp meters, power analyzers, flue gas analyzers, lux meters, infrared thermometers, ultrasonic flow meters.
- **Output:** Hourly / shift-wise load profiles and **specific energy consumption (SEC)** values.

### Step 3: Data Analysis & Energy Balance
- **Why:** Converts raw data into engineering insight.
- **How:** Develop **Sankey diagrams** of energy flow (Input → Useful Output + Losses) and compute efficiency of each subsystem.
- **Output:** Quantified energy losses in kWh, ₹ (INR), and tonnes of oil equivalent (TOE).

### Step 4: Identification of Energy Conservation Measures (ECMs)
- **Why:** Recommendations must be engineered, not generic.
- **How:** For each loss, propose a techno-economically viable measure (technology upgrade, O&M improvement, behavioral change).
- **Output:** A list of ECMs tagged with estimated savings and investment.

### Step 5: Economic Analysis & Prioritization
- **Why:** Not every technically feasible measure is financially viable.
- **How:** Compute **Simple Payback Period (SPP)**, **Net Present Value (NPV)**, **Internal Rate of Return (IRR)**, and **CO₂ reduction potential**.
- **Output:** A shortlist of "bankable" ECMs ready for management approval.

### Step 6: Reporting & Implementation Support
- **Why:** An unimplemented report is wasted engineering effort.
- **How:** Submit a **Detailed Audit Report** (DAR) with executive summary, methodology, observations, recommendations, and an **Implementation Roadmap**.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Audit Calculations)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table contains every formula a KTU student must memorize for Module 2 numerical problems. **Memorize the units** — valuation scripts lose marks when units are missing.

**Table 2.1 — Essential Energy Audit Formulas**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Engineering Use |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|-----------------|
| 1 | Energy Consumption (Electrical) | $E = V \times I \times t \times \sqrt{3} \times PF$ for 3-phase; $E = V \times I \times t \times PF$ for 1-phase | kWh | Bill verification |
| 2 | Power (3-phase) | $P = \sqrt{3} \times V_L \times I_L \times \cos\phi$ | kW | Motor sizing audit |
| 3 | Specific Energy Consumption | $SEC = \dfrac{\text{Total Energy Consumed}}{\text{Production Output}}$ | kWh/tonne; MJ/kg | Benchmarking |
| 4 | Energy Cost Saving (Annual) | $\Delta C = \Delta E \times R$ | ₹/year | ECM financial impact |
| 5 | Simple Payback Period | $SPP = \dfrac{I}{\Delta C}$ | years | First-pass screening |
| 6 | Net Present Value | $NPV = -I + \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{\Delta C_t}{(1+r)^t}$ | ₹ | Long-term viability |
| 7 | Internal Rate of Return | $NPV = 0 = -I + \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{\Delta C_t}{(1+IRR)^t}$ | % | Compare with MARR |
| 8 | CO₂ Emissions Avoided | $\Delta CO_2 = \Delta E \times EF_{grid}$ | tonnes/year | Sustainability reporting |
| 9 | Boiler Efficiency (Direct) | $\eta_{boiler} = \dfrac{m_{steam}(h_{steam}-h_{feedwater})}{m_{fuel}\times GCV_{fuel}} \times 100$ | % | Thermal audit |
| 10 | Compressed Air Leakage Cost | $C_{leak} = P_{leak} \times T_{op} \times R \times 0.9$ | ₹/year | Pneumatic audit |
| 11 | Lighting Energy (per fixture) | $E_{fixture} = W_{lamp} \times N \times H_{use} \times 365$ | kWh/year | Lighting retrofit |
| 12 | Cooling Tower Make-up Water Energy | $E_{ct} = m_{makeup} \times C_p \times \Delta T \times 0.001$ | kWh | HVAC audit |

Where:
- $V$ = Voltage (V), $I$ = Current (A), $t$ = time (h)
- $PF$ = Power Factor (cos φ), typically **0.80 to 0.95**
- $R$ = Energy tariff (₹/kWh), $I$ = Investment (₹)
- $EF_{grid}$ = Indian grid emission factor = **0.716 kg CO₂/kWh** (CEA 2023 baseline)
- $MARR$ = Minimum Attractive Rate of Return, generally **12–15%** in India
- $GCV$ = Gross Calorific Value (MJ/kg), $h$ = specific enthalpy (kJ/kg)

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

These formulas are not academic exercises — they are the daily toolkit of:
- **BEE Certified Energy Auditors** in steel, cement, fertilizer, and textile plants
- **ESCo (Energy Service Company) professionals** structuring **shared-savings** and **guaranteed-savings** contracts
- **IGBC / LEED / GRIHA green-building consultants** designing net-zero commercial buildings
- **PAT scheme compliance officers** in Designated Consumers (DCs)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When asked "**list the types of energy audit**," always present them in a **table form** with columns: Type, Depth, Duration, Cost, Output, and Typical Client. This structured format guarantees full marks.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION_3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Simple Payback Period (SPP) from First Principles

**Engineering Problem Statement:** A factory installs a VFD (Variable Frequency Drive) on a 75 kW motor. The annual energy saving is estimated at 95,000 kWh. The cost of electricity is ₹7.50/kWh and the cost of the VFD with installation is ₹4,50,000. The motor operates 300 days/year, 16 h/day. Compute the simple payback period.

**Given Data (Step 1 — Extract):**
- Motor rating $P = 75$ kW
- Annual energy saved $\Delta E = 95{,}000$ kWh
- Tariff $R = 7.50$ ₹/kWh
- Investment $I = 4{,}50{,}000$ ₹

**Step 2 — Annual Monetary Saving:**

$$
\Delta C = \Delta E \times R
$$

$$
\Delta C = 95{,}000 \times 7.50
$$

$$
\Delta C = 7{,}12{,}500 \text{ ₹/year}
$$

**Step 3 — Simple Payback Period Formula Derivation:**

The simple payback period is defined as the time required for cumulative cash inflows from energy savings to equal the initial investment. By definition:

$$
\sum_{t=1}^{SPP} \Delta C = I
$$

For the case of **uniform annual cash flow** (constant savings every year), the sum simplifies to:

$$
\Delta C \times SPP = I
$$

Solving for SPP:

$$
SPP = \dfrac{I}{\Delta C}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical Substitution:**

$$
SPP = \dfrac{4{,}50{,}000}{7{,}12{,}500}
$$

$$
SPP = 0.6316 \text{ years}
$$

Converting to months:

$$
SPP_{months} = 0.6316 \times 12
$$

$$
SPP_{months} \approx 7.58 \text{ months}
$$

**Step 5 — Engineering Decision:**

Since $SPP \ll 3$ years (the typical **bankable threshold** for industrial ECMs in India), the VFD installation is **highly recommended for immediate implementation**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Annualised CO₂ Emission Reduction

**Problem Statement:** Compute the annual CO₂ reduction for the same VFD installation. Use the CEA grid emission factor $EF_{grid} = 0.716$ kg CO₂/kWh.

**Step 1 — Convert kWh Saved to kg CO₂ Avoided:**

$$
\Delta CO_2 = \Delta E \times EF_{grid}
$$

$$
\Delta CO_2 = 95{,}000 \times 0.716
$$

$$
\Delta CO_2 = 68{,}020 \text{ kg CO}_2/\text{year}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert to tonnes:**

$$
\Delta CO_2 = \dfrac{68{,}020}{1000} = 68.02 \text{ tonnes CO}_2/\text{year}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to "Equivalent Passenger Cars Off the Road" (US EPA convention):**

One average passenger car emits ~4.6 tonnes CO₂/year. So:

$$
N_{cars} = \dfrac{68.02}{4.6}
$$

$$
N_{cars} \approx 14.79 \approx 15 \text{ cars}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This "**equivalence statement**" is highly valued by KTU examiners in descriptive answers on **environmental impact of energy audits** — it converts engineering data into a **publicly relatable metric**.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Boiler Efficiency by Direct Method (Thermal Audit)

**Problem Statement:** A 4 TPH (tonnes per hour) packaged boiler operates on furnace oil with GCV = 42 MJ/kg. The steam is generated at 10 bar (g) with enthalpy $h_s = 2778$ kJ/kg, and the feedwater enters at 85 °C with enthalpy $h_f = 355$ kJ/kg. Fuel consumption is 320 kg/h. Calculate the boiler efficiency by the direct method.

**Step 1 — Enthalpy of Steam Generation (per kg):**

$$
\Delta h = h_s - h_f
$$

$$
\Delta h = 2778 - 355 = 2423 \text{ kJ/kg of steam}
$$

**Step 2 — Useful Heat Output per Hour:**

$$
Q_{out} = m_{steam} \times \Delta h
$$

$$
Q_{out} = 4000 \text{ kg/h} \times 2423 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

$$
Q_{out} = 9{,}69{,}20{,}000 \text{ kJ/h} = 9.692 \times 10^7 \text{ kJ/h}
$$

**Step 3 — Heat Input from Fuel per Hour:**

$$
Q_{in} = m_{fuel} \times GCV
$$

$$
Q_{in} = 320 \text{ kg/h} \times 42{,}000 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

$$
Q_{in} = 1{,}34{,}40{,}000 \text{ kJ/h} = 1.344 \times 10^7 \text{ kJ/h}
$$

**Step 4 — Boiler Efficiency (Direct Method):**

$$
\eta_{boiler} = \dfrac{Q_{out}}{Q_{in}} \times 100
$$

$$
\eta_{boiler} = \dfrac{9.692 \times 10^7}{1.344 \times 10^7} \times 100
$$

$$
\eta_{boiler} = 7.21 \times 100
$$

$$
\eta_{boiler} \approx 72.1\%
$$

**Step 5 — Engineering Interpretation:**

A modern oil-fired packaged boiler should achieve **85%–88%** efficiency. The audited value of **72.1%** indicates **significant scope for improvement** — likely due to soot on tubes, excess air, or poor combustion tuning. This becomes a **top-priority ECM** in the audit report.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Audit Types

**Table 3.1 — Three Types of Energy Audits Compared**

| Parameter | Walk-through (Preliminary) Audit | Detailed Energy Audit | Investment-Grade Audit |
|-----------|----------------------------------|-----------------------|------------------------|
| **Also Called** | Level 1 / Quick Audit | Level 2 / Comprehensive Audit | Level 3 / Fine-Tuning Audit |
| **Duration** | 1–3 days | 2–6 weeks | 4–12 weeks |
| **Cost (typical)** | ₹50k – ₹2 lakh | ₹2 – ₹15 lakh | ₹15 – ₹50+ lakh |
| **Data Required** | Utility bills, plant tour | 12–24 month bills + detailed measurements | Real-time M&V instruments for weeks/months |
| **Instruments Used** | Visual inspection, basic clamp meter | Power analyzer, flue gas analyzer, flow meters | Data loggers, ultrasonic flow, thermal imaging, EMS |
| **Output** | List of "low-hanging fruit" ECMs | Detailed Sankey diagram + ECMs with Class A/B/C confidence | Bankable DPR (Detailed Project Report) for ESco / lender |
| **Accuracy of Saving Estimate** | ±30–50% | ±10–20% | ±2–5% |
| **Typical Client** | SMEs, malls, hotels | Large industries, hospitals | EScos, Banks, Infrastructure PPP projects |
| **Conducted By** | Energy manager / consultant | Certified Energy Auditor (CEA) | Lead auditor + multi-disciplinary team |
| **KTU Marks Weightage** | 3-mark definition question | 14-mark descriptive question | Often asked as "investment grade" 7-mark sub-part |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Types of Energy Audit (Classification Tree)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Energy Audit] --> B[Walk-Through Audit]
    A --> C[Detailed Energy Audit]
    A --> D[Investment Grade Audit]
    B --> B1[Level 1: Preliminary]
    B --> B2[Duration: 1 to 3 Days]
    B --> B3[Output: Quick Wins List]
    C --> C1[Level 2: Comprehensive]
    C --> C2[Duration: 2 to 6 Weeks]
    C --> C3[Output: Sankey Diagram and ECMs]
    D --> D1[Level 3: Bankable Study]
    D --> D2[Duration: 4 to 12 Weeks]
    D --> D3[Output: DPR for ESco Finance]
    A --> E[Scope Basis]
    E --> E1[Internal Audit by In-house Team]
    E --> E2[External Audit by Third Party]
    E --> E3[Statutory Audit under EC Act 2001]
```

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Detailed Energy Audit Methodology (Block-Level Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Phase 1: Pre-Audit Planning] --> P2[Phase 2: On-Site Survey]
    P2 --> P3[Phase 3: Data Logging and Monitoring]
    P3 --> P4[Phase 4: Energy Balance and Sankey Diagram]
    P4 --> P5[Phase 5: ECM Identification]
    P5 --> P6[Phase 6: Economic Analysis]
    P6 --> P7[Phase 7: Detailed Audit Report]
    P7 --> P8[Phase 8: Implementation Support]
    P8 --> P9[Phase 9: Post-Implementation M and V]
    P9 --> P1
    P2 --> P2A[Walk-Through Inspection]
    P2 --> P2B[Interviews with Operators]
    P2 --> P2C[Review of Past Bills]
    P3 --> P3A[Power Analyzers]
    P3 --> P3B[Flue Gas Analyzers]
    P3 --> P3C[Fluid Flow Meters]
    P3 --> P3D[Lux Meters and IR Thermometers]
    P5 --> P5A[Low Cost No Cost ECMs]
    P5 --> P5B[Medium Investment ECMs]
    P5 --> P5C[High Investment ECMs]
    P6 --> P6A[Simple Payback Period]
    P6 --> P6B[Net Present Value]
    P6 --> P6C[Internal Rate of Return]
    P6 --> P6D[CO2 Reduction Potential]
```

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Energy Audit as Part of the PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act) Cycle

```mermaid
graph LR
    Q1[Plan: Define Energy Policy and Baseline] --> Q2[Do: Implement ECMs]
    Q2 --> Q3[Check: Conduct Energy Audit]
    Q3 --> Q4[Act: Review and Improve]
    Q4 --> Q1
    Q3 --> R1[Identifies Gaps]
    R1 --> Q2
    Q4 --> R2[Continual Improvement]
    R2 --> Q1
```

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Cross-Sectional Energy Flow in a Typical Audited Plant

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IN1[Grid Electricity: 1000 kWh] --> IN2[Distribution Transformer]
    IN3[Diesel Generator: 200 kWh] --> IN2
    IN2 --> M1[Lighting 15 percent]
    IN2 --> M2[HVAC 25 percent]
    IN2 --> M3[Compressed Air 20 percent]
    IN2 --> M4[Motors and Drives 30 percent]
    IN2 --> M5[Others 10 percent]
    M3 --> L1[Leakage Losses 8 percent]
    M4 --> L2[Mis-sized Motor Losses 7 percent]
    M2 --> L3[Chiller Inefficiency 6 percent]
    L1 --> RECO[Identified ECMs for Savings]
    L2 --> RECO
    L3 --> RECO
    RECO --> SAV[Annual Savings 18 percent of Total Input]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation (for KTU answers):** The block diagram above shows how an **energy audit deconstructs a unified electricity bill** into meaningful **end-use categories**. This is the **single most important visual** a KTU student should reproduce in the 14-mark "types of energy audit" answer.
