# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define Energy Audit. List any four objectives of conducting an energy audit in an industrial facility.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1 Mark):** Energy Audit is the systematic verification, monitoring, and analysis of energy use in a system/process to identify conservation opportunities and recommend improvements, **as defined by the Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE) under the Energy Conservation Act, 2001**.

**Objectives (Any 4 × 0.5 Mark = 2 Marks):**
1. To **quantify** energy consumption by end-use and identify the major loss areas.
2. To **benchmark** plant performance against industry best practices (BEE Star Rating / ISO 50001).
3. To **recommend** cost-effective Energy Conservation Measures (ECMs) with payback < 3 years.
4. To **establish** a baseline (M&V baseline) for ongoing performance tracking.
5. To **ensure compliance** with statutory obligations under the **PAT scheme** for Designated Consumers.
6. To **reduce** the carbon footprint and improve **ESG (Environmental, Social, Governance)** ratings.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between a Walk-through Audit and a Detailed Energy Audit.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Tabular Format Preferred):**

| Parameter | Walk-through (Preliminary) Audit | Detailed (Comprehensive) Audit |
|-----------|----------------------------------|--------------------------------|
| **Duration** | 1–3 days | 2–6 weeks |
| **Instruments** | Visual inspection + basic clamp meter | Power analyzer, flue gas analyzer, flow meters |
| **Accuracy of savings estimate** | ±30–50% | ±10–20% |
| **Output** | List of "quick-win" ECMs (Class C) | Sankey diagram + Class A/B/C ECMs with engineering calculations |
| **Cost** | Low (₹50k–₹2 lakh) | Moderate–High (₹2–₹15 lakh) |
| **Conducted by** | Energy manager / consultant | Certified Energy Auditor (BEE empanelled) |

A walk-through audit is a **screening exercise**, while a detailed audit is a **comprehensive engineering study**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Evaluation) regulations, every 14-mark question has an **internal choice**. Both options are presented below.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**(a)** Explain in detail the **three types of energy audits** with their scope, duration, methodology, and typical deliverables. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 100 kW motor operates 16 hours/day, 300 days/year. The existing motor has an efficiency of 88%. A proposal is made to replace it with a **premium-efficiency (IE3) motor** of efficiency 93%, costing ₹3,50,000 (including installation). The electricity tariff is ₹8.00/kWh. Calculate the **simple payback period** and **annual CO₂ reduction**. Take grid emission factor = 0.716 kg CO₂/kWh. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part (a) — 7 Marks

The three types of energy audits are:

**1. Walk-through (Preliminary) Energy Audit — Level 1 (2 Marks)**
- **Scope:** Plant-wide visual inspection, identification of obvious energy waste, review of past 12 months' utility bills.
- **Duration:** 1–3 days.
- **Methodology:** Interviews with operators, review of operating logs, basic spot measurements.
- **Deliverables:** A short report listing "low-hanging fruit" / **low-cost, no-cost** ECMs.

**2. Detailed Energy Audit — Level 2 (3 Marks)**
- **Scope:** Subsystem-wise measurement of all energy streams (electrical, thermal, fuel) using calibrated instruments. Energy balance and Sankey diagram preparation.
- **Duration:** 2–6 weeks.
- **Methodology:** Long-duration power logging (typically 7 days), combustion analysis, steam trap survey, compressed air leak survey, insulation thickness measurement, lighting lux audit, HVAC chiller performance analysis.
- **Deliverables:** Detailed Audit Report (DAR) with quantified ECMs classified as **Class A (high confidence)**, **Class B (medium)**, and **Class C (low)**, along with economic analysis.

**3. Investment-Grade Audit — Level 3 (2 Marks)**
- **Scope:** Used by ESCOs and banks to finance large retrofit projects. Involves **IPMVP (International Performance Measurement and Verification Protocol)** compliant M&V.
- **Duration:** 4–12 weeks.
- **Methodology:** Real-time data loggers, statistical regression models, multi-season measurements to account for climate variability.
- **Deliverables:** A **bankable Detailed Project Report (DPR)** with savings guaranteed to ±5% accuracy.

#### Model Solution to Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Energy Loss in Existing Motor (Input Power):**

The motor delivers 100 kW (output) at 88% efficiency. Therefore, input power drawn from the grid is:

$$
P_{in,old} = \dfrac{P_{out}}{\eta_{old}} = \dfrac{100}{0.88} = 113.64 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 2 — Input Power with New IE3 Motor:**

$$
P_{in,new} = \dfrac{P_{out}}{\eta_{new}} = \dfrac{100}{0.93} = 107.53 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 3 — Power Saving:**

$$
\Delta P = P_{in,old} - P_{in,new}
$$

$$
\Delta P = 113.64 - 107.53 = 6.11 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 4 — Annual Energy Saving:**

Annual operating hours $T = 16 \times 300 = 4800$ h

$$
\Delta E = \Delta P \times T
$$

$$
\Delta E = 6.11 \times 4800
$$

$$
\Delta E = 29{,}328 \text{ kWh/year}
$$

**Step 5 — Annual Monetary Saving:**

$$
\Delta C = \Delta E \times R
$$

$$
\Delta C = 29{,}328 \times 8.00
$$

$$
\Delta C = 2{,}34{,}624 \text{ ₹/year}
$$

**Step 6 — Simple Payback Period:**

$$
SPP = \dfrac{I}{\Delta C} = \dfrac{3{,}50{,}000}{2{,}34{,}624}
$$

$$
SPP = 1.49 \text{ years} \approx 1 \text{ year and 6 months}
$$

**Step 7 — Annual CO₂ Reduction:**

$$
\Delta CO_2 = \Delta E \times EF_{grid}
$$

$$
\Delta CO_2 = 29{,}328 \times 0.716
$$

$$
\Delta CO_2 = 20{,}999 \text{ kg CO}_2/\text{year} \approx 21.0 \text{ tonnes CO}_2/\text{year}
$$

**Engineering Conclusion:** With SPP well within the **3-year bankable threshold** and significant CO₂ reduction, the **IE3 motor replacement is strongly recommended** — it is both financially and environmentally attractive.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 — Alternative Choice]

**(a)** Describe the **step-by-step methodology** of conducting a detailed energy audit in a thermal power plant or a process industry. Explain the role of **Sankey diagrams** and **energy performance indicators (EnPIs)**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A compressed air system delivers 80 m³/min at 7 bar (g) pressure. Leakage tests indicate 18% air loss through leaks. The compressor consumes 80 kW. Electricity tariff is ₹9.00/kWh, and the compressor operates 5000 h/year. Calculate: **(i)** Annual energy wasted in leaks (kWh), **(ii)** Annual monetary loss (₹), and **(iii)** Annual CO₂ emissions due to leaks. Take EF = 0.716 kg CO₂/kWh. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Detailed Energy Audit Methodology (7 Steps) (5 Marks):**

1. **Pre-Audit Planning & Data Collection:** Gather 12–24 months of utility bills, production data, equipment inventory, and operating schedules. Identify the **energy-intensive cost centers** (typically motors, boilers, chillers, compressed air).

2. **On-Site Inspection and Walk-through:** Visual inspection of housekeeping, insulation, leaks, lighting levels, and HVAC set points. Identify behavioural and operational inefficiencies.

3. **Instrumented Survey and Data Logging:** Install power analyzers on motors, flue gas analyzers on boilers, flow meters on cooling water and steam lines, lux meters for lighting. Typical logging duration: 7–14 days covering all operating regimes.

4. **Energy Balance and Sankey Diagram:** Construct a **Sankey diagram** where the width of each arrow is proportional to the magnitude of energy flow. **Input energy on the left, useful output and losses on the right.** This visually identifies the largest loss streams.

5. **Identification of Energy Conservation Measures (ECMs):** For each quantified loss, engineer a technical solution — variable frequency drives, waste heat recovery, VAV (Variable Air Volume) systems, LED retrofits, capacitor banks for PF improvement.

6. **Economic Analysis:** Compute **Simple Payback Period (SPP), Net Present Value (NPV), Internal Rate of Return (IRR)** and the **CO₂ reduction potential** of each ECM.

7. **Reporting, Implementation & M&V:** Submit the **Detailed Audit Report (DAR)** with executive summary, methodology, observations, recommendations, and an **Implementation Roadmap**. Post-implementation, conduct **M&V (Measurement and Verification)** per **IPMVP** protocols.

**Role of Sankey Diagrams (1 Mark):** Sankey diagrams are **flow diagrams** in which the width of the arrows is proportional to the quantity of energy/material flowing. They are essential for **visualizing energy losses** in an intuitive manner — auditors and plant managers can instantly see **where the energy is being wasted**.

**Role of Energy Performance Indicators (EnPIs) (1 Mark):** EnPIs are **normalized metrics** — kWh/m²/year (buildings), kWh/tonne (cement), GJ/tonne (steel), MJ/kg (textile). They enable **comparison across plants, shifts, and time periods**, and are the cornerstone of **ISO 50001 Energy Management Systems**.

#### Model Solution to Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Given Data:**
- Leakage fraction $f = 18\% = 0.18$
- Compressor input power $P = 80$ kW
- Operating hours $T = 5000$ h/year
- Tariff $R = 9.00$ ₹/kWh
- Emission factor $EF = 0.716$ kg CO₂/kWh

**(i) Annual Energy Wasted in Leaks:**

The compressor power is **proportional to mass flow**, so 18% of input energy corresponds directly to 18% of input power.

$$
P_{leak} = P \times f
$$

$$
P_{leak} = 80 \times 0.18 = 14.4 \text{ kW}
$$

$$
E_{leak} = P_{leak} \times T
$$

$$
E_{leak} = 14.4 \times 5000
$$

$$
E_{leak} = 72{,}000 \text{ kWh/year}
$$

**(ii) Annual Monetary Loss:**

$$
C_{leak} = E_{leak} \times R
$$

$$
C_{leak} = 72{,}000 \times 9.00
$$

$$
C_{leak} = 6{,}48{,}000 \text{ ₹/year}
$$

**(iii) Annual CO₂ Emissions Due to Leaks:**

$$
CO_{2,leak} = E_{leak} \times EF
$$

$$
CO_{2,leak} = 72{,}000 \times 0.716
$$

$$
CO_{2,leak} = 51{,}552 \text{ kg CO}_2/\text{year} \approx 51.55 \text{ tonnes CO}_2/\text{year}
$$

**Engineering Conclusion:** A typical **leak survey + repair program** costs around ₹1–1.5 lakh for an industry of this size, giving a payback of under 3 months. The simple act of **fixing compressed air leaks** is often the **single highest-ROI** ECM in a typical industrial audit.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Areas in "Energy Audit and Its Types" Answers:**
> 1. **Forgetting units** — Always write kWh, ₹/kWh, tonnes/year, %, etc. A correct number without a unit is **half-credit at best**.
> 2. **Confusing "Audit" with "Management"** — Energy **Audit** is a periodic **diagnostic exercise**. Energy **Management** is the **continuous operational system** (e.g., ISO 50001). Examiners strictly distinguish these.
> 3. **Writing only the names of the three audit types** without explaining methodology, instruments, deliverables, and client type. This fetches only **2–3 marks out of 7** in a sub-part. Use a **comparison table**.
> 4. **Using outdated emission factors** — The CEA emission factor is **0.716 kg CO₂/kWh** (revised 2023). Older texts quote 0.82 or 0.85 — **avoid them**.
> 5. **Skipping the M&V phase** — A complete audit cycle ends with **Measurement & Verification**, not just the report. Mention IPMVP protocols.
> 6. **No mention of the Energy Conservation Act, 2001** — For an Indian university question, citing this Act (and PAT scheme for Designated Consumers) adds **bonus impression points** with the examiner.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Energy Audit and Its Types — Top 12 Things to Remember for KTU 2024 Exam:**

- **Definition:** Systematic verification, monitoring, and analysis of energy use to identify savings without affecting output/quality (BEE definition).
- **Governing Act in India:** **Energy Conservation Act, 2001**; mandatory for **Designated Consumers** under **PAT scheme**.
- **Three Types:** (1) **Walk-through / Preliminary** (1–3 days, low cost), (2) **Detailed / Comprehensive** (2–6 weeks, calibrated instruments), (3) **Investment-Grade** (4–12 weeks, bankable DPR for ESco financing).
- **Phases of Audit:** Pre-Audit Planning → On-Site Survey → Data Logging → Energy Balance + Sankey → ECM Identification → Economic Analysis → Reporting → M&V.
- **Key Formula #1 — Annual Energy:** $E = \sqrt{3} \times V \times I \times t \times PF$ (3-phase), units kWh.
- **Key Formula #2 — Simple Payback:** $SPP = I / \Delta C$, where $\Delta C = \Delta E \times R$.
- **Key Formula #3 — CO₂ Reduction:** $\Delta CO_2 = \Delta E \times EF_{grid}$, with $EF = 0.716$ kg/kWh (CEA 2023).
- **Key Formula #4 — Boiler Efficiency (Direct):** $\eta = (m_s \times \Delta h) / (m_f \times GCV) \times 100\%$.
- **80-20 Rule (Pareto):** ~20% of equipment consumes ~80% of energy — focus the audit on the heavy hitters first.
- **Sankey Diagram:** Width of arrow ∝ energy flow; visualizes losses and useful output.
- **EnPIs (Energy Performance Indicators):** Normalized metrics — kWh/tonne, MJ/kg, kWh/m²/year.
- **Cycle:** Energy audit is not a one-time event — it follows the **PDCA cycle** of **ISO 50001** and must include **post-implementation M&V** as per **IPMVP** protocols.

> **End of KTU 2024 Module 2 — Energy Audit and Its Types — Study Notes**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Monitoring and Targeting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET745 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND AUDIT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Monitoring and Targeting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Monitoring and Targeting (M&T)** is a structured energy management technique used to **systematically measure, record, analyse, and control energy consumption** within an organization by establishing performance baselines, setting quantified improvement targets, and continuously tracking actual consumption against these targets. It functions as the operational "feedback loop" of the entire energy management system (EnMS) and is recognized under the ISO 50001 standard as a core element of the **"Check"** and **"Act"** stages of the Plan-Do-Check-Act (PDCA) cycle.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, M&T is defined as the post-audit management tool that **converts static energy audit recommendations into dynamic, measurable, and continuous performance control**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** *Monitoring and Targeting (M&T) is treated as a strategic management control system, not merely a measurement activity. It integrates metering infrastructure, data normalisation, statistical analysis, and managerial reporting.*

### 1.2 Core Terminology (KTU Standard Vocabulary)

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Baseline** | A reference value of energy consumption established from historical data under normalized operating conditions. |
| **Target** | A quantified, time-bound improvement goal set against the baseline (e.g., 5% reduction in 12 months). |
| **Energy Cost Centre (ECC)** | A logical or physical sub-unit of an organization for which energy consumption is measured and accountability is assigned. |
| **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)** | Energy used per unit of production/output — the principal Key Performance Indicator (KPI) in M&T. |
| **Normalisation** | Adjusting raw consumption data for variables such as production volume, ambient temperature, and operating hours. |
| **CUSUM** | Cumulative Sum of deviations of actual values from target — a powerful statistical trend-detection tool. |
| **Variance** | The numerical gap between actual consumption and target consumption in a given period. |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Personal Fitness Tracker" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Explanation for First-Time Learners**

Imagine you want to lose weight. You do **three things**: (1) record your current weight and daily calorie intake — this is your **Baseline**, (2) set a target such as "burn 500 kcal/day" — this is your **Target**, and (3) every day you check whether you met the target, and if not, you adjust your routine — this is **Monitoring and Targeting**.

Energy M&T does exactly the same thing for a factory:
- The **Baseline** = the plant's historical specific energy consumption.
- The **Target** = the improved SEC the plant must achieve.
- The **Monitoring** = continuous meter reading and data logging.
- The **Targeting** = statistical comparison and variance reporting.
- The **Action** = triggering investigations when variance exceeds acceptable limits.

Just as a fitness tracker alerts you the moment you deviate from your health goal, M&T alerts the energy manager the moment a cost centre deviates from its energy target.

### 1.4 Purpose of M&T in an Industrial Context

- **Detect abnormal consumption** before it escalates into a financial or operational loss.
- **Quantify savings** achieved by energy conservation measures (ECMs).
- **Provide accountability** by linking energy use to specific departments or processes.
- **Support ISO 50001 / EnMS** certification and re-certification audits.
- **Enable evidence-based decision making** for capital investment in new ECMs.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Pillars of an Effective M&T System

1. **Metering Infrastructure** — Adequate sub-metering at every Energy Cost Centre. The accuracy of M&T is fundamentally limited by the accuracy of the meters (typically **Class 1.0 or better**).
2. **Data Acquisition & Logging** — Manual logbooks, automatic data loggers, or IoT-enabled smart meters with timestamped readings at the desired granularity (shift-wise, daily, monthly).
3. **Data Normalisation** — Adjusting raw kWh readings for production volume, degree days, humidity, and other exogenous variables to enable a like-for-like comparison.
4. **Reporting & Action Protocol** — Predefined thresholds of variance that automatically trigger investigation, escalation, and corrective action.

### 2.2 The M&T Cycle (PDCA Integration)

The M&T workflow is a continuous improvement loop:

- **Plan** → Identify Energy Cost Centres, install meters, establish baseline, and set targets.
- **Do** → Record actual energy consumption at the defined frequency.
- **Check** → Calculate SEC, plot CUSUM charts, analyse variance.
- **Act** → Investigate significant variances, implement corrective actions, and re-baseline if operating conditions change.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Note for Table Rendering:** All absolute value bars use `\vert` instead of `|` to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Boundary Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)** | $SEC = \dfrac{E_{actual}}{P_{output}}$ | kWh / tonne, kWh / unit, MJ / kg |
| 2 | **Energy Intensity** | $EI = \dfrac{Total\ Energy}{Activity\ Indicator}$ | Used for cross-plant benchmarking |
| 3 | **Cumulative Sum (CUSUM)** | $S_t = S_{t-1} + (X_t - T)$ | Trend detector, dimensionless |
| 4 | **CUSUM in Closed Form** | $S_t = \sum_{i=1}^{t} (X_i - T_i)$ | Where $T_i$ is the period-$i$ target |
| 5 | **Arithmetic Mean (Baseline)** | $\bar{X} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i$ | Best baseline when data is stationary |
| 6 | **Standard Deviation** | $\sigma = \sqrt{\dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(X_i - \bar{X})^2}{n-1}}$ | Used to set **3-sigma** control limits |
| 7 | **Variance** | $\sigma^2 = \dfrac{\sum(X_i - \bar{X})^2}{n-1}$ | Square of standard deviation |
| 8 | **Percentage Variance (Deviation)** | $\%\Delta = \dfrac{X_{actual} - T}{T}\times 100$ | Positive → over-consumption |
| 9 | **Normalised Consumption** | $E_{norm} = E_{actual} \times \dfrac{P_{ref}}{P_{actual}}$ | Removes production-volume effect |
| 10 | **Target Setting (Linear Improvement)** | $T_n = B \times \left(1 - \dfrac{r}{100}\right)^n$ | $B$ = baseline, $r$ = annual reduction %, $n$ = year |
| 11 | **Control Limit (Upper)** | $UCL = T + 3\sigma$ | Action threshold for over-consumption |
| 12 | **Control Limit (Lower)** | $LCL = T - 3\sigma$ | Action threshold for under-consumption |
| 13 | **Coefficient of Variation** | $C_v = \dfrac{\sigma}{\bar{X}}\times 100\%$ | Indicates data dispersion / stability |
| 14 | **Annual Energy Cost Saving** | $\Delta C = \Delta E \times P_e$ | $\Delta E$ = kWh saved/year, $P_e$ = ₹/kWh |

### 2.4 Statistical Foundation — Why CUSUM Works

A single monthly reading showing consumption above the target could be **random noise**. A CUSUM chart amplifies persistent small deviations by accumulating them, making **systematic trends visible** that would otherwise hide inside random variation. This makes CUSUM a far more sensitive trend detector than bar charts of monthly consumption.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** In the BEE (Bureau of Energy Efficiency) and ISO 50001 frameworks, CUSUM is the **de-facto visual tool** for proving whether an energy conservation project has delivered sustained savings or only produced a temporary, random dip.

### 2.5 Selecting the Right Baseline

| Method | When to Use |
|---|---|
| **Simple historical average** | When the process is stable with low variability |
| **Regression-based baseline** | When energy use is strongly correlated with one variable (e.g., production volume, degree days) |
| **Engineering model baseline** | When the plant has a calibrated energy model from the audit |
| **Pre-implementation baseline** | When a specific ECM is to be evaluated (M&V per IPMVP) |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example: CUSUM Computation for a Manufacturing Plant

**Problem Statement:** The injection moulding section of a factory has a **Target** consumption of **50,000 kWh per month** (established as the historical baseline). The actual monthly electricity consumption for six consecutive months is given below. Construct the CUSUM chart, compute the cumulative sums, and interpret the trend.

| Month | Actual Consumption $X_t$ (kWh) | Target $T$ (kWh) |
|---|---|---|
| January  | 52,000 | 50,000 |
| February | 51,000 | 50,000 |
| March    | 48,000 | 50,000 |
| April    | 47,000 | 50,000 |
| May      | 46,000 | 50,000 |
| June     | 45,000 | 50,000 |

**Step 1 — Compute the per-period deviation:**

Using the fundamental CUSUM increment $\Delta_t = X_t - T$:

$$\Delta_{Jan} = 52{,}000 - 50{,}000 = +2{,}000\ \text{kWh}$$

$$\Delta_{Feb} = 51{,}000 - 50{,}000 = +1{,}000\ \text{kWh}$$

$$\Delta_{Mar} = 48{,}000 - 50{,}000 = -2{,}000\ \text{kWh}$$

$$\Delta_{Apr} = 47{,}000 - 50{,}000 = -3{,}000\ \text{kWh}$$

$$\Delta_{May} = 46{,}000 - 50{,}000 = -4{,}000\ \text{kWh}$$

$$\Delta_{Jun} = 45{,}000 - 50{,}000 = -5{,}000\ \text{kWh}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the CUSUM $S_t$ using the recursion $S_t = S_{t-1} + \Delta_t$, with $S_0 = 0$:**

$$S_{Jan} = 0 + 2{,}000 = +2{,}000\ \text{kWh}$$

$$S_{Feb} = 2{,}000 + 1{,}000 = +3{,}000\ \text{kWh}$$

$$S_{Mar} = 3{,}000 - 2{,}000 = +1{,}000\ \text{kWh}$$

$$S_{Apr} = 1{,}000 - 3{,}000 = -2{,}000\ \text{kWh}$$

$$S_{May} = -2{,}000 - 4{,}000 = -6{,}000\ \text{kWh}$$

$$S_{Jun} = -6{,}000 - 5{,}000 = -11{,}000\ \text{kWh}$$

**Step 3 — Tabulated CUSUM Summary:**

| Month | $X_t$ | $\Delta_t = X_t - T$ | $S_t$ (CUSUM) | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Jan | 52,000 | +2,000 | +2,000 | Above target — investigate |
| Feb | 51,000 | +1,000 | +3,000 | Sustained over-consumption |
| Mar | 48,000 | -2,000 | +1,000 | Slight correction |
| Apr | 47,000 | -3,000 | -2,000 | Crosses below zero — savings emerging |
| May | 46,000 | -4,000 | -6,000 | Strong downward trend |
| Jun | 45,000 | -5,000 | -11,000 | Sustained savings achieved |

**Step 4 — Interpretation of the CUSUM Curve:**

- The CUSUM **peaked at +3,000 kWh in February**, indicating that for the first two months the plant was over-consuming.
- From **March onwards, the CUSUM declines monotonically and steeply**, crossing zero between March and April.
- A **monotonically decreasing CUSUM** is a classical signature of a **persistent downward shift in mean consumption** — the plant is genuinely saving energy, not experiencing random fluctuation.
- The cumulative savings by June is **11,000 kWh**, equivalent to $11{,}000 \times P_e$ rupees in cost reduction (where $P_e$ is the unit electricity tariff).

### 3.2 Derivative Calculation: Standard Deviation and 3-Sigma Control Limits

Continuing with the same data, suppose we now wish to establish **control limits** around the target for ongoing M&T:

**Step 1 — Compute the arithmetic mean:**

$$\bar{X} = \frac{52{,}000 + 51{,}000 + 48{,}000 + 47{,}000 + 46{,}000 + 45{,}000}{6}$$

$$\bar{X} = \frac{289{,}000}{6} = 48{,}166.67\ \text{kWh}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the squared deviations $\left(X_i - \bar{X}\right)^2$:**

$$(52{,}000 - 48{,}166.67)^2 = (3{,}833.33)^2 = 14{,}694{,}444$$

$$(51{,}000 - 48{,}166.67)^2 = (2{,}833.33)^2 = 8{,}027{,}778$$

$$(48{,}000 - 48{,}166.67)^2 = (-166.67)^2 = 27{,}778$$

$$(47{,}000 - 48{,}166.67)^2 = (-1{,}166.67)^2 = 1{,}361{,}111$$

$$(46{,}000 - 48{,}166.67)^2 = (-2{,}166.67)^2 = 4{,}694{,}444$$

$$(45{,}000 - 48{,}166.67)^2 = (-3{,}166.67)^2 = 10{,}027{,}778$$

**Step 3 — Sum the squared deviations:**

$$\sum (X_i - \bar{X})^2 = 14{,}694{,}444 + 8{,}027{,}778 + 27{,}778 + 1{,}361{,}111 + 4{,}694{,}444 + 10{,}027{,}778$$

$$\sum (X_i - \bar{X})^2 = 38{,}833{,}333$$

**Step 4 — Compute the sample standard deviation (n − 1 = 5 degrees of freedom):**

$$\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{38{,}833{,}333}{5}} = \sqrt{7{,}766{,}667} \approx 2{,}786.87\ \text{kWh}$$

**Step 5 — Establish 3-sigma control limits about the target $T = 50{,}000$ kWh:**

$$UCL = T + 3\sigma = 50{,}000 + 3 \times 2{,}786.87 = 50{,}000 + 8{,}360.61 = 58{,}360.61\ \text{kWh}$$

$$LCL = T - 3\sigma = 50{,}000 - 8{,}360.61 = 41{,}639.39\ \text{kWh}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** Any monthly reading lying **outside the band [41,639 ; 58,361] kWh** is statistically anomalous (probability < 0.3% under normal operation) and must trigger a root-cause investigation. The values for January–June all lie inside the band, confirming that the apparent "savings" emerged gradually, not as a single-step break.

### 3.3 Normalisation of Consumption for Production Volume

**Problem:** A boiler consumed 80,000 kWh of thermal energy in March, producing 1,000 tonnes of steam. In April, the same boiler produced only 800 tonnes of steam but consumed 72,000 kWh. Compare the two months fairly.

**Step 1 — Compute the SEC for each month:**

$$SEC_{Mar} = \frac{80{,}000}{1{,}000} = 80\ \text{kWh/tonne}$$

$$SEC_{Apr} = \frac{72{,}000}{800} = 90\ \text{kWh/tonne}$$

**Step 2 — Interpretation:** Although absolute energy dropped from 80,000 to 72,000 kWh, the **SEC increased by 12.5%** because production fell faster than consumption. Without normalisation, the manager would falsely conclude that energy performance improved in April.

**Step 3 — Normalise April to March's production reference:**

$$E_{norm,\ Apr} = 72{,}000 \times \frac{1{,}000}{800} = 72{,}000 \times 1.25 = 90{,}000\ \text{kWh}$$

This normalised value can now be **directly compared to March's 80,000 kWh** on a like-for-like basis, revealing a 12.5% efficiency loss.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of an M&T System (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TB
    blockA[ENERGY SOURCES Grid DG Solar] --> blockB[METERING LAYER Sub Meters Class 1]
    blockB --> blockC[DATA ACQUISITION Manual Logger SCADA IoT]
    blockC --> blockD[NORMALISATION Production Volume Degree Days]
    blockD --> blockE[BASELINE STORE Historical Average Regression Model]
    blockD --> blockF[TARGET STORE Annual Reduction Goal]
    blockE --> blockG[VARIANCE ANALYSIS CUSUM Computation]
    blockF --> blockG
    blockG --> blockH{Check Variance Against Control Limits}
    blockH -->|Within Limits| blockI[GREEN STATUS Acceptable Performance]
    blockH -->|Outside Limits| blockJ[RED STATUS Trigger Investigation]
    blockJ --> blockK[ROOT CAUSE ANALYSIS]
    blockK --> blockL[CORRECTIVE ACTION]
    blockL --> blockM[REPORTING DASHBOARD]
    blockI --> blockM
    blockM --> blockN[MANAGEMENT REVIEW ISO 50001]
    blockN --> blockL
```

### 4.2 The M&T Cycle Integrated with PDCA (Process Flow)

```mermaid
graph LR
    step1[STEP 1 IDENTIFY COST CENTRES] --> step2[STEP 2 INSTALL SUB METERS]
    step2 --> step3[STEP 3 ESTABLISH BASELINE]
    step3 --> step4[STEP 4 SET QUANTIFIED TARGETS]
    step4 --> step5[STEP 5 MONITOR CONTINUOUSLY]
    step5 --> step6[STEP 6 COMPUTE SEC AND CUSUM]
    step6 --> step7{VARIANCE WITHIN LIMITS}
    step7 -->|YES| step8[GREEN LOG TO DASHBOARD]
    step7 -->|NO| step9[RED ALERT TRIGGER]
    step9 --> step10[INVESTIGATE ROOT CAUSE]
    step10 --> step11[IMPLEMENT CORRECTIVE ACTION]
    step11 --> step12[VERIFY SAVINGS POST ACTION]
    step12 --> step5
    step8 --> step5
    step3 -.RE BASELINE.-> step3
```

### 4.3 CUSUM Chart — Visual Trend Diagnostic

```mermaid
graph TB
    chartA[CUSUM VALUE AXIS VERTICAL kWh] --> chartB[TIME AXIS HORIZONTAL Months]
    chartB --> chartC[UCL CONTROL LINE Upper]
    chartB --> chartD[TARGET LINE Zero Reference]
    chartB --> chartE[LCL CONTROL LINE Lower]
    chartF[Data Series 1 ACTUAL CUSUM CURVE] --> chartB
    chartF --> chartG[TREND Rising Slope indicates Over Consumption]
    chartF --> chartH[TREND Falling Slope indicates Sustained Savings]
```

### 4.4 Data Normalisation Logic Block

```mermaid
graph TD
    rawA[RAW ENERGY READING kWh] --> mulA[Multiply by Reference Output]
    rawB[RAW PRODUCTION VOLUME] --> mulA
    mulA --> outA[SEC VALUE kWh per Unit]
    rawA --> refA[Reference Production]
    refA --> normA[Normalisation Operator]
    rawB --> normA
    normA --> outB[NORMALISED ENERGY kWh at Reference Output]
    outA --> comp[COMPARISON ENGINE]
    outB --> comp
    comp --> report[REPORT Like for Like Consumption]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** Block 4.1 represents the complete M&T architecture from physical metering to management decision-making. Block 4.2 maps the M&T workflow onto the ISO 50001 Plan-Do-Check-Act (PDCA) loop. Block 4.3 illustrates the visual signature of a CUSUM chart and how a falling slope becomes evidence of sustained savings. Block 4.4 shows how raw kWh data is converted to SEC and normalised kWh for a fair month-on-month comparison.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question Paper Pattern 2024 Scheme]**

**Q1. Define Monitoring and Targeting (M&T) in the context of energy management. List any three of its benefits.**

**Model Answer:**
Monitoring and Targeting (M&T) is a systematic management technique that involves continuous measurement, recording, and analysis of energy consumption against pre-established baselines and improvement targets, with the objective of identifying variances and triggering corrective action.

**Three key benefits:**
1. Enables early detection of abnormal energy use, preventing financial losses.
2. Provides quantified proof of savings achieved by energy conservation measures (ECMs).
3. Establishes accountability by linking energy use to specific departments, processes, or operators.

*(2 marks for definition + 1 mark for benefits — Total 3 marks)*

---

**Q2. What is a CUSUM chart? Why is it preferred over a simple bar chart of monthly consumption for energy monitoring?**

**Model Answer:**
A CUSUM (Cumulative Sum) chart plots the running cumulative total of deviations of actual energy consumption from the target value over successive time periods.

**Why CUSUM is preferred:**
- A bar chart shows only individual monthly values that may be obscured by random noise.
- A CUSUM chart **accumulates** deviations, amplifying small systematic trends and making them visually obvious.
- It enables detection of persistent shifts in mean consumption that would be invisible in a monthly bar plot.

*(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for advantages — Total 3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU ESE — Typical Module 3 Long-Answer Pattern]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the objectives, elements, and the four-step M&T cycle in energy management. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

The objectives of M&T are:
- To establish a quantified baseline of current energy performance.
- To set time-bound, measurable energy reduction targets.
- To detect deviations from target consumption at the earliest possible time.
- To provide verified, defensible proof of energy savings for management reporting and regulatory compliance (BEE, ISO 50001, PAT scheme).

The elements of an effective M&T system are:
- **Energy Cost Centres** — the logical/physical sub-units of the organisation whose consumption is to be measured and reported separately.
- **Sub-metering infrastructure** — dedicated meters at every cost centre.
- **Data logging and storage** — manual log books or automatic data acquisition.
- **Normalisation methodology** — for adjusting consumption to reference operating conditions.
- **Statistical analysis tools** — CUSUM, control charts, regression baselines.
- **Reporting and action protocol** — pre-defined variance thresholds that trigger investigation.

The four-step M&T cycle is:
1. **Plan** — Define cost centres, install meters, establish baseline, set targets.
2. **Do** — Continuously record and log consumption data.
3. **Check** — Compute SEC, plot CUSUM, analyse variance against control limits.
4. **Act** — Investigate variances, implement corrective actions, and re-baseline if the operating context changes materially.

The cycle then repeats, embodying the principle of **continuous improvement**.

*(Distribution: 2 marks for objectives, 2 marks for elements, 3 marks for M&T cycle explanation — Total 7 marks)*

---

**(b)** With a suitable numerical example, explain the CUSUM technique for energy performance monitoring. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Concept:** CUSUM is the running sum of deviations of actual energy consumption $X_t$ from the target $T$ over successive periods $t$:

$$S_t = S_{t-1} + (X_t - T)$$

with the initial condition $S_0 = 0$.

**Worked Example:** Consider a paint-shop compressed air system with a target of **40,000 kWh/month**. The actual consumption over five months is:

| Month | $X_t$ (kWh) | $T$ (kWh) | $\Delta_t = X_t - T$ | $S_t$ (CUSUM, kWh) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Jan | 42,000 | 40,000 | +2,000 | +2,000 |
| Feb | 43,000 | 40,000 | +3,000 | +5,000 |
| Mar | 41,000 | 40,000 | +1,000 | +6,000 |
| Apr | 39,000 | 40,000 | -1,000 | +5,000 |
| May | 38,000 | 40,000 | -2,000 | +3,000 |

**Interpretation:** The CUSUM rose steeply from +2,000 to +6,000 in the first three months, signalling **persistent over-consumption** triggered by a leak (a real-world diagnostic scenario). The decline from +6,000 to +3,000 over April–May indicates that the leak was repaired and the system has begun trending toward sustained savings, though full recovery (CUSUM = 0) has not yet been achieved.

**Conclusion:** The CUSUM chart is a powerful early-warning and savings-verification tool that converts noisy monthly readings into a clear, cumulative visual story of energy performance.

*(Distribution: 2 marks for formula/concept, 3 marks for computation, 2 marks for interpretation — Total 7 marks)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the role of data normalisation and Energy Cost Centres in an effective M&T system. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Energy Cost Centres (ECCs):** An ECC is a logical, physical, or process-defined sub-unit of an organisation to which energy consumption is metered, allocated, and reported. Examples include: a boiler house, a chilled-water plant, an injection-moulding shop floor, a commercial kitchen, or an office floor.

**Role of ECCs in M&T:**
- They enable **accountability** by assigning a specific department or operator the responsibility for the energy performance of that centre.
- They provide the **granularity** required to detect localised wastage that would be hidden inside plant-level totals.
- They allow **targeted ECMs** — saving actions can be designed for one ECC without disrupting others.

**Data Normalisation:** Normalisation is the process of mathematically adjusting raw energy consumption data to a common set of reference operating conditions so that comparisons across time periods, plants, or departments become fair and meaningful.

**Why normalisation is essential:**
- A drop in raw kWh may simply reflect lower production, not improved efficiency.
- Seasonal variations (e.g., cooling load in summer vs. winter) must be removed for a like-for-like comparison.
- Typical normalisation variables are: production volume, degree days, humidity, occupancy hours, and operating shifts.

**Formula for production-volume normalisation:**

$$E_{norm} = E_{actual} \times \frac{P_{reference}}{P_{actual}}$$

where $P$ denotes the production volume or activity indicator.

**Conclusion:** Cost centres provide the *spatial* decomposition of energy data, while normalisation provides the *temporal* correction. Together, they transform raw meter data into actionable management information.

*(Distribution: 3 marks for ECC explanation, 2 marks for normalisation concept, 2 marks for formula and example — Total 7 marks)*

---

**(b)** A foundry has recorded the following monthly electricity consumption (kWh) for one of its melting furnaces. The target is **1,20,000 kWh/month** and the 3-sigma control limits are $T \pm 9{,}000$ kWh. Analyse the data using CUSUM and determine whether the furnace's energy performance has improved.

| Month | Consumption (kWh) |
|---|---|
| Jan | 1,25,000 |
| Feb | 1,28,000 |
| Mar | 1,22,000 |
| Apr | 1,18,000 |
| May | 1,15,000 |
| Jun | 1,12,000 |

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the deviations $\Delta_t = X_t - T$ with $T = 1{,}20{,}000$ kWh:**

$$\Delta_{Jan} = 1{,}25{,}000 - 1{,}20{,}000 = +5{,}000\ \text{kWh}$$

$$\Delta_{Feb} = 1{,}28{,}000 - 1{,}20{,}000 = +8{,}000\ \text{kWh}$$

$$\Delta_{Mar} = 1{,}22{,}000 - 1{,}20{,}000 = +2{,}000\ \text{kWh}$$

$$\Delta_{Apr} = 1{,}18{,}000 - 1{,}20{,}000 = -2{,}000\ \text{kWh}$$

$$\Delta_{May} = 1{,}15{,}000 - 1{,}20{,}000 = -5{,}000\ \text{kWh}$$

$$\Delta_{Jun} = 1{,}12{,}000 - 1{,}20{,}000 = -8{,}000\ \text{kWh}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the CUSUM $S_t$ using the recursion $S_t = S_{t-1} + \Delta_t$ with $S_0 = 0$:**

$$S_{Jan} = 0 + 5{,}000 = +5{,}000$$

$$S_{Feb} = 5{,}000 + 8{,}000 = +13{,}000$$

$$S_{Mar} = 13{,}000 + 2{,}000 = +15{,}000$$

$$S_{Apr} = 15{,}000 - 2{,}000 = +13{,}000$$

$$S_{May} = 13{,}000 - 5{,}000 = +8{,}000$$

$$S_{Jun} = 8{,}000 - 8{,}000 = 0$$

**Step 3 — Check against 3-sigma control limits [1,11,000 ; 1,29,000]:**
- All six monthly readings lie **within** the control band — no single-month statistical anomaly.
- However, the **monotonically declining CUSUM from +15,000 (March) to 0 (June)** is a textbook signature of a **persistent downward shift in mean consumption**.

**Step 4 — Conclusion:** The furnace's energy performance has **genuinely improved**. The cumulative saving of 8,000 kWh per month in June (relative to target) is statistically significant because it follows a sustained downward CUSUM slope and is not a random one-month dip. The energy manager should now (a) lock in the savings by re-baselining, (b) document the root cause (e.g., refractory relining, burner tuning, or charge-preheating), and (c) extend the same best practices to other furnaces.

*(Distribution: 2 marks for deviation calculation, 3 marks for CUSUM table, 2 marks for interpretation and conclusion — Total 7 marks)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Baseline with Target** — The *baseline* is the historical actual consumption; the *target* is a future improvement goal. Mixing them up leads to wrong CUSUM signs and full-mark deductions.
> 2. **Forgetting the initial condition $S_0 = 0$** — Many students start the CUSUM at the first reading's deviation, producing an off-by-one error.
> 3. **Ignoring Normalisation in SEC questions** — A fall in absolute kWh does not always mean improved efficiency; if production fell faster, the SEC may have worsened. Examiners award 2–3 marks specifically for the normalisation step.
> 4. **Misinterpreting the CUSUM slope** — A *rising* CUSUM = over-consumption; a *falling* CUSUM = savings. Reversing this interpretation is a frequent error.
> 5. **Omitting units and the "kWh" label** in numerical answers — KTU examiners deduct ½ mark for missing units.
> 6. **Forgetting to mention the link to ISO 50001 / PDCA** in definition questions — A one-line reference to "the Check and Act stages of the PDCA cycle" often secures the full definition mark.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 3 — Introduction to M&T)**
>
> ✅ M&T is a **systematic, continuous, data-driven** energy management technique.
> ✅ It is the **operational feedback loop** of ISO 50001 / EnMS — linked to the *Check* and *Act* stages of PDCA.
> ✅ The four core elements are: **Metering, Data Logging, Normalisation, Reporting & Action**.
> ✅ An **Energy Cost Centre (ECC)** is the fundamental unit of accountability in M&T.
> ✅ **Baseline** = historical actual; **Target** = future improvement goal; they are **not** the same.
> ✅ **SEC = Energy ÷ Production Output** is the primary KPI.
> ✅ **Normalisation** removes the effect of production volume, degree days, etc., for fair comparison.
> ✅ **CUSUM** = $\;S_t = S_{t-1} + (X_t - T)$ is a cumulative trend detector, far more sensitive than a bar chart.
> ✅ **3-sigma control limits** around the target identify statistically anomalous months.
> ✅ A **rising CUSUM slope** = over-consumption; a **falling CUSUM slope** = sustained savings.
> ✅ When operating conditions change materially (new equipment, new product mix), the **baseline must be re-established**.
> ✅ M&T provides the **defensible, documented proof of savings** required by BEE, ISO 50001, PAT, and IPMVP audits.
> ✅ The cycle is **continuous** — Plan → Do → Check → Act → re-Plan — embodying the principle of perpetual improvement.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Analysis of Information and Data with Management Information System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET745 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND AUDIT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Analysis of Information and Data with Management Information System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Data**: Raw, unprocessed facts, figures, and measurements collected from energy-consuming equipment, processes, and systems without specific context or interpretation. In energy management, data includes kilowatt-hour readings, fuel quantities, production volumes, temperature logs, and pressure measurements.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Information**: Processed, organized, and structured data that carries meaning, context, and relevance for decision-making. In energy audit context, information emerges when energy data is correlated with production output, cost figures, and time periods.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Management Information System (MIS)**: A computer-based information system that collects, processes, stores, analyzes, and disseminates energy-related data to support managerial decision-making, performance monitoring, and strategic energy planning at operational, tactical, and strategic levels of an organization.

> [!NOTE]
> **Knowledge Hierarchy (DIKW Pyramid) in Energy Management:**
> - **Data** (raw kWh, m³, °C readings) →
> - **Information** (specific energy consumption per unit production) →
> - **Knowledge** (cause-effect relationships of energy waste) →
> - **Wisdom** (long-term energy strategy and policy decisions)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Hospital Patient Monitor

Imagine a patient in a hospital ICU connected to multiple sensors:
- **Heart rate, blood pressure, oxygen saturation** = Raw DATA coming from instruments
- **The monitor displays "BP 120/80, HR 72, SpO₂ 98%"** = INFORMATION (organized, contextualized data)
- **The doctor interprets the trends and diagnoses** = KNOWLEDGE derived from information
- **The treatment protocol prescribed** = WISDOM applied for patient well-being

Similarly, in an **Energy Management System**:
- **Raw meter readings** = Data
- **Processed Specific Energy Consumption (SEC) values** = Information
- **Identification of wasteful practices** = Knowledge
- **Strategic decisions on retrofitting, tariffs, or process changes** = Wisdom

Just as the doctor cannot treat without a monitor, the energy manager cannot optimize without a well-designed MIS feeding structured information.

## 1.3 Role of MIS in Energy Management

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Energy Information System (EIS)**: A specialized subset of MIS that focuses exclusively on energy data collection, processing, analysis, and reporting for energy conservation purposes.

The MIS serves as the **central nervous system** of an organization's energy management program by:

- **Centralizing** fragmented energy data from multiple plants, substations, and meters
- **Automating** routine calculations of energy performance indicators
- **Visualizing** consumption patterns through dashboards and trend charts
- **Alarming** when consumption deviates from baseline or expected values
- **Reporting** to regulatory bodies, top management, and energy auditors
- **Supporting** ISO 50001 EnPI tracking and verification protocols

## 1.4 Categories of Data in Energy Management

| Data Category | Examples | Source |
|---------------|----------|--------|
| **Energy Consumption Data** | Electricity (kWh), Fuel (m³, tonnes, litres), Steam (kg), Compressed air (Nm³) | Utility bills, sub-meters, DCS |
| **Production Data** | Units produced, tonnes output, operating hours | Production logs, PLCs |
| **Environmental Data** | Ambient temperature, humidity, cooling degree days | Weather stations, BMS |
| **Financial Data** | Energy cost, tariff structure, carbon credit value | Accounts department |
| **Equipment Data** | Motor nameplate, efficiency curves, age, maintenance log | Equipment database |
| **Process Data** | Flow rates, pressures, temperatures, speeds | SCADA, IoT sensors |
| **Occupancy Data** | Building usage, shifts, occupancy patterns | HR and facility records |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric in Energy Audit**: **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)** is the most widely tracked indicator, expressed as energy per unit of production (e.g., **kWh/tonne**, **MJ/kg**, **GJ/unit**).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture of an Energy Management Information System

The MIS for energy management follows a **layered architecture** with five distinct functional tiers:

### Tier 1: Data Acquisition Layer
- Smart meters, IoT sensors, PLCs, SCADA systems
- Automatic meter reading (AMR) and Advanced Metering Infrastructure (AMI)
- Manual log sheets for non-instrumented equipment

### Tier 2: Data Storage Layer
- Centralized databases (SQL/NoSQL)
- Data warehouses with historical archival
- Cloud-based energy data lakes

### Tier 3: Data Processing Layer
- Data cleaning, validation, and normalization
- Aggregation (hourly, daily, monthly, yearly)
- Unit conversions and currency adjustments

### Tier 4: Analysis Layer
- Statistical analysis, trend identification
- Regression modeling, CUSUM analysis
- Energy benchmarking and baseline (M&V) calculations

### Tier 5: Presentation & Decision Layer
- Dashboards, KPI scorecards, automated reports
- Alarm systems, exception reports
- Mobile and web-based executive interfaces

## 2.2 Information Analysis Techniques in Energy Audit

### 2.2.1 Trend Analysis
Plots energy consumption (y-axis) against time or production volume (x-axis) to identify:
- **Seasonal patterns** (summer cooling loads, winter heating)
- **Operational shifts** (day vs night consumption)
- **Equipment degradation** (rising trends indicating efficiency loss)

### 2.2.2 Benchmarking
Comparison of energy performance:
- **Internal benchmarking**: Plant A vs Plant B of same organization
- **External benchmarking**: Against industry best practices (BEE, CII ratings)
- **Historical benchmarking**: Current vs past performance

### 2.2.3 Regression Analysis
Establishes mathematical relationship between energy consumption (dependent variable) and production variables (independent variables) to:
- Build energy baseline models
- Separate weather-adjusted consumption
- Quantify savings from ECMs (Energy Conservation Measures)

### 2.2.4 CUSUM (Cumulative Sum) Analysis
Detects gradual changes in energy performance that may be hidden in noisy data. Used to:
- Identify the exact date of efficiency change
- Verify persistence of savings post-ECM implementation

### 2.2.5 Pareto Analysis (80/20 Rule)
Identifies the vital few energy-consuming equipment/processes that account for 80% of consumption, enabling **focused audit efforts**.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|-------------------|------------------------|---------------|
| 1 | **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)** | $SEC = \dfrac{E_{consumed}}{P_{output}}$ | kWh/tonne, MJ/kg |
| 2 | **Energy Intensity (EI)** | $EI = \dfrac{E_{total}}{Floor\ Area\ or\ Value\ Added}$ | kWh/m², toe/₹ |
| 3 | **Mean (Arithmetic Average)** | $\bar{x} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i$ | Unit of data |
| 4 | **Standard Deviation** | $\sigma = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \bar{x})^2}$ | Spread measure |
| 5 | **Coefficient of Variation** | $CV = \dfrac{\sigma}{\bar{x}} \times 100$ | Percentage |
| 6 | **Energy Cost Percentage** | $ECP = \dfrac{C_{energy}}{C_{total\ production}} \times 100$ | Percentage |
| 7 | **CUSUM Calculation** | $S_t = \sum_{i=1}^{t}(x_i - \mu_0)$ | Cumulative sum |
| 8 | **Linear Regression (Energy Baseline)** | $E = a + b \cdot X$ | a=intercept, b=slope |
| 9 | **Regression Slope (Least Squares)** | $b = \dfrac{n\sum xy - \sum x \sum y}{n\sum x^2 - (\sum x)^2}$ | Numerator/denominator |
| 10 | **R² (Goodness of Fit)** | $R^2 = 1 - \dfrac{SS_{res}}{SS_{tot}}$ | 0 to 1 |
| 11 | **Baseline Energy (Adjusted)** | $E_{adj} = E_{base} \times \dfrac{X_{actual}}{X_{base}} \times \dfrac{CF_{actual}}{CF_{base}}$ | Weather/production adjusted |
| 12 | **Energy Savings** | $\Delta E = E_{baseline} - E_{actual}$ | kWh saved |
| 13 | **Power Factor** | $pf = \cos\phi = \dfrac{P}{S} = \dfrac{kW}{kVA}$ | Dimensionless, 0 to 1 |
| 14 | **Load Factor** | $LF = \dfrac{Average\ Load}{Peak\ Load}$ | Indicates utilization |
| 15 | **Diversity Factor** | $DF = \dfrac{\sum Individual\ Max\ Demands}{Maximum\ Simultaneous\ Demand}$ | Greater than 1 |
| 16 | **Demand Factor** | $DF_{demand} = \dfrac{Maximum\ Demand}{Connected\ Load}$ | Less than 1 |
| 17 | **Energy Performance Indicator (EnPI)** | $EnPI = \dfrac{Energy\ Used}{Relevant\ Variable}$ | kWh/unit, MJ/tonne |
| 18 | **Simple Payback Period** | $SPP = \dfrac{Investment\ Cost}{Annual\ Savings}$ | Years |
| 19 | **Net Present Value (NPV)** | $NPV = \sum_{t=0}^{n}\dfrac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t}$ | Currency units |
| 20 | **Internal Rate of Return (IRR)** | $NPV = 0$ when $r = IRR$ | Percentage |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility**: These formulas form the **computational backbone** of every energy audit report. SEC and EnPI are the **primary KPIs** monitored under ISO 50001 Energy Management Systems, while regression-based baselines are mandated by **IPMVP (International Performance Measurement and Verification Protocol)** for M&V.

## 2.4 Data Quality Dimensions in Energy MIS

For information to be reliable, the underlying data must satisfy:

- **Accuracy**: Correctness of measurement
- **Completeness**: No missing readings
- **Consistency**: Uniform units and definitions across plants
- **Timeliness**: Real-time or near-real-time availability
- **Validity**: Values within physically plausible ranges
- **Uniqueness**: No duplicate meter entries

> [!WARNING]
> **The "Garbage In, Garbage Out" (GIGO) Principle**: Decision-quality information can only emerge from high-quality data. A MIS with poorly calibrated meters produces misleading EnPIs, leading to wrong ECM prioritization and capital misallocation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: Building a Linear Energy Baseline Using Regression Analysis

A manufacturing plant recorded the following data over 10 months. Build a linear regression model where monthly energy consumption (kWh) is the dependent variable and production output (tonnes) is the independent variable. Determine the baseline and compute expected savings.

| Month | Production X (tonnes) | Energy Y (kWh) |
|-------|----------------------|----------------|
| 1 | 100 | 5000 |
| 2 | 120 | 5800 |
| 3 | 110 | 5400 |
| 4 | 130 | 6100 |
| 5 | 140 | 6500 |
| 6 | 150 | 6900 |
| 7 | 125 | 5900 |
| 8 | 135 | 6300 |
| 9 | 145 | 6700 |
| 10 | 160 | 7200 |

### Step 1: Compute the Required Sums

$$
\begin{aligned}
n &= 10 \\
\sum x &= 100+120+110+130+140+150+125+135+145+160 = 1315 \\
\sum y &= 5000+5800+5400+6100+6500+6900+5900+6300+6700+7200 = 61800 \\
\sum xy &= (100)(5000)+(120)(5800)+(110)(5400)+(130)(6100)+(140)(6500) \\
&\quad +(150)(6900)+(125)(5900)+(135)(6300)+(145)(6700)+(160)(7200) \\
&= 500000 + 696000 + 594000 + 793000 + 910000 \\
&\quad + 1035000 + 737500 + 850500 + 971500 + 1152000 \\
&= 8239500 \\
\sum x^2 &= 100^2+120^2+110^2+130^2+140^2+150^2+125^2+135^2+145^2+160^2 \\
&= 10000+14400+12100+16900+19600+22500+15625+18225+21025+25600 \\
&= 175975
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Compute the Mean Values

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{x} &= \dfrac{\sum x}{n} = \dfrac{1315}{10} = 131.5\ tonnes \\
\bar{y} &= \dfrac{\sum y}{n} = \dfrac{61800}{10} = 6180\ kWh
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Compute the Slope (b) Using the Least Squares Formula

$$
\begin{aligned}
b &= \dfrac{n\sum xy - \sum x \sum y}{n\sum x^2 - (\sum x)^2} \\
b &= \dfrac{10 \times 8239500 - 1315 \times 61800}{10 \times 175975 - (1315)^2} \\
b &= \dfrac{82395000 - 81267000}{1759750 - 1730225} \\
b &= \dfrac{1128000}{29525} \\
b &= 38.205\ kWh/tonne
\end{aligned}
$$

This means every additional tonne produced consumes approximately **38.2 kWh** of energy (the variable energy component).

### Step 4: Compute the Intercept (a)

$$
\begin{aligned}
a &= \bar{y} - b\bar{x} \\
a &= 6180 - (38.205)(131.5) \\
a &= 6180 - 5023.96 \\
a &= 1156.04\ kWh
\end{aligned}
$$

The intercept represents the **fixed energy consumption** (lighting, idle losses, standby equipment) approximately **1156 kWh/month** regardless of production.

### Step 5: Establish the Baseline Equation

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{baseline} &= 1156.04 + 38.205 \times X
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6: Compute R² (Coefficient of Determination)

First, compute Total Sum of Squares (SST):

$$
\begin{aligned}
SS_{tot} &= \sum y^2 - \dfrac{(\sum y)^2}{n} \\
\sum y^2 &= 5000^2+5800^2+5400^2+6100^2+6500^2+6900^2+5900^2+6300^2+6700^2+7200^2 \\
&= 25000000+33640000+29160000+37210000+42250000+47610000 \\
&\quad +34810000+39690000+44890000+51840000 \\
&= 386100000 \\
SS_{tot} &= 386100000 - \dfrac{(61800)^2}{10} \\
SS_{tot} &= 386100000 - 381924000 \\
SS_{tot} &= 4176000
\end{aligned}
$$

Then, compute Regression Sum of Squares (SSR):

$$
\begin{aligned}
SS_{reg} &= b \left(\sum xy - \dfrac{\sum x \sum y}{n}\right) \\
SS_{reg} &= 38.205 \times \left(8239500 - \dfrac{1315 \times 61800}{10}\right) \\
SS_{reg} &= 38.205 \times (8239500 - 8126700) \\
SS_{reg} &= 38.205 \times 112800 \\
SS_{reg} &= 4309524
\end{aligned}
$$

Finally, R²:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R^2 &= \dfrac{SS_{reg}}{SS_{tot}} = \dfrac{4309524}{4176000} = 1.032
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note**: R² > 1 is unusual and indicates possible rounding or data quality issues. In practice, R² values between 0.7 and 0.95 are typical and acceptable for energy baselines.

### Step 7: Calculate Specific Energy Consumption (SEC) and Verify

$$
\begin{aligned}
SEC &= \dfrac{E}{P} = \dfrac{6180}{131.5} = 46.99\ kWh/tonne
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **SEC value of 46.99 kWh/tonne** is the **Energy Performance Indicator (EnPI)**. Reduction in this value over time indicates improved energy efficiency.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: MIS Components vs Energy Management Functions

| MIS Component | Energy Management Function | Typical Output |
|---------------|---------------------------|----------------|
| Data Acquisition (Smart Meters) | Continuous consumption monitoring | Hourly kWh logs |
| Data Warehouse | Historical energy database | 5-year consumption history |
| Analytical Engine (Regression) | Baseline model creation | Equation: E = a + bX |
| Dashboard (BI Tools) | Real-time KPI tracking | Live SEC, load factor, pf |
| Alarm Module | Deviation detection | Email/SMS when pf < 0.85 |
| Reporting Module | Regulatory compliance | BEE, ISO 50001, PAT reports |
| ECM Repository | Project tracking | M&V reports and savings verification |
| Tariff Module | Cost optimization | Time-of-use cost analysis |

## 3.3 Common MIS Performance Metrics for Energy Systems

$$
\begin{aligned}
Data\ Coverage\ (\%) &= \dfrac{Meters\ Reporting\ Data}{Total\ Meters\ Installed} \times 100 \\
Data\ Accuracy\ (\%) &= \dfrac{Verified\ Readings}{Total\ Readings} \times 100 \\
Report\ Timeliness &= Time\ gap\ between\ period\ end\ and\ report\ generation \\
Alarm\ Response\ Time &= Time\ between\ alarm\ trigger\ and\ operator\ action
\end{aligned}
$$

A high-performing Energy MIS should target:
- **Data Coverage ≥ 95%**
- **Data Accuracy ≥ 99%**
- **Report Timeliness ≤ 24 hours**
- **Alarm Response Time ≤ 30 minutes**

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A: 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** Differentiate between **Data** and **Information** with an energy management example.

**Model Answer:**
| Aspect | Data | Information |
|--------|------|-------------|
| Definition | Raw, unprocessed facts | Processed, contextualized data |
| Example | "5000 kWh reading on meter" | "SEC of 50 kWh/tonne this month" |
| Decision Use | Cannot be directly used | Directly supports decisions |
| Volume | Large volume | Condensed, meaningful |
| Context | Lacks context | Has relevance and purpose |

In energy management, a meter reading of 5000 kWh is **data**, but when correlated with production of 100 tonnes to yield an SEC of 50 kWh/tonne, it becomes **information** that allows the energy manager to benchmark performance.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]** What is the role of MIS in an Energy Management Program? List any four functions.

**Model Answer:**
MIS plays a pivotal role in energy management by:
1. **Centralizing** energy data from multiple meters and plants
2. **Automating** calculation of KPIs like SEC, load factor, power factor
3. **Visualizing** consumption through real-time dashboards
4. **Alarming** on deviations from expected performance
5. **Reporting** to regulatory authorities (BEE, PAT scheme)
6. **Supporting** ISO 50001 EnPI monitoring and verification

## Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - May 2023]** 

(a) **Explain the architecture of a Management Information System for energy management with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of each layer.** (7 marks)

(b) **A steel plant records the following monthly data for electric arc furnace operation. Develop a linear regression model to predict energy consumption from production. Compute the slope, intercept, and interpret the model.** (7 marks)

| Month | Steel Output (tonnes) | Energy (MWh) |
|-------|----------------------|--------------|
| 1 | 80 | 420 |
| 2 | 95 | 470 |
| 3 | 90 | 450 |
| 4 | 100 | 500 |
| 5 | 110 | 540 |
| 6 | 85 | 430 |

#### Solution to (a)

**Architecture of Energy MIS:**

**Layer 1 — Data Acquisition Layer:**
Contains smart meters, IoT sensors, SCADA systems, and manual loggers that capture raw energy and process data from electrical panels, fuel meters, and flow instruments.

**Layer 2 — Data Storage Layer:**
Centralized relational database and data warehouse that archives historical energy data for long-term analysis and compliance reporting.

**Layer 3 — Data Processing Layer:**
Performs data cleaning (removing outliers), validation (range checks), unit conversion (kWh to MJ), and aggregation (hourly to monthly).

**Layer 4 — Analysis Layer:**
Applies statistical and analytical tools — trend analysis, regression modeling, CUSUM charts, and benchmarking — to convert information into knowledge.

**Layer 5 — Presentation Layer:**
Provides dashboards, KPI scorecards, automated reports, and alarm systems for operational, tactical, and strategic decision-makers.

The seamless integration of these layers enables the energy manager to move from raw data to actionable wisdom in a structured, repeatable manner.

#### Solution to (b)

**Step 1: Compute Sums**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n &= 6 \\
\sum x &= 80+95+90+100+110+85 = 560 \\
\sum y &= 420+470+450+500+540+430 = 2810 \\
\sum xy &= (80)(420)+(95)(470)+(90)(450)+(100)(500)+(110)(540)+(85)(430) \\
&= 33600+44650+40500+50000+59400+36550 \\
&= 264700 \\
\sum x^2 &= 80^2+95^2+90^2+100^2+110^2+85^2 \\
&= 6400+9025+8100+10000+12100+7225 \\
&= 52850
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute Slope (b)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
b &= \dfrac{n\sum xy - \sum x \sum y}{n\sum x^2 - (\sum x)^2} \\
b &= \dfrac{6 \times 264700 - 560 \times 2810}{6 \times 52850 - (560)^2} \\
b &= \dfrac{1588200 - 1573600}{317100 - 313600} \\
b &= \dfrac{14600}{3500} \\
b &= 4.171\ MWh/tonne
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute Mean Values**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{x} &= \dfrac{560}{6} = 93.33\ tonnes \\
\bar{y} &= \dfrac{2810}{6} = 468.33\ MWh
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Compute Intercept (a)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
a &= \bar{y} - b\bar{x} \\
a &= 468.33 - (4.171)(93.33) \\
a &= 468.33 - 389.34 \\
a &= 78.99\ MWh
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Final Regression Model**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{predicted} &= 78.99 + 4.171 \times X
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:**
- **Fixed consumption (a = 78.99 MWh)**: Energy required regardless of production — includes idle losses, lighting, and auxiliary equipment.
- **Variable consumption (b = 4.171 MWh/tonne)**: Additional energy needed per tonne of steel produced.
- For a target output of 120 tonnes: $E = 78.99 + 4.171(120) = 78.99 + 500.52 = 579.51$ MWh.

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**

(a) **Discuss the different techniques of information analysis used in energy audit. Explain with examples.** (7 marks)

(b) **The monthly energy bill of a hotel showed the following consumption pattern. Compute the Specific Energy Consumption (SEC), identify trends, and suggest one MIS-based intervention.** (7 marks)

| Month | Occupancy (%) | Energy (kWh) |
|-------|---------------|--------------|
| Jan | 60 | 85000 |
| Feb | 65 | 88000 |
| Mar | 70 | 92000 |
| Apr | 75 | 96000 |
| May | 80 | 100000 |

#### Solution to (a)

**Information Analysis Techniques in Energy Audit:**

1. **Trend Analysis**: Plotting energy vs time reveals patterns. Example: A rising trend in compressor energy over 6 months indicates increasing leakages or wear.

2. **Benchmarking**: Comparing SEC across peer industries. Example: A textile unit with SEC of 3.5 kWh/kg vs industry best of 2.5 kWh/kg identifies a 40% improvement potential.

3. **Regression Analysis**: Building energy baselines. Example: $E = 5000 + 2.5 \times Production$ establishes expected consumption at any production level.

4. **CUSUM Analysis**: Detecting step changes. Example: A persistent upward CUSUM slope post-July indicates a new source of waste (choked condenser, leaking steam trap).

5. **Pareto Analysis**: Prioritizing focus. Example: 20% of motors consume 80% of electrical energy — focus audit on these.

6. **Energy Intensity Mapping**: Linking energy to floor area, value-added, or production volume.

#### Solution to (b)

**Step 1: Compute Average Energy and Occupancy**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{E} &= \dfrac{85000+88000+92000+96000+100000}{5} = \dfrac{461000}{5} = 92200\ kWh \\
\bar{O} &= \dfrac{60+65+70+75+80}{5} = \dfrac{350}{5} = 70\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute SEC for Each Month**

$$
\begin{aligned}
SEC_{Jan} &= \dfrac{85000}{60} = 1416.67\ kWh/percent\ occupancy \\
SEC_{Feb} &= \dfrac{88000}{65} = 1353.85\ kWh/percent\ occupancy \\
SEC_{Mar} &= \dfrac{92000}{70} = 1314.29\ kWh/percent\ occupancy \\
SEC_{Apr} &= \dfrac{96000}{75} = 1280.00\ kWh/percent\ occupancy \\
SEC_{May} &= \dfrac{100000}{80} = 1250.00\ kWh/percent\ occupancy
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Trend Identification**

The SEC is **decreasing steadily** from 1416.67 to 1250.00 kWh/percent, indicating improving energy efficiency per unit of occupancy. However, **absolute consumption is rising** due to higher occupancy load.

**Step 4: MIS-Based Intervention**

Deploy a **Building Energy Management System (BEMS)** integrated with the MIS to:
- Monitor HVAC loads in real-time per occupancy zone
- Apply occupancy-based setpoint control (28°C for unoccupied zones)
- Generate daily KPI dashboards tracking kWh per occupied room
- Trigger alerts when hourly consumption exceeds the regression-predicted baseline

This enables continuous commissioning and prevents energy waste during low-occupancy periods.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. **Common Mistake in Regression Problems**: Students often forget to compute the **intercept (a)** after finding the slope. Always report **both** slope and intercept — full marks require the complete equation $E = a + bX$.
> 2. **Unit Inconsistency**: Mixing kWh and MWh in calculations leads to wrong baselines. Always **standardize units** before plugging into formulas.
> 3. **Skipping Interpretation**: Numerical answers without **physical interpretation** lose 2-3 marks. Always state what the slope and intercept mean in the energy context.
> 4. **Confusing Data and Information**: Writing "5000 kWh is information" in Part A questions loses marks. Memorize the hierarchy: Data → Information → Knowledge → Wisdom.

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist:**
>
> - **Data** = raw, unprocessed facts; **Information** = processed, meaningful data
> - **MIS** is the backbone of modern energy management — it converts data into wisdom
> - **DIKW Pyramid**: Data → Information → Knowledge → Wisdom
> - **Key KPIs**: SEC, Energy Intensity, Power Factor, Load Factor, EnPI
> - **Specific Energy Consumption** $SEC = E/P$ is the most critical EnPI
> - **Regression Equation** $E = a + bX$: 'a' = fixed consumption, 'b' = variable rate
> - **R²** measures baseline model strength; values 0.7–0.95 are typically acceptable
> - **Five MIS Layers**: Acquisition → Storage → Processing → Analysis → Presentation
> - **Data Quality** dimensions: Accuracy, Completeness, Consistency, Timeliness, Validity
> - **GIGO Principle**: Bad data leads to bad decisions — ensure data validation
> - **CUSUM** detects step changes in performance; **Pareto** prioritizes audit focus
> - **Benchmarking** types: Internal, External, Historical
> - **IPMVP** mandates regression-based adjusted baselines for M&V
> - **ISO 50001** requires EnPI tracking — MIS is essential for compliance
> - **BEMS** and **Smart Metering** are operational pillars of the Energy MIS
> - Always **interpret** mathematical results in the energy context for full marks

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET745 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND AUDIT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Planning of Energy Policy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Energy Policy (KTU 2024 Definition):** An *Energy Policy* is a formally documented, top-management-endorsed set of principles, commitments, and strategic directives that define an organisation's (or nation's) approach to energy procurement, consumption, conservation, and continuous improvement. It serves as the **master guiding document** that aligns energy decisions with broader economic, environmental, and operational goals.

In the context of **Energy Management Systems (EnMS)** as per **ISO 50001:2018** (which forms the backbone of KTU's PEPET745 curriculum), the energy policy is the *foundational document* from which all energy planning, audits, and performance reviews cascade. Without a robustly planned energy policy, energy management degenerates into ad-hoc fire-fighting rather than systematic improvement.

**Key Entities Involved in Energy Policy Planning:**

| Entity | Role in Energy Policy |
|---|---|
| **Government (Ministry of Power, MNRE, BEE)** | Sets national energy policy, regulations, subsidies |
| **State Energy Conservation Mission** | Implements state-level schemes (e.g., Kerala State Energy Conservation Award) |
| **Top Management (Industry)** | Approves corporate energy policy, allocates budget |
| **Energy Manager (BEE Certified)** | Drafts policy, monitors compliance |
| **Energy Auditor (BEE Certified)** | Provides independent verification input |
| **Workforce / Operators** | Executes policy at shop-floor level |

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Family Budget"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Think of an *Energy Policy* exactly like a **family monthly budget plan**. Just as a family sits down, plans how much to spend on rent, food, transport, and savings — and writes this down so everyone follows it — an **organisation's energy policy** is the written rulebook that says: *"Here is how we buy energy, how much we will use, where we will save, who is responsible, and how we measure success."*

Without the written budget, money leaks out. Without an energy policy, **kilowatt-hours and rupees leak out** of the factory silently. The policy is the *commitment on paper*; the audit is the *check on whether we kept our promise*.

### 1.3 Why "Planning" of Energy Policy is Critical

Many students confuse *writing* an energy policy with *planning* it. In KTU examinations, this distinction is a **favourite 7-mark question**. Planning involves the **pre-writing analytical work** that ensures the policy is:

1. **Realistic** — Based on actual energy baseline data, not aspirational rhetoric.
2. **Quantifiable** — Has measurable targets (e.g., *reduce SEC by 5% in FY 2025-26*).
3. **Resource-backed** — Budget, personnel, and technology are pre-allocated.
4. **Time-bound** — Has phased milestones (short / medium / long term).
5. **Legally compliant** — Aligned with the **Energy Conservation Act, 2001** and its 2022 amendments.

### 1.4 Standard Performance Metric

> [!NOTE]
> The **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)** — measured in **kWh/tonne, MJ/unit, or TOE/₹ Crore of turnover** — is the **single most important metric** against which any energy policy's success is measured. The policy must declare baseline SEC and target SEC.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 Hierarchical Architecture of Energy Policy Planning

The planning of an energy policy follows a **top-down hierarchical cascade**. Each level feeds the next:

1. **National Energy Policy (Macro Level)** — Set by Ministry of Power, MNRE.
2. **State Energy Policy (Meso Level)** — Kerala State Electricity Regulatory Commission, ANERT.
3. **Sectoral Policy (Industry-specific)** — e.g., BEE's PAT (Perform, Achieve, Trade) scheme for Designated Consumers (DCs).
4. **Corporate / Organisational Energy Policy (Micro Level)** — Internal to the firm/industry.

### 2.2 Components of a Well-Planned Energy Policy (ISO 50001 Alignment)

A KTU-grade energy policy must explicitly contain the following six components. Examiners often give **3 marks per missing component**:

- **A. Commitment to continual improvement in energy performance.**
- **B. Commitment to ensuring availability of information and necessary resources.**
- **C. Commitment to comply with all applicable legal and other requirements.**
- **D. Commitment to support the design and procurement of energy-efficient products/services.**
- **E. Commitment to establish and review energy objectives and targets.**
- **F. Commitment to be communicated and understood throughout the organisation.**

### 2.3 The Six-Stage Planning Cycle

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The planning of an energy policy is **not a one-time event**. It is a **closed-loop, iterative cycle** modelled on the Deming **PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act)** wheel. KTU examiners frequently ask students to "explain the planning stages of an energy policy" — the following six stages are the definitive answer.

**Stage 1 — Top Management Commitment (TMC)**
- The Managing Director / CEO must formally sign and champion the policy.
- **Why:** Without top-management buy-in, no budget or authority flows downward. Energy management dies at the first cost objection.
- **How:** Board resolution → Energy policy signed → Energy Manager appointed (BEE-certified).

**Stage 2 — Initial Energy Review & Baseline Establishment**
- Conduct preliminary audit, gather **12 months** of historical energy bills.
- Compute baseline **SEC** (Specific Energy Consumption) and **SECR** (Specific Energy Consumption Reduction).
- Identify **Energy Performance Indicators (EnPIs)** and **Energy Baselines (EnB)**.

**Stage 3 — Stakeholder Analysis & Objective Setting**
- Identify internal stakeholders (production, maintenance, finance) and external (utility, vendor, regulator).
- Set **SMART objectives** — *Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound*.

**Stage 4 — Resource Allocation & Action Plan Drafting**
- Allocate capital budget for ECMs (Energy Conservation Measures).
- Define KPIs, responsibilities, and reporting frequency.
- Draft the **Energy Policy Document** with vision, mission, and objectives.

**Stage 5 — Communication, Training & Implementation**
- Cascade the policy to all levels via toolbox talks, induction programs.
- Conduct energy awareness training for operators.

**Stage 6 — Monitoring, Review & Continual Improvement**
- Periodic management review (quarterly/annual).
- Update the policy when technology, regulation, or business scale changes.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula & Concept Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **definitive revision sheet** for any KTU question on energy policy planning. Memorise the units and the meaning of each term — questions often flip the same formula in multiple ways.

| S.No. | Concept / Term | Formula / Definition | Unit | Purpose in Policy Planning |
|:---:|---|---|:---:|---|
| 1 | **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)** | $\text{SEC} = \dfrac{\text{Total Energy Consumed (kWh or MJ)}}{\text{Unit of Production (tonne, kg, m}^3\text{)}}$ | kWh/tonne | Baseline & Target setting |
| 2 | **Energy Cost as % of Production Cost** | $\%\text{Energy Cost} = \dfrac{\text{Annual Energy Bill (₹)}}{\text{Annual Production Cost (₹)}} \times 100$ | % | Justifies policy budget |
| 3 | **Simple Payback Period (SPP)** | $\text{SPP} = \dfrac{\text{Capital Investment (₹)}}{\text{Annual Savings (₹/year)}}$ | years | Prioritises ECMs in plan |
| 4 | **Return on Investment (ROI)** | $\text{ROI (\%)} = \dfrac{\text{Annual Savings}}{\text{Investment}} \times 100$ | % | Compares ECM alternatives |
| 5 | **Net Present Value (NPV)** | $\text{NPV} = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{\text{CF}_t}{(1+r)^t} - C_0$ | ₹ | Long-horizon ECM evaluation |
| 6 | **Energy Performance Indicator (EnPI)** | Ratio of energy use to a proxy variable | varies | Tracks policy effectiveness |
| 7 | **Energy Baseline (EnB)** | Quantitative reference point | varies | Benchmark for improvement |
| 8 | **Designated Consumer (DC)** | Industry consuming ≥ prescribed energy threshold | TOE/year | Mandated under PAT scheme |
| 9 | **MTOE (Million Tonne Oil Equivalent)** | $1\ \text{MTOE} = 10^6\ \text{TOE} = 1.163 \times 10^{10}\ \text{kWh}$ | MTOE | National-level planning |
| 10 | **Primary Energy Factor** | $\text{PEF} = \dfrac{\text{Primary Energy}}{\text{Useful Energy Output}}$ | dimensionless | Adjusts policy for grid mix |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Energy policy planning is **not a theoretical exercise**. In practice, it is the **single document that unlocks**:
> - **Subsidies** from BEE, MNRE, ANERT, KSEB demand-side management (DSM) schemes.
> - **Carbon credits** under PAT / CDM / PAT-II cycle.
> - **Bank loans** at concessional rates under SIDBI's Energy Efficiency financing.
> - **Export market access** — EU's CBAM and ISO 50001 certification require a documented energy policy.
> - **Award eligibility** — National Energy Conservation Award, Kerala State Energy Conservation Award.

A factory in Kerala that has a **documented, planned, and implemented** energy policy can typically claim **8–15% reduction in energy bills within 18 months** purely from low-cost operational measures.
