# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]

**Q1. Define an Energy Saving Measure (ESM) and classify it into three tiers, giving one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **Energy Saving Measure (ESM)** is a planned intervention—managerial, maintenance-related, or technological—designed to reduce the specific energy consumption of a process or facility without affecting the quality of the end-use service.

**Classification:**

- **Tier I — No-cost / Housekeeping:** Switching off idle motors when the line is not running *(1 mark)*.
- **Tier II — Low-cost retrofit:** Installing a Variable Frequency Drive (VFD) on a 30 kW pump motor *(1 mark)*.
- **Tier III — High-cost capital project:** Replacing an inefficient fire-tube boiler with a high-efficiency water-tube boiler with economiser *(1 mark)*.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]

**Q2. Distinguish between Simple Payback Period and Return on Investment as decision metrics for an energy project.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Simple Payback (SPP) | Return on Investment (ROI) |
|--------|----------------------|----------------------------|
| **Definition** | Time taken to recover the capital from savings | Annual percentage return on capital |
| **Formula** | $SPP = C_0 / C_t$ (yrs) | $ROI = (C_t / C_0) \times 100$ (%) |
| **Time value of money** | Ignored *(1 mark)* | Ignored |
| **Best use** | Quick screening of Tier-II projects *(1 mark)* | Comparative ranking of small projects |
| **Drawback** | Ignores post-payback savings | Static, single-year snapshot |

Both are *preliminary* screening tools; rigorous appraisal of large projects requires **NPV and IRR** *(1 mark)*.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – July 2024 Model]**

**Q A.** A 50 kW induction motor in a pumping station operates at 80 % loading for 6,000 hours/year. The plant engineer proposes **two alternatives**:

- **Option I:** Install a VFD at a capital cost of **₹ 4,50,000**; expected reduction in energy consumption = **30 %**; O&M = **₹ 5,000/yr**; life = **10 years**; no salvage.
- **Option II:** Replace the motor with a **high-efficiency IE4 motor** at a capital cost of **₹ 6,00,000**; expected reduction in energy consumption = **8 %**; O&M = **₹ 2,000/yr**; life = **12 years**; salvage = **₹ 30,000**.

Electricity tariff = **₹ 6.50/kWh**; MARR = **12 %**.

**(a)** Compute the annual energy saved, annual cost saving, and **Simple Payback Period** for *both* options. Which passes a 3-year hurdle? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Compute the **NPV** of *both* options and recommend the better one. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Solution to Q A

**Pre-step — Present Annual Energy Consumption.**

$$
E_b = 50 \text{ kW} \times 0.80 \times 6000 \text{ h/yr} = 2\,40\,000 \text{ kWh/yr}
$$

---

**Part (a) — Energy, Saving, SPP (7 marks)**

**Option I (VFD):**

- $\Delta E_I = 0.30 \times 2\,40\,000 = 72\,000 \text{ kWh/yr}$ *(1 mark)*
- $C_{g,I} = 72\,000 \times 6.50 = ₹\,4\,68\,000 \text{ /yr}$
- $C_{t,I} = 4\,68\,000 - 5\,000 = ₹\,4\,63\,000 \text{ /yr}$ *(1 mark)*
- $SPP_I = 4\,50\,000 / 4\,63\,000 = 0.972 \text{ years} \approx 11.7 \text{ months}$ *(1 mark)*
- **Passes 3-year hurdle ✓**

**Option II (IE4 motor):**

- $\Delta E_{II} = 0.08 \times 2\,40\,000 = 19\,200 \text{ kWh/yr}$ *(1 mark)*
- $C_{g,II} = 19\,200 \times 6.50 = ₹\,1\,24\,800 \text{ /yr}$
- $C_{t,II} = 1\,24\,800 - 2\,000 = ₹\,1\,22\,800 \text{ /yr}$ *(1 mark)*
- $SPP_{II} = 6\,00\,000 / 1\,22\,800 = 4.886 \text{ years}$ *(1 mark)*
- **Fails 3-year hurdle ✗** *(1 mark)*

---

**Part (b) — NPV Comparison (7 marks)**

**PVAF values:**
- $PVAF_{12\%,10} = 5.6502$
- $PVAF_{12\%,12} = 6.1944$

**Option I NPV (n = 10, no salvage):**

$$
NPV_I = -4\,50\,000 + 4\,63\,000 \times 5.6502
$$

$$
NPV_I = -4\,50\,000 + 26\,15\,943 = ₹\,21\,65\,943 \;\;(>0) \quad (2 \text{ marks})
$$

**Option II NPV (n = 12, salvage = ₹ 30,000):**

- $PV_{C_t} = 1\,22\,800 \times 6.1944 = ₹\,7\,60\,677$
- $PV_{SV} = 30\,000 / (1.12)^{12} = 30\,000 / 3.8960 = ₹\,7\,700$ *(1 mark)*

$$
NPV_{II} = -6\,00\,000 + 7\,60\,677 + 7\,700 = ₹\,1\,68\,377 \quad (2 \text{ marks})
$$

**Recommendation:**

$$
\boxed{\text{Option I (VFD) — NPV = ₹ 21,65,943, far superior to Option II (₹ 1,68,377)}} \quad (2 \text{ marks})
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do not** forget to multiply motor kW by the **load factor (0.80)** before computing energy; doing so double-counts or under-counts the load and is a *common 1-mark loss*.
> - **Do not** skip the **O&M deduction** from gross savings before computing SPP and NPV.
> - **Do not** omit the **salvage PV term** in Option II; it is a 1-mark free mark.
> - **Do not** present SPP in *months only*; always state *years and months* for clarity (e.g., "0.972 years ≈ 11.7 months").

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**Q B.** A process industry has identified the following **Energy Saving Measures** from a recent energy audit:

| Measure | Cap. Cost (₹) | Annual Saving (kWh) | Life (yrs) | O&M (₹/yr) |
|---------|---------------|---------------------|------------|------------|
| M1 — Capacitor bank for PF correction | 2,00,000 | 40,000 | 8 | 3,000 |
| M2 — Replacement of old chiller | 18,00,000 | 1,50,000 | 15 | 25,000 |
| M3 — Insulation of steam lines | 5,00,000 | 60,000 | 10 | 4,000 |

Tariff = **₹ 7.20/kWh**; MARR = **15 %**.

**(a)** Calculate the **SPP** of each measure and identify the **investment tier** (I/II/III) of each. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Calculate the **NPV** of each and rank the measures. Which measure(s) should be approved if the management has a **budget cap of ₹ 20 lakh**? *(7 marks)*

---

#### Solution to Q B

**Pre-step — Annual Cost Savings (gross).**

- $C_{g,M1} = 40\,000 \times 7.20 = ₹\,2\,88\,000$
- $C_{g,M2} = 1\,50\,000 \times 7.20 = ₹\,10\,80\,000$
- $C_{g,M3} = 60\,000 \times 7.20 = ₹\,4\,32\,000$

**Net annual cash flows:**

- $C_{t,M1} = 2\,88\,000 - 3\,000 = ₹\,2\,85\,000$
- $C_{t,M2} = 10\,80\,000 - 25\,000 = ₹\,10\,55\,000$
- $C_{t,M3} = 4\,32\,000 - 4\,000 = ₹\,4\,28\,000$

---

**Part (a) — SPP and Tier Classification (7 marks)**

- $SPP_{M1} = 2\,00\,000 / 2\,85\,000 = 0.702 \text{ yr} \approx 8.4 \text{ months}$ *(1 mark)*
- $SPP_{M2} = 18\,00\,000 / 10\,55\,000 = 1.706 \text{ yr} \approx 1 \text{ yr 8.5 months}$ *(1 mark)*
- $SPP_{M3} = 5\,00\,000 / 4\,28\,000 = 1.168 \text{ yr} \approx 1 \text{ yr 2 months}$ *(1 mark)*

**Tier classification (using KTU thresholds from Section 1.3):**

- M1: ₹ 2 L → **Tier II** (Low-cost) *(1 mark)*
- M2: ₹ 18 L → **Tier III** (High-cost) *(1 mark)*
- M3: ₹ 5 L → **Tier II** (Low-cost) *(1 mark)*

*Bonus: All three pass a 2-year hurdle, but M2 is borderline given its high capital intensity.* *(1 mark)*

---

**Part (b) — NPV at 15 % (7 marks)**

**PVAF values:**

- $PVAF_{15\%,8} = \dfrac{1 - (1.15)^{-8}}{0.15} = \dfrac{1 - 0.3269}{0.15} = 4.4873$
- $PVAF_{15\%,10} = \dfrac{1 - (1.15)^{-10}}{0.15} = \dfrac{1 - 0.2472}{0.15} = 5.0188$
- $PVAF_{15\%,15} = \dfrac{1 - (1.15)^{-15}}{0.15} = \dfrac{1 - 0.1229}{0.15} = 5.8474$

**NPV calculations:**

**M1:**

$$
NPV_{M1} = -2\,00\,000 + 2\,85\,000 \times 4.4873 = -2\,00\,000 + 12\,78\,881 = ₹\,10\,78\,881 \quad (2 \text{ marks})
$$

**M2:**

$$
NPV_{M2} = -18\,00\,000 + 10\,55\,000 \times 5.8474 = -18\,00\,000 + 61\,68\,007 = ₹\,43\,68\,007 \quad (2 \text{ marks})
$$

**M3:**

$$
NPV_{M3} = -5\,00\,000 + 4\,28\,000 \times 5.0188 = -5\,00\,000 + 21\,48\,046 = ₹\,16\,48\,046 \quad (2 \text{ marks})
$$

**Ranking by NPV:**

$$
M2 \gg M3 \gg M1 \quad (1 \text{ mark})
$$

**Budget-Cap Recommendation (₹ 20 L cap):**

- **M2 alone costs ₹ 18 L** → fits within budget; **APPROVE M2** as it has the highest NPV per rupee invested and exceeds M1+M3 combined NPV.
- *Profitability Index (PI) check:* PI of M2 = $43\,68\,007 / 18\,00\,000 = 2.43$, which is the **highest** of the three. *(1 mark)*

$$
\boxed{\text{Approve M2 (Chiller replacement). It uses ₹ 18 L of the ₹ 20 L cap, leaves ₹ 2 L contingency, and yields NPV of ₹ 43.68 L.}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do not** confuse SPP with *Discounted Payback*; the question says SPP, not discounted payback.
> - **Do not** apply the wrong PVAF — each measure has a *different project life* (8, 15, 10 years), and using the wrong $n$ will yield a wrong NPV.
> - **Do not** rank projects by SPP alone when MARR is given; **NPV and PI are the correct ranking criteria under capital rationing**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Energy Saving Measures and Projects**
>
> - [ ] An **ESM** is an intervention; an **ESP** is a *budgeted, time-bound, M&V-backed* implementation of one or more ESMs.
> - [ ] Three **investment tiers**: No-cost (I), Low-cost (II, ₹ 1–25 L), High-cost (III, > ₹ 25 L).
> - [ ] The **"3M" framework**: Maintenance, Managerial, Modernisation — every ESM must be classified.
> - [ ] **SPP** is for screening; **NPV + IRR** is for final sanction; **PI** is for capital-rationed ranking.
> - [ ] **NPV ≥ 0** ⇒ accept; **IRR ≥ MARR** ⇒ accept; **SPP ≤ Hurdle** ⇒ proceed to detailed appraisal.
> - [ ] Always **subtract O&M from gross savings** before computing $C_t$.
> - [ ] For multi-year projects, use **PVAF** for constant cash flows and **year-wise discount factor** for variable cash flows.
> - [ ] **Salvage value** must be discounted to present value and added to NPV inflows.
> - [ ] **IPMVP** has four options (A, B, C, D); KTU expects awareness of M&V as the *last stage* of every ESP.
> - [ ] **Sensitivity analysis** must be done on the **top three input variables** (tariff, energy saving %, capital cost) before investment sanction.
> - [ ] **Profitability Index (PI)** = PV of future cash flows / Initial investment — used to rank projects under budget cap.
> - [ ] The **Capital Recovery Factor (CRF)** converts a present value into an equivalent annual cost, used in **LCC/EAC** comparison of equipment alternatives.
> - [ ] **% Energy saving** = $(\Delta E / E_b) \times 100$; **SEC** = total energy / production — both are KTU-board standard KPIs.
> - [ ] The **decision tree** in §4.4 is a board-friendly sketch that scores *easy marks* if reproduced in the answer script.
> - [ ] **KTU valuation mantra:** Show the formula, substitute values with units, compute stepwise, and conclude with a *boxed recommendation*.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Environment Impact Assessment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET745 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND AUDIT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Environment Impact Assessment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA)** is defined under the **United Nations Environment Programme (UNEP)** and adopted in the **KTU 2024 syllabus** as:

> *"A systematic, anticipatory, participatory and iterative process to evaluate the likely environmental consequences (positive and negative) of a proposed policy, plan, programme, or project, with the aim of integrating these considerations into the decision-making framework before the project is sanctioned or executed."*

In the **Indian regulatory context**, EIA is governed by the **EIA Notification, 2006** (and its 2020 draft amendments) issued under the **Environment (Protection) Act, 1986**, making it a **statutory requirement** before **Environmental Clearance (EC)** is granted for listed projects.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you want to introduce a new, very loud appliance into a quiet study room. Before turning it on, you would:
1. **Check** if the noise level is too high for the room (Screening).
2. **Identify** who is sitting inside and what might be disturbed (Scoping).
3. **Measure** the current sound and air levels in the room beforehand (Baseline Study).
4. **Predict** whether turning the appliance on will disturb others (Impact Prediction).
5. **Plan** to keep it short or use a silencer (Mitigation).
6. **Monitor** during and after use (Monitoring & Audit).

EIA follows **exactly the same logic**, but applied to large engineering projects (thermal power plants, highways, industries) and the natural environment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** For the Energy Management & Audit (PEPET745) course, EIA is studied specifically in the context of **energy projects** — thermal power plants, renewable energy installations, hydropower, and industrial energy systems — to ensure that energy generation does not irreversibly degrade the surrounding ecosystem.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics and Definitions

| Standard Term | Meaning (KTU-aligned) |
|---|---|
| **Project Proponent (PP)** | The entity (company/agency) proposing the project and responsible for commissioning the EIA. |
| **EIA Report (EIAR)** | The comprehensive document containing baseline data, impact prediction, and mitigation. |
| **EMP** | Environmental Management Plan – the actionable roadmap for mitigation. |
| **EAC / SEAC** | Expert Appraisal Committee (Central) / State Expert Appraisal Committee. |
| **TOR** | Terms of Reference issued by EAC/SEAC for preparing the EIA Report. |
| **Public Hearing** | Statutory consultation with project-affected persons under Section 7 of EIA 2006 Notification. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Carbon Footprint equivalence:** While EIA is environmental, it directly influences **Energy Audit** — a project with high pollution loads (CO₂, SO₂, NOₓ) often signals poor energy efficiency. EIA therefore serves as a **macroscopic complement** to the energy audit methodologies covered in Modules 2 and 3 of PEPET745.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 Objectives and Principles of EIA

The **raison d'être** of EIA rests on seven foundational objectives, which form a high-yield KTU answer framework:

1. **Identification** – Systematic identification of potential environmental, social, and economic impacts.
2. **Prediction** – Quantitative and qualitative forecasting of impact magnitude, duration, and reversibility.
3. **Evaluation** – Comparing alternatives (including the "no-project" alternative).
4. **Mitigation** – Proposing the BATNEEC principle (**Best Available Techniques Not Entailing Excessive Cost**).
5. **Decision Support** – Providing a transparent, evidence-based input to regulators and stakeholders.
6. **Public Participation** – Ensuring democratic consent and grievance redressal.
7. **Post-Project Monitoring** – Auditing actual impacts against predicted impacts (the **Adaptive EIA** principle).

### 2.2 The Ten Core Steps of the EIA Process

The KTU 2024 PEPET745 syllabus explicitly requires students to enumerate and explain the steps:

| Step | Stage | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Screening** | Decide whether EIA is mandatory for the project category. |
| 2 | **Scoping** | Identify key issues, impacts, and TOR for the study. |
| 3 | **Baseline Data Collection** | Establish existing environmental, social, and economic conditions. |
| 4 | **Impact Identification & Prediction** | Forecast changes due to project activities. |
| 5 | **Evaluation of Alternatives** | Compare site, technology, design, and the "no-project" scenario. |
| 6 | **Mitigation Measures** | Propose avoidance, reduction, remediation, or compensation. |
| 7 | **Environmental Management Plan (EMP)** | Document institutional, monitoring, and budget frameworks. |
| 8 | **Public Hearing / Consultation** | Capture stakeholder concerns. |
| 9 | **EIA Report Preparation & Review** | Compile EIAR; review by EAC/SEAC. |
| 10 | **Decision Making & Monitoring** | Grant/Reject EC; enforce post-clearance compliance. |

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet: Impact Identification Methods

These methods are **exam-favourites** under Part B (14 marks):

| Method | Core Idea | Strength / Limitation |
|---|---|---|
| **Checklists** | Simple list of environmental factors to consider. | Easy to use; misses interactions. |
| **Matrices (Leopold Matrix)** | Cross-tabulates project activities vs. environmental components; scores magnitude (1–10) × importance (1–10). | Visual; subjective. |
| **Networks** | Maps cause-effect-impact chains (e.g., power plant → cooling water → thermal discharge → aquatic ecology). | Shows linkages; complex. |
| **Overlay Mapping (McHarg)** | Superimposes transparent thematic maps (slope, land use, ecology). | Spatial; requires GIS. |
| **Battelle Environmental Evaluation System (BEES)** | Converts qualitative impacts into a single **Environmental Impact Unit (EIU)** score using parameter weights. | Quantitative; parameter-heavy. |
| **GIS-based Spatial Analysis** | Uses satellite imagery and layers to model dispersion of pollutants. | High precision; cost-intensive. |

### 2.4 Categories of Projects (India-Specific, EIA Notification 2006)

Under the **EIA Notification 2006**, projects are classified as:

- **Category A** — Cleared at the **Central Government** level by the **Expert Appraisal Committee (EAC)** under the **Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change (MoEFCC)**.
- **Category B** — Cleared at the **State Level** by the **State Environment Impact Assessment Authority (SEIAA)** based on SEAC recommendations.

Category B is further sub-divided:
- **Category B1** — Requires full EIA report.
- **Category B2** — Requires only a brief EMP and forms.

### 2.5 The Leopold Matrix Formulation (Mathematical Expression)

The Leopold Matrix assigns two scores to each cell:

$$
M_{ij} = \text{Magnitude score (} -10 \text{ to } +10 \text{)} \quad ; \quad I_{ij} = \text{Importance/Significance score (1 to 10)}
$$

The **Impact Significance Index (ISI)** for cell $(i,j)$ is the product:

$$
ISI_{ij} = M_{ij} \times I_{ij}
$$

For the **entire project**, the aggregate Impact Significance is:

$$
S_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{m} ISI_{ij}
$$

Where $n$ = number of project actions and $m$ = number of environmental components.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Absolute Value Convention:** When ranking only the magnitude of impact irrespective of sign (positive/negative), examiners expect $\vert M_{ij} \vert$. Always use `\vert` (LaTeX) to express absolute value; never use the pipe character inside markdown tables.

### 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Power Plants:** EIA assesses thermal discharge, SO₂/NOₓ/PM emissions, fly ash disposal, and impact on **Core Zones of Biosphere Reserves**.
- **Hydropower:** EIA evaluates submergence of forest land, displacement of tribal communities, and downstream **environmental flows** for rivers.
- **Renewable Energy (Wind/Solar):** EIA examines bird/bat collisions, land-use change, glare, and rare earth mineral extraction impacts.
- **Industrial Energy Efficiency Retrofits:** A modified EIA (often called a **"Mini-EIA"** or **Environmental Audit**) is required under ISO 14001 for any expansion that increases pollution load.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivation & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Mathematical Modelling of Impact Magnitude

The **fundamental impact prediction equation** used universally (and accepted by KTU examiners) is:

$$
I = f(P, A, L)
$$

Where:
- $I$ = Predicted environmental impact.
- $P$ = Project characteristics (capacity, emissions, effluents, footprint).
- $A$ = Affected environment (baseline ecological, social, climatic conditions).
- $L$ = Linkage mechanism (how pollutants transport, disperse, accumulate).

Expanding this for an **air pollution scenario**, the **Gaussian Plume Model** gives the ground-level concentration of a pollutant:

$$
C(x, y, 0) = \frac{Q}{2\pi \cdot u \cdot \sigma_y \cdot \sigma_z} \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{y^2}{2\sigma_y^2}\right) \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{H^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)
$$

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation of Impact Significance (for KTU Numerical Problems)

A typical KTU Part B numerical on EIA asks: *"Compute the overall impact significance from a given Leopold Matrix table."*

**Step 1:** Write the given matrix data with actions as rows and components as columns.

**Step 2:** For each cell, compute:

$$
ISI_{ij} = M_{ij} \times I_{ij}
$$

**Step 3:** Sum across all cells to get $S_{total}$:

$$
S_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{m} M_{ij} \cdot I_{ij}
$$

**Step 4:** Normalize using the maximum possible score:

$$
S_{norm} = \frac{S_{total}}{n \cdot m \cdot 100} \quad \text{(since } M_{max}=10 \text{ and } I_{max}=10 \text{)}
$$

**Step 5:** Interpret using the following **KTU-accepted classification table**:

| Normalized Score | Classification | Decision Implication |
|---|---|---|
| 0.00 – 0.25 | **Negligible** | EC granted with routine monitoring. |
| 0.25 – 0.50 | **Moderate** | EC granted with conditional mitigation. |
| 0.50 – 0.75 | **Significant** | Detailed EMP required; possible site modifications. |
| 0.75 – 1.00 | **Critical** | Likely rejection or major redesign. |

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example

> **Question:** A 4-action × 3-component Leopold Matrix has the following scores (M, I):
> Action 1: (8,9), (5,6), (-3,4)
> Action 2: (7,8), (-6,7), (2,3)
> Action 3: (4,5), (-2,3), (5,7)
> Action 4: (-9,10), (3,4), (6,8)
> Compute total impact significance and normalized score.

**Step 1: Compute ISI per cell**

$$
ISI_{1,1} = 8 \times 9 = 72
$$

$$
ISI_{1,2} = 5 \times 6 = 30
$$

$$
ISI_{1,3} = (-3) \times 4 = -12
$$

$$
ISI_{2,1} = 7 \times 8 = 56
$$

$$
ISI_{2,2} = (-6) \times 7 = -42
$$

$$
ISI_{2,3} = 2 \times 3 = 6
$$

$$
ISI_{3,1} = 4 \times 5 = 20
$$

$$
ISI_{3,2} = (-2) \times 3 = -6
$$

$$
ISI_{3,3} = 5 \times 7 = 35
$$

$$
ISI_{4,1} = (-9) \times 10 = -90
$$

$$
ISI_{4,2} = 3 \times 4 = 12
$$

$$
ISI_{4,3} = 6 \times 8 = 48
$$

**Step 2: Summation**

$$
S_{total} = 72 + 30 + (-12) + 56 + (-42) + 6 + 20 + (-6) + 35 + (-90) + 12 + 48
$$

$$
S_{total} = 129
$$

**Step 3: Normalized score**

$$
S_{norm} = \frac{129}{4 \times 3 \times 100} = \frac{129}{1200} = 0.1075
$$

**Step 4: Interpretation**

Since $0.00 \leq 0.1075 < 0.25$, the project impact is classified as **Negligible**, and EC can be granted with routine monitoring.

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** KTU examiners typically award **full marks** if students clearly show the matrix dimensions, the ISI formula, and the classification table interpretation. Skipping the classification step results in a **2-mark deduction** even when the calculation is correct.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 EIA Process Flow Diagram (Mermaid)

The following Mermaid flowchart maps the **complete EIA lifecycle** as prescribed by MoEFCC and adopted by KTU 2024 PEPET745:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Project Proposal by Proponent"] --> B["Screening as Cat A or Cat B"]
    B --> C1{"Category A"}
    B --> C2{"Category B"}
    C1 --> D["EAC Appraisal at Central Level"]
    C2 --> E["SEAC Appraisal at State Level"]
    D --> F["Issue of Terms of Reference TOR"]
    E --> F
    F --> G["Baseline Data Collection"]
    G --> H["Impact Prediction and Leopold Matrix"]
    H --> I["Evaluation of Alternatives"]
    I --> J["Formulation of EMP"]
    J --> K["Public Hearing and Consultation"]
    K --> L["EIA Report Preparation"]
    L --> M["Final Review by EAC or SEAC"]
    M --> N{"Environmental Clearance Decision"}
    N --> O1["Grant of EC with Conditions"]
    N --> O2["Rejection of Project"]
    O1 --> P["Implementation and Construction"]
    P --> Q["Post Clearance Monitoring"]
    Q --> R["Six Monthly Compliance Reports"]
    R --> S["Periodic Environmental Audit"]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of EIA Components

For understanding the **structural composition** of an EIA Report, the following block diagram shows the **architectural inter-relationships** between its modules:

    end

    subgraph AnalysisStage["ANALYSIS STAGE"]

    subgraph OutputStage["OUTPUT STAGE"]
        C1["EIA Report"]
        C2["Environmental Management Plan"]

    end

    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B2

    B1 --> C3

```

### 4.3 Schematic of the Leopold Matrix Structure

The following diagram illustrates how a Leopold Matrix is conceptually constructed for a thermal power plant EIA:

```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Note for Students:** When reproducing these diagrams in your answer sheets (even as hand-drawn block diagrams), always show **directionality with arrows**, label every block, and ensure the logical sequence is unambiguous. KTU examiners allocate marks for the **flow logic** of the diagram, not artistic quality.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. Define Environmental Impact Assessment. Mention any two objectives of EIA.**
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA) is a systematic process used to evaluate the likely environmental, social, and economic consequences of a proposed project before a decision is made to proceed.

**Two objectives:**
1. To **identify and predict** likely environmental impacts of proposed projects.
2. To **propose mitigation measures** and an Environmental Management Plan (EMP) to avoid or reduce adverse impacts.

> [!TIP]
> Examiners accept any two valid objectives (transparency, public participation, alternatives evaluation, post-project monitoring). Writing more than two objectives is **not penalized** but writing fewer than two **costs marks**.

#### **Q2. What is meant by "Screening" and "Scoping" in EIA?**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **Screening:** The preliminary stage where the regulatory authority determines whether a proposed project requires a full EIA or can be exempted. It is based on project size, location, type, and threshold limits specified in the EIA Notification, 2006. Projects are classified as **Category A** or **Category B** during screening.
- **Scoping:** The second stage where the key environmental issues, study area boundaries, methodologies, and **Terms of Reference (TOR)** are defined after consultation with stakeholders and expert committees. It narrows the focus of the EIA to the most significant impacts.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Mandate (KTU 2024 Scheme):** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the **step-by-step methodology of the EIA process** as adopted in India, with reference to the EIA Notification 2006. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the **Leopold Matrix method** of impact identification. A project has the following Leopold Matrix data. Compute the **total impact significance** and classify the project.

| Action | Air ($M \times I$) | Water ($M \times I$) | Soil ($M \times I$) | Ecology ($M \times I$) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Construction | 8 × 9 | 7 × 8 | 6 × 7 | -4 × 5 |
| Operation | 9 × 10 | -5 × 6 | -3 × 4 | -8 × 9 |
| Waste Disposal | -7 × 8 | -9 × 10 | -8 × 9 | -6 × 7 |

**(7 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4 Internal Choice]**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) EIA Methodology (7 Marks):**

The EIA process in India, governed by the **EIA Notification 2006** under the **Environment (Protection) Act, 1986**, consists of **ten sequential steps**:

1. **Screening:** Projects are classified as Category A (central clearance by EAC) or Category B (state clearance by SEIAA/SEAC).
2. **Scoping:** Identification of key issues and issuance of **Terms of Reference (TOR)** by EAC/SEAC.
3. **Baseline Data Collection:** Existing environmental conditions — air, water, soil, noise, ecology, socio-economic — are measured for at least one season.
4. **Impact Identification and Prediction:** Using Leopold Matrix, mathematical models (Gaussian plume, hydrological models), and GIS overlay analysis.
5. **Evaluation of Alternatives:** Including **site alternatives**, **technology alternatives**, and the **"no-project" alternative**.
6. **Mitigation Measures:** Proposing avoidance, reduction, remediation, or compensation based on the **BATNEEC** principle.
7. **Environmental Management Plan (EMP):** Documenting institutional responsibilities, mitigation budget, and monitoring schedule.
8. **Public Hearing:** Mandatory for Category A and B1 projects; conducted by the State Pollution Control Board (SPCB).
9. **EIA Report Preparation and Review:** Final EIAR submitted to EAC/SEAC for appraisal.
10. **Decision Making and Monitoring:** Grant or refusal of **Environmental Clearance**; **six-monthly compliance reports** and **environmental audit** every 3 years.

**(b) Leopold Matrix Calculation (7 Marks):**

**Step 1: Compute ISI per cell**

For Construction row:
- Air: $8 \times 9 = 72$
- Water: $7 \times 8 = 56$
- Soil: $6 \times 7 = 42$
- Ecology: $(-4) \times 5 = -20$

For Operation row:
- Air: $9 \times 10 = 90$
- Water: $(-5) \times 6 = -30$
- Soil: $(-3) \times 4 = -12$
- Ecology: $(-8) \times 9 = -72$

For Waste Disposal row:
- Air: $(-7) \times 8 = -56$
- Water: $(-9) \times 10 = -90$
- Soil: $(-8) \times 9 = -72$
- Ecology: $(-6) \times 7 = -42$

**Step 2: Total Sum**

$$
S_{total} = (72 + 56 + 42 - 20) + (90 - 30 - 12 - 72) + (-56 - 90 - 72 - 42)
$$

$$
S_{total} = 150 + (-24) + (-260)
$$

$$
S_{total} = 150 - 24 - 260 = -134
$$

**Step 3: Normalized Score**

The matrix has 3 actions and 4 components, so:

$$
S_{norm} = \frac{\vert -134 \vert}{3 \times 4 \times 100} = \frac{134}{1200} = 0.1117
$$

**Step 4: Classification**

Since $0.00 \leq 0.1117 < 0.25$, the project impact is classified as **Negligible**, and **Environmental Clearance can be granted** with routine monitoring.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **roles and responsibilities** of the **Project Proponent**, **EAC/SEAC**, and **State Pollution Control Board (SPCB)** in the EIA process. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Environmental Management Plan (EMP)** structure with its **key components**. How does post-clearance monitoring differ from the initial EIA study? **(7 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4 Internal Choice]**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Roles and Responsibilities (7 Marks):**

1. **Project Proponent (PP):**
   - Submits the **Form-1** and **Form-1A** (pre-feasibility) along with the conceptual plan.
   - Engages an **accredited EIA consultant** (QCI/NABET accredited).
   - Funds and supervises baseline data collection and impact prediction.
   - Publishes a **summary EIA report** in at least two regional newspapers.
   - Implements the EMP and submits **six-monthly compliance reports**.

2. **EAC / SEAC (Expert / State Expert Appraisal Committee):**
   - Multi-disciplinary body with experts in ecology, air/water quality, hydrology, social sciences, and project engineering.
   - Issues **Terms of Reference (TOR)** for the EIA study.
   - Appraises the final EIA Report and recommends **grant, modification, or rejection** of EC to the regulatory authority.
   - Reviews compliance reports during the **EC validity period**.

3. **State Pollution Control Board (SPCB):**
   - Conducts the **public hearing** and forwards the proceedings to the SEIAA.
   - Issues **Consent to Establish (CTE)** and **Consent to Operate (CTO)** under the Air and Water Acts.
   - Monitors ambient air, water, and noise quality throughout the project life.
   - Has the power to **close down** non-compliant units under Section 5 of the Environment Protection Act.

**(b) Environmental Management Plan & Post-Clearance Monitoring (7 Marks):**

The **EMP** is the actionable mitigation document with four key components:

| Component | Purpose |
|---|---|
| **Mitigation Matrix** | Links each identified impact to a specific control measure with responsibility, cost, and timeline. |
| **Monitoring Plan** | Specifies parameters, frequency, location, and method of measurement. |
| **Institutional Framework** | Defines the **Environmental Cell** within the organization with designated officers. |
| **Budget Allocation** | Capital and recurring expenditure for EMP implementation (typically 2–5% of total project cost). |

**Difference between EIA Study and Post-Clearance Monitoring:**

| Aspect | Initial EIA Study | Post-Clearance Monitoring |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Predictive forecasting. | Verification of actual vs. predicted impacts. |
| **Timing** | Pre-project (before EC). | During construction, operation, and decommissioning. |
| **Frequency** | One-time detailed study. | Periodic (six-monthly compliance, annual audits). |
| **Data** | Predicted models and projections. | Measured ambient and source data. |
| **Outcome** | EC decision. | Adaptive management, penalties, or revocation. |

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Areas in EIA Answers:**
> 1. **Confusing Screening with Scoping:** Many students interchange these two. Screening decides *whether* EIA is needed; Scoping decides *what* EIA must study. **1–2 marks lost** if confused.
> 2. **Forgetting the "No-Project Alternative":** Evaluation of alternatives is **mandatory** and the "no-project" option is the most important alternative. Omitting it loses 1 mark in Part B.
> 3. **Public Hearing is NOT optional:** Skipping or wrongly stating that public hearing is optional for Category A projects will lead to a **3-mark deduction** in Part B.
> 4. **Numerical Sign Convention:** In Leopold Matrix, **magnitude can be negative** (for adverse impacts). Forgetting the negative sign changes the entire normalized score and interpretation. **2 marks lost**.
> 5. **CITIZEN vs EIA Report:** Some students mix up EIA with **Citizen's Report** (an NGO document) — never confuse the two. The EIA Report is technical and government-acknowledged.
> 6. **Naming the Wrong Year of Notification:** India has had EIA Notifications in **1994** and **2006**, with **2020 draft amendments**. Mentioning the right year strengthens the answer.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **EIA = Predictive + Participatory + Iterative.** Not just a one-time report.
- **Statutory Basis in India:** Environment (Protection) Act, **1986**; EIA Notification, **2006**.
- **Two-Tier System:** Central (EAC) for Category A; State (SEAC/SEIAA) for Category B.
- **Ten EIA Steps:** Screening → Scoping → Baseline → Prediction → Alternatives → Mitigation → EMP → Public Hearing → Review → Decision & Monitoring.
- **Leopold Matrix Formula:**

$$
ISI_{ij} = M_{ij} \times I_{ij} \quad ; \quad S_{norm} = \frac{\sum ISI_{ij}}{n \cdot m \cdot 100}
$$

- **Impact Identification Tools:** Checklist, Matrix (Leopold), Network, Overlay (McHarg), BEES, GIS.
- **Public Hearing is statutory** under Section 7 of the EIA Notification 2006.
- **EMP Components:** Mitigation, Monitoring, Institutional Setup, Budget.
- **Post-clearance:** Six-monthly compliance reports, environmental audit every 3 years.
- **Key Organizations:** MoEFCC (Central), SPCB (State), EAC/SEAC (Appraisal), SEIAA (Decision at State Level).
- **Energy Management Linkage:** EIA for energy projects must quantify **CO₂ equivalent emissions, fly ash, and water consumption per MWh** generated — this is the direct PEPET745 cross-module link.
- **BATNEEC** principle governs mitigation choice.

> **Final Exam Tip:** A well-structured answer mentioning at least one **Indian example** (e.g., *Sardar Sarovar Dam EIA, Delhi Metro EIA, or Kudankulam Nuclear Power Plant EIA*) earns **bonus credibility marks** from KTU examiners, even when not explicitly asked.
